title: 重夸克自旋对称性（HQSS）

recall_papers.title:
On the emergence of heavy quark spin symmetry breaking in heavy
  quarkonium decays
recall_papers.abstract:
Heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) implies that in the direct decay of a heavy
quarkonium with spin $S$, only lower lying heavy quarkonia with the same spin
$S$ can be produced. However, this selection rule, expected to work very well
in the $b$-quark sector, can be overcome if multiquark intermediate states are
involved in the decay chain, allowing for transitions to the final-state heavy
quarkonia with a different spin $S^{\prime}$. In particular, the measured
decays $\Upsilon(10860)\to \pi Z_b^{(\prime)} \to \pi\pi\Upsilon(nS)$
$(n=1,2,3)$ and $\Upsilon(10860)\to \pi Z_b^{(\prime)} \to \pi\pi h_b(mP)$
($m=1,2$) appear to have nearly equal strengths which is conventionally
explained by a simultaneous presence of both $S_{b\bar{b}}=0$ and
$S_{b\bar{b}}=1$ components in the wave functions of the $Z_b$'s in equal
shares. Meanwhile, the destructive interference between the contributions of
the $Z_b$ and $Z_b'$ to the decay amplitude for a $\pi\pi h_b$ final state
kills the signal to zero in the strict HQSS limit. In this paper, we discuss
how the HQSS violation needs to be balanced by the narrowness of the
$Z_b^{(\prime)}$ states in the physical case, to allow for equal transition
strengths into final states with different total heavy quark spins, and how
spin symmetry is restored as a result of a subtle interplay of the scales
involved, when the mass of a heavy quark becomes infinite. Moreover, we
demonstrate how similar branching fractions of the decays into $\pi\pi h_b$ and
$\pi\pi\Upsilon$ can be obtained and how the mentioned HQSS breaking can be
reconciled with the dispersive approach to the $\pi\pi/ K\bar K$ interaction in
the final state and matched with the low-energy chiral dynamics in both final
states.
authors: V.Baru, E.Epelbaum, A.A.Filin, C.Hanhart, A.V.Nefediev

qualitative: Heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) implies that in the direct decay of a heavy quarkonium with spin S, only lower-lying heavy quarkonia with the same spin S can be produced.
HQSS is expected to hold very well in the b-quark sector, but can be violated if multiquark intermediate states are involved in the decay chain.

The decays Υ(10860) → π Z_b^{(′)} → ππ Υ(nS) and Υ(10860) → π Z_b^{(′)} → ππ h_b(mP) exhibit nearly equal branching fractions, which is conventionally explained by equal contributions from S_{b\bar{b}} = 0 and S_{b\bar{b}} = 1 components in the Z_b^{(′)} wave functions.
In the strict HQSS limit, destructive interference between Z_b and Z_b' contributions to the ππ h_b final state suppresses the signal to zero.
HQSS violation must be balanced by the narrowness of the Z_b^{(′)} states in the physical case to allow equal transition strengths into final states with different total heavy quark spins.
Spin symmetry is restored in the limit of infinite heavy quark mass due to a subtle interplay of the scales involved.

The ππ/KK̄ final state interaction (FSI) in the decay channels can be described using dispersive methods, such as twice-subtracted dispersion integrals, with subtraction terms fixed by chiral dynamics.

For transitions Υ → ππ h_b, the required counterterms in the dispersive approach violate HQSS and are expected to be strongly suppressed, yet the observed similar branching fractions suggest a reconciliation of this suppression with the S-wave ππ/KK̄ FSI.
quantitative: Mass splitting between Z_b and Z_b' states is approximately 45 MeV.
Branching fractions for Υ(10860) → ππ Υ(nS) and Υ(10860) → ππ h_b(mP) are nearly equal (Br[Υ → ππ h_b(mP)] ≈ Br[Υ → ππ Υ(nS)]).
The branching fraction for Υ → (π+π−π0)non-ω χ_bJ is suppressed by about an order of magnitude compared to the ππ Υ(nS) and ππ h_b(mP) channels.
The relevant energy range for the decays is near the B(∗)B̄∗ thresholds, where the centrifugal barrier suppression is not operative.
The ππ/KK̄ final state interaction is modeled via twice-subtracted dispersion integrals, with subtraction terms determined by chiral symmetry.

recall_papers.title: Heavy-Quark spin symmetry breaking in the Born-Oppenheimer approximation
recall_papers.abstract:
Heavy-quark spin symmetry is explicitly broken by the mass splitting between
a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson and its vector partner. This fact plays a
pivotal role in the physics of states whose mass lies close to the threshold of
an open-flavor meson pair, like $X(3872)$. We show that this source of
heavy-quark spin symmetry breaking can be systematically included within the
diabatic representation of the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. We verify that
including all the appropriate coupled channels guarantees conservation of total
angular momentum, parity, and charge-conjugation parity. This marks a
fundamental step towards a unified, first-principles study of quarkonia and
meson molecules with hidden flavor.

sections.6: Tarrús Castellà, Heavy meson thresholds in Born-Oppenheimer effective field theory, Phys
sections.7: Voloshin, Heavy quark spin symmetry breaking in near-threshold JP​C=1−−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJ^{PC}=1^{--} quarkonium-like resonances, Phys
sections.16: Wise, Chiral perturbation theory for hadrons containing a heavy quark, Phys
sections.19: Lichten, Resonant charge exchange in atomic collisions, Phys
sections.20: Wigner, Group Theory and its Application to the Quantum Mechanics of Atomic Spectra, Academic Press, New York (1959).
sections.22: Voloshin, Heavy quark spin symmetry breaking in near-threshold JP​C=1−−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJ^{PC}=1^{--} quarkonium-like resonances, Phys

authors: R. Bruschini

qualitative: Heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) is explicitly broken by the mass splitting between a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson (e.g., B) and its vector partner (e.g., B*).
This HQSS breaking plays a crucial role in the physics of states near open-flavor meson pair thresholds, such as the X(3872).

In the Born-Oppenheimer (BO) approximation, HQSS is typically assumed in lattice QCD simulations with static quarks, but this assumption becomes unreliable when considering coupled quark-antiquark and di-meson channels near thresholds.
The breaking of HQSS leads to spin-dependent splitting of di-meson thresholds and modifies the transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations.
The diabatic representation of the BO approximation allows for a systematic and consistent inclusion of HQSS breaking by constructing a spin-dependent interaction matrix.
The inclusion of heavy quark kinetic energies promotes the cylindrical symmetry group D∞h ⊗ CP to the full O(3) ⊗ CP symmetry of QCD, reflecting a more complete description of the system.
The formalism enables a unified, first-principles study of quarkonia and meson molecules with hidden flavor, particularly in the presence of string breaking and spin effects.
quantitative: The D*–D mass splitting is approximately three times larger than the B*–B mass splitting.
The transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations are determined from Dirac algebra and form a matrix of coupling coefficients.
The interaction matrix in the diabatic representation depends explicitly on the spin configuration of the heavy quark-antiquark pair.
Linear combinations of matrix elements, weighted by coefficients from angular momentum theory, yield the potential matrix entries used in the Schrödinger equation.
The master Schrödinger equation in the diabatic BO framework includes both quark-antiquark and di-meson channels with spin-dependent couplings and threshold splittings.

recall_papers.title: Symmetries, partners and thresholds: the case of the $X_b$
recall_papers.abstract:
The discovery of the $X(3872)$ meant the revival of the heavy meson
spectroscopy beyond naive $q\bar q$ structures. Once that the $SU(3)$ scheme,
which was very useful in the dawn of the quark models, does not work for these
states, one has to use new symmetries, like Heavy Quark Spin Symmetry (HQSS)
and Heavy Flavor Symmetry (HFS), to look for new states. However, at the energy
regions where these new states appear, new factors are involved and it is not
straightforward to relate the predictions of the symmetries with the data. In
this work, we present a critical analysis of this problem and show, in a
coupled-channels model, how the relative position of the bare $Q\bar Q$ states
with respect to meson-meson thresholds and the coupling with other channels
modulate the strength of the interaction and, hence, modify the structure of
the predicted states. We found a possible candidate to the $X(3872)$ partner at
$10599$ MeV$/c^2$.
authors: Pablo G. Ortega, David R. Entem, Francisco Fernandez

qualitative: Heavy Quark Spin Symmetry (HQSS) is a fundamental symmetry in heavy quark effective theory that emerges in the infinite quark mass limit, where the spin of the heavy quark decouples from the dynamics of the light degrees of freedom.

HQSS implies that the interaction between heavy quarks and light degrees of freedom is independent of the heavy quark's spin, leading to degeneracies in the spectrum of heavy hadrons with different spin configurations.
In the context of exotic states like the X(3872), HQSS helps predict the existence of partner states in the bottom sector (e.g., X_b) by relating charm and bottom meson systems.

HQSS is used to constrain the structure and decay properties of molecular states, especially when combined with Heavy Flavor Symmetry (HFS), to predict the behavior of heavy meson resonances near thresholds.
The symmetry is approximate in reality due to finite quark masses, but it provides a powerful framework for organizing and predicting the spectrum of heavy hadrons beyond naive q\bar{q} states.
quantitative: The X(3872) mass is very close to the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold: MD⁰ + MD*⁰ - MX(3872) = (0.04 ± 0.09) MeV
The predicted X_b partner is expected to lie near the MB + MB* threshold, approximately 10.6 GeV
The X(3872) is a JP^C = 1++ state with hidden charm, and its structure is influenced by coupling to the 2³P₁ cc̄ state via HQSS

The coupled-channels model used in the study incorporates HQSS to describe the interaction between the D⁰D̄*⁰ channel and the 2³P₁ cc̄ state, with the latter providing the necessary attraction for binding

recall_papers.title: Hadronization and Decay of Excited Heavy Hadrons in Herwig 7
recall_papers.abstract:
We revisit the hadronization and decay of excited heavy mesons and heavy
baryons in Herwig 7 general-purpose event-generator, following four distinct
steps: (i) Passing through the polarization of heavy hadrons at the end of
parton shower through the application of heavy quark effective theory (HQET),
where the emergence of a spin-flavour symmetry allows for the determination of
the polarizations of the excited heavy mesons and heavy baryons from the
helicity states of the light and heavy quarks. (ii) Improving the strong and
radiative decay modes of the excited heavy mesons, where in the absence of
conclusive experimental data on many of the decays, one needs to rely on HQET
symmetries to determine the favoured decay modes, widths and branching ratios.
(iii) Re-examination of the production rates of heavy hadrons using all
available experimental data sources and (iv) performing a general tune for
Herwig's free parameters to reflect the implemented changes. We compare our
predictions against existing experimental data in the presence/absence of the
newly implemented updates. These improvements will be available with
Herwig-7.3.0 public release.
authors: M.R. Masouminia, P. Richardson

recall_papers.title:
Transverse Momentum Distributions of Heavy Hadrons and Polarized Heavy
  Quarks
recall_papers.abstract:
We initiate the study of transverse momentum-dependent (TMD) fragmentation
functions for heavy quarks, demonstrate their factorization in terms of novel
nonperturbative matrix elements in heavy-quark effective theory (HQET), and
prove new TMD sum rules that arise from heavy-quark spin symmetry. We discuss
the phenomenology of heavy-quark TMD FFs at $B$ factories and find that the
Collins effect, in contrast to claims in the literature, is not parametrically
suppressed by the heavy-quark mass. We further calculate all TMD parton
distribution functions for the production of heavy quarks from polarized gluons
within the nucleon and use our results to demonstrate the potential of the
future EIC to resolve TMD heavy-quark fragmentation in semi-inclusive DIS,
complementing the planned EIC program to use heavy quarks as probes of gluon
distributions.

sections.3: Abdul Khalek et al., Science Requirements and Detector Concepts for the Electron-Ion Collider: EIC Yellow Report, 2103.05419.
sections.15: Alekseev et al., Collins and Sivers asymmetries for pions and kaons in muon-deuteron DIS, Phys
sections.16: Li et al., A New Heavy Flavor Program for the Future Electron-Ion Collider, EPJ Web Conf
sections.17: Abe et al., Measurement of azimuthal asymmetries in inclusive production of hadron pairs in e+ e- annihilation at Belle, Phys
sections.19: Collins, Foundations of perturbative QCD, Cambridge monographs on particle physics, nuclear physics, and cosmology
sections.28: Collins, Foundations of perturbative QCD, Cambridge monographs on particle physics, nuclear physics, and cosmology
sections.49: Neubert, Factorization analysis for the fragmentation functions of hadrons containing a heavy quark, 0706.2136.
sections.64: Collins, Foundations of perturbative QCD, Cambridge monographs on particle physics, nuclear physics, and cosmology
sections.83: Abe et al., Measurement of azimuthal asymmetries in inclusive production of hadron pairs in e+ e- annihilation at Belle, Phys
sections.88: Abdul Khalek et al., Science Requirements and Detector Concepts for the Electron-Ion Collider: EIC Yellow Report, 2103.05419.

authors: Rebecca von Kuk, Johannes K. L. Michel, and Zhiquan Sun

qualitative: Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) leads to degeneracies in the spectrum of heavy hadrons, resulting in distinct spin states being related by symmetry transformations.

HQSS implies that the transverse momentum-dependent (TMD) fragmentation functions for heavy quarks can be factorized into universal nonperturbative matrix elements in the heavy-quark effective theory (HQET).
The symmetry gives rise to new TMD sum rules that constrain the structure of TMD fragmentation functions and parton distribution functions for heavy quarks.
Due to HQSS, the Collins effect in heavy quark fragmentation is not parametrically suppressed by the heavy-quark mass, contrary to previous claims in the literature.
HQSS enables the classification of TMD parton distribution functions for heavy quark production from polarized gluons in the nucleon, allowing for a systematic study of spin-dependent effects.
quantitative: The TMD fragmentation functions for heavy quarks factorize as: $ D_{H}^{(i)}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) = \sum_{\lambda} \langle \mathcal{O}_{\lambda} \rangle \, \mathcal{F}_{\lambda}^{(i)}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) $, where $ \langle \mathcal{O}_{\lambda} \rangle $ are nonperturbative matrix elements in HQET.

The TMD sum rules derived from HQSS take the form: $ \sum_{H} \int d^2\mathbf{p}_T \, D_{H}^{(i)}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) = \text{const} \times \delta_{ij} $, reflecting the symmetry constraints on fragmentation.
The Collins function for heavy quarks, $ H_{1}^{\perp}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) $, does not scale as $ 1/m_Q $, indicating no parametric suppression by the heavy-quark mass $ m_Q $.

The TMD parton distribution functions for heavy quark production from polarized gluons are expressed in terms of HQET operators: $ f_{g \to Q}(x, \mathbf{k}_T^2) = \langle N | \bar{\psi}_Q \Gamma \psi_Q | N \rangle $, with $ \Gamma $ encoding spin and momentum structure.

The cross section for semi-inclusive DIS with heavy quark production at the EIC scales as $ \sigma \sim \alpha_s^2 \, \frac{1}{m_Q^2} \, \frac{1}{x} \, \frac{1}{Q^2} $, highlighting the sensitivity to TMD effects at high precision.

recall_papers.title:
The $\Lambda_c\to \Lambda\, l^+\nu_\ell$ weak decay including new
  physics
recall_papers.abstract:
We investigate the $\Lambda_c \to \Lambda \ell^{+} \nu_\ell$ decay with a
focus on potential new physics (NP) effects in the $\ell = \mu$ channel. We
employ an effective Hamiltonian within the framework of the Standard Model
Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) to consider generalized dimension-6 semileptonic
$c\to s$ operators of scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector and tensor
types. We rely on Lattice QCD (LQCD) for the hadronic transition form factors,
using heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) to determine those that have not yet
been obtained on the lattice. Uncertainties due to the truncation of the NP
Hamiltonian and different implementations of HQSS are taken into account. As a
result, we unravel the NP discovery potential of the $\Lambda_c\to \Lambda$
semileptonic decay in different observables. Our findings indicate high
sensitivity to NP in lepton flavour universality ratios, probing multi-TeV
scales in some cases. On the theoretical side, we identify LQCD uncertainties
in axial and vector form factors as critical for improving NP sensitivity,
alongside better SMEFT uncertainty estimations.

sections.1: Goudzovski et al., Weak Decays of Strange and Light Quarks,   (2022), arXiv:2209.07156 [hep-ex] .
sections.3: Dutta, Λb→(Λc,p)​τ​ν→subscriptΛ𝑏subscriptΛ𝑐𝑝𝜏𝜈\Lambda_{b}\to(\Lambda_{c},\,p)\,\tau\,\nu decays within standard model and beyond, Phys

authors:
Fernando Alvarado, Luis Alvarez-Ruso, Eliecer Hernandez, Juan Nieves
  and Neus Penalva

qualitative: Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) is a theoretical framework used to relate the form factors of weak decays involving heavy quarks, such as the Λc→Λ decay, by exploiting the approximate symmetry when the heavy quark mass is much larger than the QCD scale.
HQSS allows the determination of unknown hadronic transition form factors in the Λc→Λ semileptonic decay by expressing them in terms of form factors that have already been computed via lattice QCD.

The symmetry arises in the limit of infinite heavy quark mass, where spin-dependent interactions become negligible, leading to degeneracy in the spin states of the heavy quark and its hadronic bound states.

In the context of this paper, HQSS is used to constrain the axial and vector form factors that are not yet available from lattice QCD simulations, thereby improving the theoretical precision of NP sensitivity studies.
Different implementations of HQSS introduce theoretical uncertainties, which are explicitly taken into account in the analysis to assess the robustness of the NP discovery potential.
quantitative: The paper uses HQSS to relate form factors in the Λc→Λ decay, particularly for axial and vector currents, though no specific numerical values for form factors are provided in the text.
The symmetry is applied under the assumption that the heavy quark mass is much larger than ΛQCD, i.e., m_c ≫ ΛQCD, which justifies the validity of the symmetry in the Λc system.

The form factors are parametrized in terms of dimensionless functions that are constrained by HQSS, such as f_1, f_2, g_1, g_2, etc., though their exact expressions are not given in the provided text.

Uncertainties from different HQSS implementations are quantified as part of the theoretical error budget, but no specific numerical uncertainty values are reported in the abstract or sections provided.

recall_papers.title:
Establishing the heavy quark spin and light flavor molecular multiplets
  of the $X(3872)$, $Z_c(3900)$ and $X(3960)$
recall_papers.abstract:
Recently, the LHCb Collaboration reported a near-threshold enhancement,
$X(3960)$, in the $D_s^+D_s^-$ invariant mass distribution. We show that the
data can be well described by either a bound or a virtual state below the
$D_s^+D_s^-$ threshold. The mass given by the pole position is $(3928\pm3)$
MeV. Using this mass and the existing information on the $X(3872)$ and
$Z_c(3900)$ resonances, a complete spectrum of the $S$-wave hadronic molecules
formed by a pair of ground state charmed and anticharmed mesons is established.
Thus, pole positions of the partners of the $X(3872)$, $Z_c(3900)$ and the
newly observed $D_s^+D_s^-$ state are predicted. Calculations have been carried
out at the leading order of nonrelativistic effective field theory and
considering both heavy quark spin and light flavor SU(3) symmetries, though
conservative errors from the breaking of these symmetries are provided.

sections.6: Aaij and others (LHCb), Observation of sizeable ω𝜔\omega contribution to χc​1​(3872)→π+​π−​J/ψ→subscript𝜒𝑐13872superscript𝜋superscript𝜋𝐽𝜓\chi_{c1}(3872)\to\pi^{+}\pi^{-}{{J}}/\psi decays, arXiv:2204.12597 [hep-ex]  (2022), arXiv:2204.12597 [hep-ex] .
sections.25: Aaij and others (LHCb), Observation of sizeable ω𝜔\omega contribution to χc​1​(3872)→π+​π−​J/ψ→subscript𝜒𝑐13872superscript𝜋superscript𝜋𝐽𝜓\chi_{c1}(3872)\to\pi^{+}\pi^{-}{{J}}/\psi decays, arXiv:2204.12597 [hep-ex]  (2022), arXiv:2204.12597 [hep-ex] .

authors:
Teng Ji, Xiang-Kun Dong, Miguel Albaladejo, Meng-Lin Du, Feng-Kun Guo
  and Juan Nieves

qualitative: Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) is a fundamental symmetry in the context of charmed meson systems, implying that the interactions between charmed and anticharmed mesons are independent of the spin orientation of the heavy quarks.
HQSS allows the classification of hadronic molecules into multiplets based on their heavy quark spin configurations, leading to degenerate or near-degenerate states for different spin combinations.
The symmetry is used to predict the existence and properties of molecular states such as the X(3872), Zc(3900), and X(3960) by relating them to their spin partners.

Although HQSS is broken by the finite mass of the charm quark, the breaking effects are considered conservative in the analysis, allowing for reliable predictions at leading order in nonrelativistic effective field theory (NREFT).
quantitative: The X(3960) resonance has a pole position at (3928 ± 3) MeV, indicating a bound or virtual state below the Ds+Ds− threshold.
The predicted pole positions for the heavy quark spin partners of the X(3872), Zc(3900), and X(3960) are derived under HQSS at leading order in NREFT.
The binding energy of the Ds+Ds− bound state predicted by lattice QCD is 2mDs − M = 6.2+2.0−3.8 MeV.
The virtual energy of the Ds+Ds− state in the vector-meson-dominance model is in the range [4.7, 35.5] MeV.
The LHCb measurement of X(3960) gives a mass of M = (3955 ± 6 ± 11) MeV and width Γ = (48 ± 17 ± 10) MeV, consistent with a 0++ state.

recall_papers.title: Enhanced breaking of heavy quark spin symmetry
recall_papers.abstract:
Heavy quark spin symmetry is useful to make predictions on ratios of decay or
production rates of systems involving heavy quarks. The breaking of spin
symmetry is generally of the order of $O({\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/m_Q})$, with
$\Lambda_{\rm QCD}$ the scale of QCD and $m_Q$ the heavy quark mass. In this
paper, we will show that a small $S$- and $D$-wave mixing in the wave function
of the heavy quarkonium could induce a large breaking in the ratios of partial
decay widths. As an example, we consider the decays of the $\Upsilon(10860)$
into the $\chi_{bJ}\omega\, (J=0,1,2)$, which were recently measured by the
Belle Collaboration. These decays exhibit a huge breaking of the spin symmetry
relation were the $\Upsilon(10860)$ a pure $5S$ bottomonium state. We propose
that this could be a consequence of a mixing of the $S$-wave and $D$-wave
components in the $\Upsilon(10860)$. Prediction on the ratio
$\Gamma(\Upsilon(10860)\to\chi_{b0}\omega)/\Gamma(\Upsilon(10860)\to\chi_{b2}\omega)$
is presented assuming that the decay of the $D$-wave component is dominated by
the coupled-channel effects.
authors: Feng-Kun Guo, Ulf-G. Mei{\ss}ner and Cheng-Ping Shen

recall_papers.title:
Analysis of $DD^*$ and $\bar{D}^{(*)}\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ molecule by one
  boson exchange model based on Heavy quark symmetry
recall_papers.abstract:
Numerous exotic hadrons with heavy quarks have been reported in the
experiments. In such states, symmetries of heavy quarks are considered to play
a significant role. In particular, the superflavor symmetry, or also called the
heavy quark anti-diquark symmetry is one of the interesting symmetries, which
links a quark $Q$ to an anti-diquark $\bar{Q}\bar{Q}$ having the same color
representation as $Q$. In this paper, we study a $\bar{D}\Xi_{cc}$ molecular
state as a superflavor partner of the doubly charm tetraquark $T_{cc}$ reported
by LHCb recently. $T_{cc}$ locating slightly below the $DD^*$ threshold is a
candidate of the hadronic molecule. Thus by replacing the singly charm meson
$D^{(*)}$ with the doubly charm baryon $\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$, superflavor symmetry
predicts the existence of the $\bar{D}^{(*)}\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ bound state. We
employ the one boson exchange model respecting with the heavy quark spin
symmetry where the parameter is obtained to reproduce the $T_{cc}$ binding
energy. We apply this model with superflavor symmetry to the
$\bar{D}^{(*)}\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ molecule and predict a bound state with $I(J^P) =
0(\frac{1}{2}^-)$. If the pentaquark state corresponding to $\bar{D}^{(*)}
\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ molecular state is observed in future experiments as predicted
in this work, it is more likely that $T_{cc}$ is a $DD^*$ molecular state.

sections.5: Gell-Mann, A Schematic Model of Baryons and Mesons, Phys
sections.12: Zweig, An SU(3) model for strong interaction symmetry and its breaking
sections.17: Suzuki, The X(3872) boson: Molecule or charmonium, Phys

authors: Tatsuya Asanuma, Yasuhiro Yamaguchi, Masayasu Harada

qualitative: Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) arises due to the suppression of heavy quark spin interactions by a factor of 𝒪(1/mQ), leading to approximate degeneracy in masses of heavy-light meson spin multiplets.

HQSS implies that the mass differences between pseudoscalar (spin-0) and vector (spin-1) mesons are small, as seen in the charm sector where the mass difference between D* and D is about 140 MeV, comparable to the pion mass.
HQSS is used to justify coupled-channel analyses involving D and D* mesons (or Ξcc and Ξcc* baryons) by treating them as nearly degenerate states, allowing for simplified interaction models.
The symmetry is respected in the one boson exchange model (OBEM) used in this study to describe both the Tcc tetraquark and its superflavor partner D̄(∗)Ξcc(∗) molecule.
quantitative: Mass difference between D* and D mesons: mD∗ − mD ≃ 140 MeV
Mass difference between Ξcc* and Ξcc baryons: mΞcc∗ − mΞcc ≃ 105 MeV
Binding energy of Tcc from Breit-Wigner fit: δmBW = −273 ± 61 ± 5+11−14 keV
Pole position of Tcc: δmpole = −360 ± 40+4−0 keV
Decay width from Breit-Wigner: ΓBW = 410 ± 165 ± 43+18−38 keV
Pole decay width: Γpole = 48 ± 2+0−14 keV

recall_papers.title: Relations among $B_c \to J/\psi, \eta_c$ form factors
recall_papers.abstract:
We analyze the form factors parametrizing the $B_c \to J/\psi, \eta_c$ matrix
elements of the operators in a generalized low-energy $b \to c$ semileptonic
Hamiltonian. We consider an expansion in nonrelativistic QCD, classifying the
heavy quark spin symmetry breaking terms and expressing the form factors in
terms of universal functions in a selected kinematical range. Using as an input
the lattice QCD results for the $B_c \to J/\psi$ matrix element of the SM
operator, we obtain information on other form factors. The extrapolation to the
full kinematical range is also presented.
authors: P. Colangelo, F. De Fazio, F. Loparco, N. Losacco, M. Novoa-Brunet

recall_papers.title:
Is the $\Lambda_c(2625)^+$ the heavy quark spin symmetry partner of the
  $\Lambda_c(2595)^+$?
recall_papers.abstract:
We use a $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_\text{QCD}/m_c)$ heavy quark effective theory
scheme, where only $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_\text{QCD}/m_b)$ and perturbative QCD
short distance corrections are neglected, to study the matrix elements of the
scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector and tensor currents between the
$\Lambda_b$ ground state and the odd parity charm $\Lambda_c(2595)^+$ and
$\Lambda_c(2625)^+$ resonances. We show that in the near-zero recoil regime,
the scheme describes reasonably well, taking into account uncertainties, the
results for the 24 form-factors obtained in lattice QCD (LQCD) just in terms of
only 4 Isgur-Wise (IW) functions. We also find some support for the possibility
that the $\Lambda_c(2595)^+$ and $\Lambda_c(2625)^+$ resonances might form a
heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) doublet. However, we argue that the available
LQCD description of these two resonances is not accurate enough to disentangle
the possible effects of the $\Sigma_c \pi$ and $\Sigma_c^*\pi$ thresholds,
located only a few MeV above their position, and that it cannot be ruled out
that these states are not HQSS partners. Finally, we study the ratio
$\frac{d\Gamma[\Lambda_b\to \Lambda_{c,1/2^-}^* \ell
\bar\nu_\ell]/dq^2}{d\Gamma[\Lambda_b\to \Lambda_{c,3/2^-}^*\ell
\bar\nu_\ell]/dq^2}$ of the Standard Model differential semileptonic decay
widths, with $q^\mu$ the momentum transferred between the initial and final
hadrons. We provide a natural explanation for the existence of large
deviations, near the zero recoil, of this ratio from 1/2 (value predicted in
the infinite heavy quark mass limit, assuming that the $\Lambda_{c,1/2^-}^*$
and $\Lambda_{c,3/2^-}^*$ are the two members of a HQSS doublet) based on
S-wave contributions to the $\Lambda_b\to \Lambda_{c,1/2^-}^*$ decay amplitude
driven by a sub-leading IW function.

authors: Meng-Lin Du, Eliecer Hern\'andez, and Juan Nieves

recall_papers.title: New insights into the doubly charmed exotic mesons
recall_papers.abstract:
Using effective Lagrangians constrained by the heavy quark spin symmetry and
chiral symmetry, for the light quarks, we analyze the $D^0 D^0\pi^+$,
$\bar{D}^0D^0\pi^0$ and $D^0\bar{D}^{*0}$ invariant mass spectra. Performing a
simultaneous analysis of the doubly charmed and charm-anti-charm states gives
further insights into the nature of the $T^+_{cc}$ and $\chi^0_{c1}(3872)$,
exotic hadrons. It is confirmed that both states should lie below their
respective $DD^*$/$D\bar{D}^*$ thresholds. Also, the contributions of the
triangle and box diagrams are negligible.
authors: Di Guo, Qin-He Yang, Ling-Yun Dai, and A. P. Szczepaniak

recall_papers.title:
Masses and radiative decay widths of the $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and
  $D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ and their bottom analogs
recall_papers.abstract:
We study the mass spectra and radiative decays of $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and
$D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ in an unquenched framework. In addition to coupled
channel effects between the $c\bar{s}$ cores and $D^{(*)}K$ channels,
$D^{(*)}K$-$D^{(*)}K$ self interactions are also considered in this work and we
succeed to reproduce their mass spectra. Furthermore, we study the radiative
decays of the $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and $D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ by simultaneously
including the compound structures of conventional $c\bar{s}$ cores and
$D^{(*)}K$ components. We also calculate their bottom analogs with heavy quark
symmetry. Our study offers useful insights into the important unquenched
effects in the formation of $D_{s0}^*(2317)$, $D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ and the
bottom counterparts.

sorted_papers.title:
On the emergence of heavy quark spin symmetry breaking in heavy
  quarkonium decays
sorted_papers.abstract:
Heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) implies that in the direct decay of a heavy
quarkonium with spin $S$, only lower lying heavy quarkonia with the same spin
$S$ can be produced. However, this selection rule, expected to work very well
in the $b$-quark sector, can be overcome if multiquark intermediate states are
involved in the decay chain, allowing for transitions to the final-state heavy
quarkonia with a different spin $S^{\prime}$. In particular, the measured
decays $\Upsilon(10860)\to \pi Z_b^{(\prime)} \to \pi\pi\Upsilon(nS)$
$(n=1,2,3)$ and $\Upsilon(10860)\to \pi Z_b^{(\prime)} \to \pi\pi h_b(mP)$
($m=1,2$) appear to have nearly equal strengths which is conventionally
explained by a simultaneous presence of both $S_{b\bar{b}}=0$ and
$S_{b\bar{b}}=1$ components in the wave functions of the $Z_b$'s in equal
shares. Meanwhile, the destructive interference between the contributions of
the $Z_b$ and $Z_b'$ to the decay amplitude for a $\pi\pi h_b$ final state
kills the signal to zero in the strict HQSS limit. In this paper, we discuss
how the HQSS violation needs to be balanced by the narrowness of the
$Z_b^{(\prime)}$ states in the physical case, to allow for equal transition
strengths into final states with different total heavy quark spins, and how
spin symmetry is restored as a result of a subtle interplay of the scales
involved, when the mass of a heavy quark becomes infinite. Moreover, we
demonstrate how similar branching fractions of the decays into $\pi\pi h_b$ and
$\pi\pi\Upsilon$ can be obtained and how the mentioned HQSS breaking can be
reconciled with the dispersive approach to the $\pi\pi/ K\bar K$ interaction in
the final state and matched with the low-energy chiral dynamics in both final
states.

qualitative: Heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) implies that in the direct decay of a heavy quarkonium with spin S, only lower-lying heavy quarkonia with the same spin S can be produced.
HQSS is expected to hold very well in the b-quark sector, but can be violated if multiquark intermediate states are involved in the decay chain.

The decays Υ(10860) → π Z_b^{(′)} → ππ Υ(nS) and Υ(10860) → π Z_b^{(′)} → ππ h_b(mP) exhibit nearly equal branching fractions, which is conventionally explained by equal contributions from S_{b\bar{b}} = 0 and S_{b\bar{b}} = 1 components in the Z_b^{(′)} wave functions.
In the strict HQSS limit, destructive interference between Z_b and Z_b' contributions to the ππ h_b final state suppresses the signal to zero.
HQSS violation must be balanced by the narrowness of the Z_b^{(′)} states in the physical case to allow equal transition strengths into final states with different total heavy quark spins.
Spin symmetry is restored in the limit of infinite heavy quark mass due to a subtle interplay of the scales involved.

The ππ/KK̄ final state interaction (FSI) in the decay channels can be described using dispersive methods, such as twice-subtracted dispersion integrals, with subtraction terms fixed by chiral dynamics.

For transitions Υ → ππ h_b, the required counterterms in the dispersive approach violate HQSS and are expected to be strongly suppressed, yet the observed similar branching fractions suggest a reconciliation of this suppression with the S-wave ππ/KK̄ FSI.
quantitative: Mass splitting between Z_b and Z_b' states is approximately 45 MeV.
Branching fractions for Υ(10860) → ππ Υ(nS) and Υ(10860) → ππ h_b(mP) are nearly equal (Br[Υ → ππ h_b(mP)] ≈ Br[Υ → ππ Υ(nS)]).
The branching fraction for Υ → (π+π−π0)non-ω χ_bJ is suppressed by about an order of magnitude compared to the ππ Υ(nS) and ππ h_b(mP) channels.
The relevant energy range for the decays is near the B(∗)B̄∗ thresholds, where the centrifugal barrier suppression is not operative.
The ππ/KK̄ final state interaction is modeled via twice-subtracted dispersion integrals, with subtraction terms determined by chiral symmetry.

content_sections.I Introduction: The spectroscopy of hadronic states containing heavy quarks remains one of the fastest developing and most intriguing branches of studies of the strong interaction. Many new states have been discovered in the spectrum of charmonium and bottomonium, which do not fit into the quark model scheme and qualify as exotic states. For example, the states Zb±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10610Zbpm, Zb±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10650Zbpm citebib.bib1, Zc±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑐plus-or-minus3900Zcpm citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, Zc±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑐plus-or-minus4020Zcpm citebib.bib4, Z±​superscript𝑍plus-or-minus4430Zpmcitebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8 are charged and decay into final states containing a heavy quark Q𝑄Q and its antiquark Q¯¯𝑄barQ plus light hadrons. Therefore, their minimal quark content is four quarks. The interested reader can find a comprehensive overview of the current experimental and theoretical status of the exotic hadrons with heavy quarks in the dedicated review papers, for example, in Refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15. The bottomoniumlike states Zb±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10610Zbpm and Zb±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10650Zbpm (for brevity in what follows often referred to as Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb and Zb′superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′Zbprime, respectively) are an ideal laboratory to get a better understanding of exotic states, since they exist as two resonances with the same JP​C=1+−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1+-citebib.bib16, split by only about 45 MeV and are seen simultaneously in several modes. Specifically, the Belle Collaboration observed them as distinct peaks (i) in the invariant mass distributions of the π±​Υ​(n​S)superscript𝜋plus-or-minusΥ𝑛𝑆pipmUpsilon(nS) (n=1,2,3𝑛123n=1,2,3) and π±​hb​(m​P)superscript𝜋plus-or-minussubscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃pipmhb(mP) (m=1,2𝑚12m=1,2) subsystems in the dipion transitions from the vector bottomonium Υ​Υ10860Upsilon citebib.bib1 and (ii) in the elastic B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB*1Hereinafter a properly normalised C𝐶C-odd combination of the B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* and B¯​B∗¯𝐵superscript𝐵barBB* components is understood. and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* channels in the decays Υ​→π​B(∗)​B¯∗→Υ10860𝜋superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵Upsilontopi B(*)barB* with the dominant branching fractions citebib.bib17, bib.bib18. In addition, the Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb states could in principle be experimentally searched for (and hopefully seen) in the ρ​ηb𝜌subscript𝜂𝑏rhoetab channel. More specifically, because of a large ρ𝜌rho-meson width, the relevant channel to look at would be Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​(π​π)I=1​ηb→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋subscript𝜋𝜋𝐼1subscript𝜂𝑏Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topi(pipi)I=1,etab, which is however difficult experimentally because of a potentially large background related to the presence of the neutral pion in the final state. Meanwhile, no data for the given channel exist to date. On the other hand, the measured branching fractions Υ​→(π+​π−​π0)non−ω​χb​J→Υ10860subscriptsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0non𝜔subscript𝜒𝑏𝐽Upsilonto(pi+pi-pi0)rm non-omega,chibJ (J=1,2𝐽12J=1,2) are by an order of magnitude suppressed citebib.bib19 relative to the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) and π​π​hb​(m​P)𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃pipi hb(mP) hidden-flavour decay channels considered in this work. One may therefore expect that the analogous branching for the π​(π​π)I=1​ηb𝜋subscript𝜋𝜋𝐼1subscript𝜂𝑏pi(pipi)I=1,etab final state is also negligibly small. The fact that the ρ𝜌rho and χb​Jsubscript𝜒𝑏𝐽chibJ are in a relative P𝑃P wave in contrast to the S𝑆S-wave final state ρ​ηb𝜌subscript𝜂𝑏rhoetab should not affect this estimate since both channels open far away from the relevant energy range near the B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* thresholds, so the centrifugal barrier suppression should not be operative any longer. Also, the measured elastic and inelastic branching fractions for Υ​Υ10860Upsilon listed in the items (i) and (ii) above leave only very little room to other possible contributions. The two most prominent explanations for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s claimed to be consistent with the data are provided by a tetraquark model and a hadronic molecule picture, see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib15 for review articles and references therein. A review of the sum rules approach to the exotic states with heavy quarks and relevant references on the subject can be found in Ref. citebib.bib20. It should be noted that a particularly close location of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s to the thresholds of the B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* channels provides a strong hint in favour of their molecular interpretation. Both the Zb±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10610Zbpm and Zb±​superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10650Zbpm contain a heavy b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb pair, so it is commonly accepted that heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) should be realised to high accuracy in these systems and indeed, as will be reviewed in Sec. , HQSS is able to explain naturally the interference pattern in the channels Zb(′)→π​Υ​(n​S)→superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆Zb(prime)topiUpsilon(nS) and Zb(′)→π​hb​(m​P)→superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Zb(prime)topi hb(mP) citebib.bib21. In Sec. we demonstrate how the experimental observation that Br​[Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)]Brdelimited-→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃mboxBr[Upsilontopipi hb(mP)] is qualitatively similar or actually even larger than Br​[Υ​→π​π​Υ​(n​S)]Brdelimited-→Υ10860𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆mboxBr[UpsilontopipiUpsilon(nS)] citebib.bib19 can be reconciled with HQSS and its violation. It appears as a result of a subtle interplay of the scales involved in the system — most relevant being the relationship between the widths of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states and their mass difference. Also, we discuss the restoration of spin symmetry as the heavy quark mass is taken to be infinitely large. Section is devoted to the inclusion of the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK interaction in the final state. In particular, in Refs. citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24 it is explained how the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK final state interaction (FSI) can be included in the spin conserving heavy meson decays Υ→π​π​Υ′→Υ𝜋𝜋superscriptΥ′UpsilontopipiUpsilonprime by means of twice subtracted dispersion integrals. Here the structure of the subtraction terms can be fixed by the chiral structure of the transition amplitudes, giving rise to Υ→π​π​Υ′→Υ𝜋𝜋superscriptΥ′UpsilontopipiUpsilonprime contact interactions. While the chiral structure for the transitions of the kind Υ→π​π​hb→Υ𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏Upsilontopipi hb is the same, such counter terms violate HQSS and thus are expected to be strongly suppressed quantitatively. How this pattern can be reconciled with the S𝑆S-wave π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI also for this kind of transitions is discussed in Sec. . We summarise in Sec. .

Therefore, relations (LABEL:Zbs3) entail two important messages2Because of the coupled-channel transitions, the physical Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s appear to be dynamical mixtures of the basis states introduced in Eq. . Thus, these messages should be regarded as being originated from the strict HQSS. • in the strict HQSS limit both Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states carry spin-0 and spin-1 components with equal weight and • the spin-0 and spin-1 components appear with different relative signs in the two states. Importantly, as demonstrated in Ref. citebib.bib21, the above nontrivial interference pattern is indeed consistent with the data, where the two Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb peaks appear to be equally strong in the decay chains Υ​Υ10860displaystyleUpsilon →→displaystyleto π​Zb(′)→π​π​Υ​(n​S),n=1,2,3,formulae-sequence→𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆𝑛123displaystylepi Zb(prime)topipiUpsilon(nS),quad n=1,2,3, Υ​Υ10860displaystyleUpsilon →→displaystyleto π​Zb(′)→π​π​hb​(m​P),m=1,2,formulae-sequence→𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃𝑚12displaystylepi Zb(prime)topipi hb(mP),quad m=1,2, with a constructive interference in the former and a destructive interference in the latter case. This underlines the fact that, although in general coupled-channel transitions mix the basis states , nevertheless, both wave functions of the physical Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s still contain the Sb​b¯=0subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏0Sbbarb=0 and Sb​b¯=1subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏1Sbbarb=1 components in comparable shares. The same pattern emerges in the tetraquark picture proposed in Ref. citebib.bib25, where the driving subclusters are compact diquarks and anti-diquarks. In contrast to the two scenarios just sketched, it should be stressed that without sizable heavy-quark spin-0 and spin-1 clusters within the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states the observed pattern is unexplained and appears to be very unnatural. In the limit of the heavy quark mass going to infinity, the heavy-quark spin needs to be conserved. It is thus interesting to investigate, how this limit is reached in the scenario outlined above. It turns out that what controls the importance of the hbsubscriptℎ𝑏hb final states is the ratio of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s mass splitting, which violates HQSS and thus vanishes in the heavy quark limit, and the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s widths, which survive the heavy quark limit. This will be discussed in detail below.
content_sections.III Spin-flip and spin-conserving transi: In this section we study the transition amplitudes for the reactions Υ→π​π​Υ′→Υ𝜋𝜋superscriptΥ′UpsilontopipiUpsilonprime and Υ→π​π​hb→Υ𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏Upsilontopipi hb via two Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states and discuss (i) the scales that make it possible for the two amplitudes to have signals of a similar size for the physical masses of the heavy states involved and (ii) how the heavy-quark limit can be restored. content_sections.IV Inclusion of the pion interaction in: In the previous section it was demonstrated how the explicit HQSS breaking due to the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb mass splitting could be balanced by their narrow widths to provide similar branching fractions for the reactions with and without the heavy-quark spin flip. Here we discuss consequences of yet another peculiar property of the HQSS violation in the reaction Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopipi hb(mP) as compared with the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final states. Namely, the tree level amplitude of the reaction Υ​→π​π​Υ​(n​S)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆UpsilontopipiUpsilon(nS) admits two momentum-dependent chiral contact terms (with two derivatives of the pion fields), which parameterize a short range interaction in the system, already in the strict heavy-quark limitcitebib.bib30, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24. These contact terms can be matched with two subtraction constants needed to include the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in this reaction. In contrast, the leading contact interactions in the decays Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopipi hb(mP), while having the same chiral structure, violate HQSS and therefore should be strongly suppressed relative to those for the transitions that conserve the heavy quark spin. In what follows, we discuss how this fact can be reconciled with the fact that in the individual reactions Υ​→π​Zb→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋subscript𝑍𝑏→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zbtopipi hb(mP) and Υ​→π​Zb′→π​π​hb​(m​P),→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zbprimetopipi hb(mP), the same number of subtractions is required to render the dispersive integrals finite as in the channel with the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final state. Specifically, we demonstrate that the destructive interference of the two amplitudes in the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb channel (as opposed to the constructive interference for the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final state) provides a better convergence of the resulting dispersive integral, so that a smaller number of subtractions is required. To bring a quantitative twist to this discussion, we employ the coupled-channel EFT-based approach developed for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states in Refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib31 with the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI for the transition Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopipi hb(mP) to demonstrate that its role in this channel is indeed suppressed, in contrast to the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final states citebib.bib24.

In this section we employ the dispersive approach developed in the previous section to generalise the analysis of the experimental data on the dipion transitions Υ​→π+​π−​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860superscript𝜋superscript𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi+pi-hb(mP) with m=1,2𝑚12m=1,2 performed in Ref. citebib.bib28. In particular, we check against the data that the effect of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI is small for a counter term with a strength in line with expectations from heavy quark spin symmetry. In order to proceed, we stick to the technique of the spectral function previously employed in Ref. citebib.bib24 to write M0hb;L​(s)=∫μmin2μmax2𝑑μ2​ρ​(μ2)​M0,stablehb;L​(s,μ),superscriptsubscript𝑀0subscriptℎ𝑏𝐿𝑠superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜇2minsubscriptsuperscript𝜇2maxdifferential-dsuperscript𝜇2𝜌superscript𝜇2superscriptsubscript𝑀0stablesubscriptℎ𝑏𝐿𝑠𝜇M0hb;L(s)=intmu2rm minmu2rm maxdmu2rho(mu2)M0,textstablehb;L(s,mu), where the amplitude M0,stablehb;Lsuperscriptsubscript𝑀0stablesubscriptℎ𝑏𝐿M0,textstablehb;L for stable Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s was defined in the previous section while the integration limits are set as in Ref. citebib.bib24. The spectral density function ρ​(μ2)𝜌superscript𝜇2rho(mu2) can be evaluated as ρ​(μ2)=−1π​Im​U​(μ2),𝜌superscript𝜇21𝜋Im𝑈superscript𝜇2rho(mu2)=-frac1pimboxIm,U(mu2), with U​(μ2)𝑈superscript𝜇2U(mu2) referring to the production amplitude for Υ​→π+​π−​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860superscript𝜋superscript𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi+pi-hb(mP) built in Ref. citebib.bib28 (fit A) via the B∗​B(∗)superscript𝐵superscript𝐵B*B(*)-meson loops but without considering the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI4The function U​(μ2)𝑈superscript𝜇2U(mu2) contains also the effect of the finite width of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states discussed above. . Furthermore, since the latter effect is expected to be small, we do not refit the data but employ all the parameters found in Ref. citebib.bib28 from the fit to the data and only check how the line shapes change upon including the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI and varying the HQSS-breaking constant within an interval consistent with naturalness. In particular, in Fig. we demonstrate the modification of the line shapes in the channels π+​π−​hb​(m​P)superscript𝜋superscript𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃pi+pi-hb(mP) with m=1,2𝑚12m=1,2 when the coefficient c𝑐c in Eq. varies within a natural interval. For definiteness and keeping in mind that the values of c𝑐c consistent with naturalness should be |c|∼1similar-to𝑐1|c|sim 1, we choose a conservative interval |c|⩽10𝑐10|c|leqslant 10. Comparing the red band, which shows the spread with the parameter c𝑐c of the line shapes with the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI included, and the black solid curve, which represents the fit built in Ref. citebib.bib28, one can conclude that, in agreement with the arguments provided in this section, the modification of the line shapes due to the inclusion of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi interaction in the final state is marginal. To be more quantitative, in terms of the χ2/Ndofsuperscript𝜒2subscript𝑁dofchi2/Nrm dof this corresponds to a spread from 1.21 to 1.36 as compared with the value 1.29 obtained for the fit A from Ref. citebib.bib28. Moreover, if c𝑐c is allowed to vary freely, the best fit to the data yields c​(1​P)=24𝑐1𝑃24c(1P)=24 and c​(2​P)=−24𝑐2𝑃24c(2P)=-24 with the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2/dof =1.16, which is only marginally smaller than the lower bound within the natural interval (the red band in Fig. ). However, such values of the parameter c𝑐c returned by the best fit are formally somewhat larger than what we regard as natural. We note also, as a disclaimer, that the fit may use the freedom of varying c𝑐c to partly compensate for other approximations made in the contact fit A of Ref. citebib.bib28, for example, the effect of the neglected one-pion exchange. Since the resulting effect for the hbsubscriptℎ𝑏hb final states is small, we do not dwell on this any further in this work. Although the inclusion of the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in an EFT approach with non-perturbative pions (see, e. g. , Ref. ) is technically more demanding because of the additional left-hand cut introduced in this way, this would be an important step in the future more quantitative studies.
content_sections.V Summary: In this work we investigate the consequences of heavy-quark spin symmetry and the pattern of its breaking for the measured decays Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​Υ​(n​S)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipiUpsilon(nS) (n=1,2,3𝑛123n=1,2,3) and Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipi hb(mP) (m=1,2𝑚12m=1,2). These decays appear to have nearly equal probabilities in spite of the fact that a direct transition from Sb​b¯=1subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏1Sbbarb=1 Υ​Υ10860Upsilon to Sb​b¯=0subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏0Sbbarb=0 hb​(m​P)subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃hb(mP) changes heavy-quark spin and therefore violates HQSS. As argued in Ref. citebib.bib21, this can be naturally understood in the molecular scenario for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states, because in this case Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb appear as orthogonal combinations of the Sb​b¯=0subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏0Sbbarb=0 and Sb​b¯=1subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏1Sbbarb=1 components of equal strength. On the other hand, the interference between the contributions of the Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb to the decay amplitude for a π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final state is destructive which results in a vanishing signal in the strict HQSS limit. In contrast to this, the signal for a π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon final state is fully in line with HQSS due to a constructive interference between the two Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states. Thus, in order to have signals of a similar strength in the π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon and π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final states, HQSS in the latter channel must be violated (mz′−mz≠0subscript𝑚superscript𝑧′subscript𝑚𝑧0mzprime-mzneq 0), and this violation should be balanced by another quantity with dimension of a mass of a similar (small) size. We demonstrate that this balance is provided by the small widths of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states ΓzsubscriptΓ𝑧Gammaz. As a consequence, the transition amplitude to the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final state strongly depends on the ratio of these two scales, r=(mz′−mz)/Γz𝑟superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑧′subscript𝑚𝑧subscriptΓ𝑧r=(mzprime-mz)/Gammaz, as given in Eqs. and . Remarkably, while the ratio r𝑟r vanishes in the strict HQSS limit, its value rphys≈3subscript𝑟phys3rrm physapprox 3 obtained for the physical masses of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s corresponds to the dynamical regime which is already quite close to the opposite limit of r→∞→𝑟rtoinfty — in this limit the absolute values of the transition amplitudes to the π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon and π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final states are shown to have exactly the same peak positions and strengths. Also, we pinpoint a crucial difference related to HQSS in the role played by the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in the dipion transitions Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​Υ​(n​S)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipiUpsilon(nS) and Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipi hb(mP). We demonstrate how the absence of the direct short-range HQSS-conserving operators in the bottomonium transitions Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilon topipi hb(mP) can be reconciled with a subtraction in the dispersive integral if the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI is taken into account. In particular, it is shown that when both Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states are included simultaneously, their leading contributions to the total amplitude cancel against each other. As a result, the dispersive integrals in the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb channels demonstrate a better convergence than those in the π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon channels and the remaining subtraction constants in the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb amplitudes are indeed suppressed by HQSS. We have verified that, in agreement with natural expectations, implementing this scheme to take into account the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI does not result in any appreciable change in the fitted line shapes in the π​π​hb​(1​P)𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏1𝑃pipi hb(1P) and π​π​hb​(2​P)𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏2𝑃pipi hb(2P) channels (contrary to the crucial role played by the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) channels), so that neglecting the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in the previous analysis of Ref. citebib.bib28, bib.bib31 is indeed justified, at least at the present level of accuracy dictated by the quality of the existing data. Meanwhile, our findings establish a way to improve on such an analysis when/if such improvements are finally called for by the experiment. As a final remark, we notice that while the numerical analysis performed in this work relies on a particular fit to the data built in the framework of the molecular approach for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s, the qualitative arguments presented in this paper are solely based on the interplay of different scales (the mass splitting mz′−mzsubscript𝑚superscript𝑧′subscript𝑚𝑧mzprime-mz and the width ΓzsubscriptΓ𝑧Gammaz in the first place) and therefore can equally apply to any approach to the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s which is consistent with HQSS and its leading breaking effects, in particular, to the tetraquark model. Our findings concerning the role played by the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI should be also important for understanding analogous charmonium transitions to the final states π​π​ψ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋𝜓𝑛𝑆pipipsi(nS) and π​π​hc​(m​P)𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑐𝑚𝑃pipi hc(mP) via the Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zc and Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐4020Zc states, though the effects of the HQSS violation in the charmonium sector are expected to be larger. Acknowledgements. The authors thank Saša Prelovšek for a fruitful discussion that triggered this project. This work is supported in part by the NSFC and the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG) through the funds provided to the Sino-German Collaborative Research Center “Symmetries and the Emergence of Structure in QCD” (NSFC Grant No. 11621131001, DFG Grant No. CRC110) and by BMBF (contract No. 05P21PCFP1). A. N. is supported by the Slovenian Research Agency (research core Funding No. P1-0035).

sorted_papers.title: Heavy-Quark spin symmetry breaking in the Born-Oppenheimer approximation
sorted_papers.abstract:
Heavy-quark spin symmetry is explicitly broken by the mass splitting between
a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson and its vector partner. This fact plays a
pivotal role in the physics of states whose mass lies close to the threshold of
an open-flavor meson pair, like $X(3872)$. We show that this source of
heavy-quark spin symmetry breaking can be systematically included within the
diabatic representation of the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. We verify that
including all the appropriate coupled channels guarantees conservation of total
angular momentum, parity, and charge-conjugation parity. This marks a
fundamental step towards a unified, first-principles study of quarkonia and
meson molecules with hidden flavor.

sections.16: Wise, Chiral perturbation theory for hadrons containing a heavy quark, Phys
sections.19: Lichten, Resonant charge exchange in atomic collisions, Phys
sections.20: Wigner, Group Theory and its Application to the Quantum Mechanics of Atomic Spectra, Academic Press, New York (1959).
sections.22: Voloshin, Heavy quark spin symmetry breaking in near-threshold JP​C=1−−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJ^{PC}=1^{--} quarkonium-like resonances, Phys

qualitative: Heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) is explicitly broken by the mass splitting between a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson (e.g., B) and its vector partner (e.g., B*).
This HQSS breaking plays a crucial role in the physics of states near open-flavor meson pair thresholds, such as the X(3872).

In the Born-Oppenheimer (BO) approximation, HQSS is typically assumed in lattice QCD simulations with static quarks, but this assumption becomes unreliable when considering coupled quark-antiquark and di-meson channels near thresholds.
The breaking of HQSS leads to spin-dependent splitting of di-meson thresholds and modifies the transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations.
The diabatic representation of the BO approximation allows for a systematic and consistent inclusion of HQSS breaking by constructing a spin-dependent interaction matrix.
The inclusion of heavy quark kinetic energies promotes the cylindrical symmetry group D∞h ⊗ CP to the full O(3) ⊗ CP symmetry of QCD, reflecting a more complete description of the system.
The formalism enables a unified, first-principles study of quarkonia and meson molecules with hidden flavor, particularly in the presence of string breaking and spin effects.
quantitative: The D*–D mass splitting is approximately three times larger than the B*–B mass splitting.
The transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations are determined from Dirac algebra and form a matrix of coupling coefficients.
The interaction matrix in the diabatic representation depends explicitly on the spin configuration of the heavy quark-antiquark pair.
Linear combinations of matrix elements, weighted by coefficients from angular momentum theory, yield the potential matrix entries used in the Schrödinger equation.
The master Schrödinger equation in the diabatic BO framework includes both quark-antiquark and di-meson channels with spin-dependent couplings and threshold splittings.

authors: R. Bruschini

content_sections.1 Introduction: In the Born-Oppenheimer (BO) approximation for QCD, the spectrum of a heavy quark-antiquark system is determined by potentials which are numerically accessible in lattice QCD citebib.bib1. Because of this feature, the BO picture is one of the most promising tools for the study of conventional and unconventional heavy quark-antiquark states from first principles; see, for instance, refs. citebib.bib2, bib.bib3 and references therein. The idea of using lattice QCD calculations of the BO potentials that include the effects of string breaking to precisely determine the spectrum and decays of heavy quark-antiquark systems has been around for quite some time citebib.bib4. Although direct lattice measurements of string breaking have been available for many years citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, achieving such a description remains a current theoretical challenge. Until recently, the main difficulty consisted in overcoming the single-channel approximation traditionally associated with BO citebib.bib2, as string breaking couples quark-antiquark and di-meson channels. Significant theoretical progress in the application of BO with coupled channels has been reported. This includes, for instance, calculations of the bottomonium spectrum in BO using the first calculation of string breaking in lattice QCD citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, phenomenological studies of quarkoniumlike mesons and open-flavor di-meson scattering in the diabatic representation of BO citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, analyses of exotic heavy hadrons in the diabatic representation of the diquark model citebib.bib11, and calculations of threshold effects in a BO effective field theory citebib.bib12. Nevertheless, there is yet another constraint customarily linked with BO that must be removed in order to carry out precise, ab initio studies of heavy quark-antiquark states: heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS). Current BO calculations of the bottomonium spectrum with string breaking citebib.bib7, bib.bib8 rely on HQSS, since it is realized for lattice QCD simulations with static quarks. However, because of the mass splitting between a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson B𝐵B and its vector partner B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast, HQSS is broken by the mixing of quarkoniumlike resonances with di-meson pairs. In fact, this HQSS-breaking effect has been used to explain the hadronic decays of Υ​(4​S)Υ4𝑆Upsilon(4S) and Υ​Υ10860Upsilon; see, for instance, refs. citebib.bib13, bib.bib14 and references therein. In light of this, one could question the reliability of HQSS-based calculations of coupled quark-antiquark and di-meson channels, especially for energies close to a meson-pair threshold. The situation is even more problematic in the charmoniumlike sector, with a D∗superscript𝐷∗Dast-D𝐷D splitting three times bigger than the B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast-B𝐵B splitting. Phenomenological analyses citebib.bib15 indicate that the precise location of the di-meson thresholds may be decisive for the understanding of exotic states like X​𝑋3872X. The problem of HQSS breaking in the BO approximation has been addressed in the case of u​d​b¯​b¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑏¯𝑏udbarbbarb tetraquarks by including the B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast-B𝐵B mass splitting and solving the resulting Schrödinger equations for the coupled BO channels citebib.bib16. However, the u​d​b¯​b¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑏¯𝑏udbarbbarb tetraquark case is made somewhat simpler by the absence of any string breaking, hence the formalism described in ref. citebib.bib16 is not easily extended to other situations where string breaking plays a prominent role. In this work, we show that HQSS breaking can be systematically and consistently included in the diabatic representation of the BO approximation. The effect of HQSS breaking is to split the thresholds and replace the single string-breaking transition rate from lattice QCD by a matrix of transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations, with coefficients determined from Dirac algebra. The quark-antiquark static energy, di-meson thresholds, and the transition rates form an interaction matrix that depends on the spin configuration of the heavy sources. Linear combinations of the interaction matrix elements for different spin configurations, with coefficients determined from angular momentum theory, form the entries of a potential matrix from which the spectrum of the heavy quark-antiquark system can be calculated. As a byproduct, we verify that the cylindrical symmetry group of a static quark-antiquark pair, D∞​h⊗C​Ptensor-productsubscript𝐷ℎ𝐶𝑃Dinfty hotimes CP, gets naturally promoted to the full O​⊗Ctensor-product𝑂3𝐶Ootimes C symmetry group of QCD when the kinetic energies of the heavy quarks are introduced. This result constitutes a substantial amendment to the previous phenomenological analyses in refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib10 by determining the matrix of coupling coefficients that in these studies was arbitrarily fixed ad hoc. It provides a foothold for a detailed, unified study of heavy quark-antiquark systems from ab initio lattice QCD simulations with static quarks. The contents of this paper are organized as follows. In section, we thoroughly review the static approximation for a heavy quark-antiquark pair. In section, we calculate the transition rates between quark-antiquark and the various di-meson configurations in terms of the string-breaking transition rate. In section, we include violation of HQSS by threshold spin splittings. In section, we introduce the kinetic energies of the heavy quarks in the diabatic representation of BO and write down the master Schrödinger equation with quark-antiquark and di-meson channels. Finally, in section, we summarize our findings and indicate possible future developments.
content_sections.2 Static approximation: For the system of a heavy quark-antiquark pair with strong interactions, the time scale for the motion of the heavy quarks, dictated by the heavy-quark mass mQsubscript𝑚𝑄mQ, is considerably larger than the time scale for the evolution of the gluon and light-quark fields, dictated by the nonperturbative QCD energy scale ΛQCDsubscriptΛQCDLambdatextupQCD. Because of this sharp separation of the time scales involved, it makes sense to expand the Hamiltonian of the heavy quark-antiquark system in powers of ΛQCD/mQsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑄LambdatextupQCD/mQ. The leading order contribution in this expansion corresponds to the infinite mass limit for the heavy quarks, mQ→∞→subscript𝑚𝑄mQtoinfty. In this limit, the heavy quark-antiquark pair has no motion and acts as a static source for the light QCD fields. The energy levels of the dynamical fields, that is, gluons and light quarks, are not affected by the spins of the static quarks. This decoupling of the heavy-quark spins is referred to as HQSS. Hence, let us begin by considering QCD with static quark (Q𝑄Q) and antiquark (Q¯¯𝑄barQ) sources at the positions +12​𝒓12𝒓+frac12r and −12​𝒓12𝒓-frac12r. In this static approximation, 𝒓𝒓r is a constant parameter. The dynamical fields are gluons and light quarks (q𝑞q) and antiquarks (q¯¯𝑞barq). The relevant symmetry group of QCD is O​⊗Ctensor-product𝑂3𝐶Ootimes C, where O​𝑂3O is the group formed by three-dimensional rotations and the parity transformation P𝑃P, and C𝐶C is the charge conjugation. However, the static quarks break the symmetry down to the cylindrical subgroup D∞​h⊗C​Ptensor-productsubscript𝐷ℎ𝐶𝑃Dinfty hotimes CP, which consists of: • rotations around the 𝒓bold-𝒓hatr axis; • a reflection ℛℛR through a plane containing 𝒓𝒓r; • the combined transformation C​P𝐶𝑃CP. There are several terms in the total angular momentum 𝑱𝑱J of a heavy quark-antiquark system. We denote the light Q​C​D𝑄𝐶𝐷QCD angular momentum of the dynamical fields by 𝑱lightsubscript𝑱lightJtextuplight. The total spin of the heavy quarks is 𝑺Q​Q¯subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄SQbarQ. The orbital angular momentum of the sources is 𝑳𝑳L. The total angular momentum is then 𝑱=𝑳+𝑺Q​Q¯+𝑱light. 𝑱𝑳subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄subscript𝑱lightJ=L+SQbarQ+Jtextuplight. It is also convenient to introduce the static spin defined by 𝑺=𝑺Q​Q¯+𝑱light. 𝑺subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄subscript𝑱lightS=SQbarQ+Jtextuplight. The representations of D∞​h⊗C​Ptensor-productsubscript𝐷ℎ𝐶𝑃Dinfty hotimes CP are conventionally labeled as ΛηϵsuperscriptsubscriptΛ𝜂italic-ϵLambdaetaepsilon, where: • Λ=|𝑱light⋅𝒓|Λ⋅subscript𝑱lightbold-𝒓Lambda=lvertJtextuplightcdothatrrvert is the modulus of the projection of the light QCD angular momentum 𝑱lightsubscript𝑱lightJtextuplight on 𝒓bold-𝒓hatr. The values of ΛΛLambda are indicated by Greek letters: ΣΣSigma, ΠΠPi, ΔΔDelta for Λ=0,1,2Λ012Lambda=0,1,2, respectively, and so on. • η=g𝜂𝑔eta=g or u𝑢u stands for C​P=+𝐶𝑃CP=+ or −-, respectively. • ϵ=±italic-ϵplus-or-minusepsilon=pm is the eigenvalue of ℛℛR in the case Λ=0Λ0Lambda=0, and it is omitted for Λ>0Λ0Lambda>0. The energy levels of light QCD in the presence of the static sources can be labeled by the light BO quantum numbers ΛηϵsuperscriptsubscriptΛ𝜂italic-ϵLambdaetaepsilon. One could also define static BO quantum numbers for the system that consists of the light QCD fields and the spins of the heavy quarks, with static spin quantum number s𝑠s and projection λ=𝑺⋅𝒓𝜆⋅𝑺bold-𝒓lambda=Scdothatr. In the case λ=0𝜆0lambda=0, the value of ϵitalic-ϵepsilon is completely determined by the static spin s𝑠s, the intrinsic parity 𝔭𝔭mathfrakp of the source, and the light BO quantum numbers Jlightsubscript𝐽lightJtextuplight and ϵlightsubscriptitalic-ϵlightepsilontextuplight as ϵ=ϵlight​𝔭​(−1)Jlight−sitalic-ϵsubscriptitalic-ϵlight𝔭superscript1subscript𝐽light𝑠epsilon=epsilontextuplightmathfrakp(-1)Jtextuplight-s (see appendix), making this quantum number redundant. Thus, we label the configurations by the static spin projection λ𝜆lambda with sign and η𝜂eta, omitting ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. We shall denote these static BO quantum numbers that also take into account the spin of the heavy quarks by ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta, such as 0gsubscript0𝑔0g, 0usubscript0𝑢0u, ±1gplus-or-minussubscript1𝑔pm 1g, ±1uplus-or-minussubscript1𝑢pm 1u, ±2gplus-or-minussubscript2𝑔pm 2g, ±2uplus-or-minussubscript2𝑢pm 2u, and so on. In this paper, we shall concentrate on light QCD configurations with BO quantum numbers Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+, which can be produced by a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source and by di-meson sources. For a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source, that is, the product of Q𝑄Q and Q¯¯𝑄barQ fields connected by a gauge transporter, Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ corresponds to Jlight=0subscript𝐽light0Jtextuplight=0, so that 𝑺=𝑺Q​Q¯𝑺subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄S=SQbarQ. For a di-meson source, that is, a product of Q​q¯𝑄¯𝑞Qbarq and q​Q¯𝑞¯𝑄qbarQ operators, 𝑺𝑺S coincides with the total spin of the di-meson pair. So, for Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and di-meson sources producing light Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ configurations, s𝑠s and λ𝜆lambda are just total spin quantum numbers. Note, however, that for a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source the individual spin quantum numbers are 1212frac12, while for a di-meson source the individual spin quantum numbers are integers. In theory, a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source generates all Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ light QCD configurations, labeled by n=1,2,3,…𝑛123…n=1,2,3,dots in increasing energy levels. However, at any given Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ distance r=|𝒓|𝑟𝒓r=lvertrrvert, many of these light QCD configurations will have a very small overlap with the quark-antiquark source, making it difficult to detect them in lattice QCD simulations with finite numerical precision. A practical workaround is to consider the correlation between quark-antiquark and di-meson sources. Following the calculations of the Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ BO potentials with string breaking in lattice QCD citebib.bib5, we truncate the operator expansion by considering only di-meson sources for negative-parity mesons, which yields a good description of the ground n=1𝑛1n=1 and the first excited n=2𝑛2n=2 Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ BO configurations.1In the case of string breaking by light and strange quarks, one can get a good description of the first three configurations by including also di-meson sources for strange negative-parity mesons citebib.bib6. Here we do not consider di-meson sources for positive-parity mesons, which are presumably required for the calculation of excited Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ light QCD configurations with larger n𝑛n. Neither do we include configurations with light BO quantum numbers ΠusubscriptΠ𝑢Piu, Σu−superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑢Sigmau-, etc. , also referred to as hybrid configurations. Hence, the current analysis is restricted to energies well below the lowest threshold for the production of a positive-parity meson (B​B¯1𝐵subscript¯𝐵1BbarB1) and the energy of the lowest quarkonium hybrid meson. We shall label the quark-antiquark and di-meson sources producing ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta configurations by their total spin s𝑠s and projection λ𝜆lambda. Note that in the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ, B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB, and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗BastbarBast cases the quantum number η𝜂eta is determined by the total spin of the source as C​P=(−1)s+1𝐶𝑃superscript1𝑠1CP=(-1)s+1 for quark-antiquark sources and C​P=(−1)s𝐶𝑃superscript1𝑠CP=(-1)s for di-meson sources. On the other hand, one can construct two independent combinations of B𝐵B and B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast, (B​B¯∗∓B∗​B¯)/2minus-or-plus𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗superscript𝐵∗¯𝐵2(BbarBastmp BastbarB)/sqrt2 with C​P=±𝐶𝑃plus-or-minusCP=pm. In this last case, we shall specify the corresponding quantum number η𝜂eta of the meson pair as B​B¯∗η𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗absent𝜂BbarBasteta whenever necessary. Hence, we shall indicate the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ sources with s=0,1𝑠01s=0,1 as 𝒬s,λsubscript𝒬𝑠𝜆Qs,lambda, the B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB source with s=0𝑠0s=0 as ℬ0,0subscriptℬ00B0,0, the B​B¯∗η𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗absent𝜂BbarBasteta sources with s=1𝑠1s=1 as ℬ1,λ∗ηsubscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent𝜂1𝜆Basteta1,lambda, and the B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗BastbarBast sources with s=0,1,2𝑠012s=0,1,2 as ℬs,λ∗∗subscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent∗𝑠𝜆Bastasts,lambda. We will sometimes use the shorthand B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) to indicate all the various meson pairs, B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB, B​B¯∗η𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗absent𝜂BbarBasteta, and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗BastbarBast. Therefore, the relevant sets of static BO quantum numbers ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta are 0gsubscript0𝑔0g, +1gsubscript1𝑔+1g, −1gsubscript1𝑔-1g, and 0usubscript0𝑢0u. The corresponding values of the total spin quantum number s𝑠s for Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) sources are summarized in Table. There are also four sets of static BO quantum numbers ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta that can be produced by di-meson sources but not quark-antiquark sources: +2gsubscript2𝑔+2g, −2gsubscript2𝑔-2g, +1usubscript1𝑢+1u, and −1usubscript1𝑢-1u. content_sections.3 String breaking and transition rates: In lattice QCD, string breaking is manifested explicitly as a nonvanishing transition rate between states created by quark-antiquark and di-meson sources. Lattice QCD has been used to calculate the transition rate between Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ states created by Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB sources in a 0gsubscript0𝑔0g static BO configuration citebib.bib5. In this section, we express the transition rates between Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) sources for different ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta in terms of the string-breaking transition rate g𝑔g between a gluonic string with light BO quantum numbers Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ and a light quark-antiquark pair within the same configuration. Any di-meson operator can be factorized into products of light-quark operators and heavy-quark operators using the Fierz identity, q​q¯=−14​[(q¯​q)+(q¯​γμ​q)​γμ+12​(q¯​σμ​ν​q)​σμ​ν−(q¯​γμ​γ5​q)​γμ​γ5+(q¯​γ5​q)​γ5]𝑞¯𝑞14delimited-¯𝑞𝑞¯𝑞superscript𝛾𝜇𝑞subscript𝛾𝜇12¯𝑞superscript𝜎𝜇𝜈𝑞subscript𝜎𝜇𝜈¯𝑞superscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾5𝑞subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾5¯𝑞subscript𝛾5𝑞subscript𝛾5qbarq=-frac14biggl[(barqq)+(barqgammamuq)gammamu+frac12(barqsigmamunuq)sigmamunu-(barqgammamugamma5q)gammamugamma5+(barqgamma5q)gamma5biggr] where the overall minus sign comes from the anticommutation of fermionic operators. The constant can be set to zero by taking the zero of the energy to be the static meson pair threshold. This ansatz provides a good fit to the lattice QCD data in citebib.bib5; see, for instance, ref. citebib.bib7. It is worth mentioning that the results presented here are straightforwardly extended to the case of dynamical quark fields with Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓Nf flavors and S​U​(Nf)𝑆𝑈subscript𝑁𝑓SU(Nf) flavor symmetry. The only notable difference is that the string-breaking transition rate with Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓Nf light flavors is equal to the single-flavor one times a factor Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓sqrtNf. If, alternatively, both light and strange quarks are considered citebib.bib6, then one needs to substitute eq. with an appropriate 3×3333times 3 string-breaking interaction matrix, like 𝑮​(r)=(V𝒬​(r)g​(r)gs​(r)g​(r)00gs​(r)02​δs)𝑮𝑟matrixsubscript𝑉𝒬𝑟𝑔𝑟subscript𝑔𝑠𝑟𝑔𝑟00subscript𝑔𝑠𝑟02subscript𝛿𝑠G(r)=beginpmatrixVQ(r)g(r)gs(r) g(r)00 gs(r)02deltas endpmatrix where gs​(r)subscript𝑔𝑠𝑟gs(r) is the string-breaking transition rate by strange quarks and δs=mBs−mBsubscript𝛿𝑠subscript𝑚subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑚𝐵deltas=mBs-mB is the mass difference between static Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs and B𝐵B mesons. Notice that we have assumed that the B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) and Bs(∗)​B¯s(∗)superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠∗Bs(ast)barBs(ast) static energies are well-approximated by their constant values at large r𝑟r.
