(We take the coefficient to vanish for hQ+hℓ≠hHsubscriptℎ𝑄subscriptℎℓsubscriptℎ𝐻hQ+hellneq hH, i. e. , one sum is always eliminated in practice by helicity conservation. ) For the case of inclusive fragmentation, it has been known for a long timecitebib.bib38, bib.bib71 that the factorized form of eq. together with parity and eq. implies relations between the fragmentation probabilities to different hadron states within the same heavy-quark spin symmetry multiplet, i. e. , with the same sℓ=12,1,32,…subscript𝑠ℓ12132…sell=tfrac12,1,tfrac32,dots. As an example, at the strict leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m, an unpolarized charm quark is exactly three times as likely to fragment into an excited spin-111 vector meson (D∗superscript𝐷D*) than into the corresponding pseudoscalar state (D𝐷D). The physical reason for this is that the light dynamics do not see the heavy-quark spin at leading power, and thus the same nonperturbative matrix elements with given sℓ,hℓsubscript𝑠ℓsubscriptℎℓsell,hell appear in several cases. This analysis is simplified by the fact that for unpolarized or linearly polarized heavy quarks, light amplitudes for different helicities cannot interfere. One key goal of the next section will be to work out the consequences of heavy-quark spin symmetry for transverse momentum-dependent fragmentation functions, where transverse quark polarization will let us access this interference for the first time.

Note that finding a nonzero result for the Collins fragmentation function during this step crucially relies on the presence of the Wilson line, which distinguished a nontrivial reference direction zμsuperscript𝑧𝜇zmu in the rest frame. From our analysis so far, we can conclude that both leading-power TMD fragmentation functions for unpolarized hadrons are allowed by the discrete symmetries of QCD in the heavy-quark limit at leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m. Written as in eq. , they are also manifestly independent of the flavor and mass of the heavy quark. However, the physical interpretation of χ1,H​(bT)subscript𝜒1𝐻subscript𝑏𝑇chi1,H(bT) and χ1,H⟂​(bT)superscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑇chi1,Hperp(bT) is still fairly unclear at this point, and in fact we have not yet made use of heavy-quark spin symmetry. As expected from its relation to the unpolarized TMD FF, the Fourier transform of χ1,Hsubscript𝜒1𝐻chi1,H is simply the total conditional probability to produce H𝐻H at rest given an initial quark momentum kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT transverse to the direction of the static color source provided by the Wilson line. The bHQET analogue χ1,H⟂superscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tochi1,Hperp of the Collins function on the other hand can be interpreted as a conditional density of quark spin ΣQsubscriptΣ𝑄SigmaQ with respect to a magnetization axis defined by the static color source and the final-state transverse momentum. These physical interpretations roughly correspond to those of the associated relativistic fragmentation functions, which are often written in a form similar to eq. . Crucially, however, the meaning of the spin space on which the density matrix is defined is different in the heavy-quark case: For light quarks, whose spin after hadronization is an ill-defined concept, it only refers to the initial spin state in which the light quark is prepared. Heavy quarks at leading power in 1/m1𝑚1/m, by contrast, retain their initial spin state throughout the fragmentation process, and thus their spin density matrix together with the hadron spin measurement instead probes the angular momentum distribution of the final-state light constituents of the heavy hadron.8A related difference is that relativistic fragmentation functions have to be interpreted as number densities rather than probabilities due to the semi-inclusive measurement acting also on hadronic states at higher multiplicity. By contrast, the unpolarized bHQET TMD fragmentation factor upon Fourier transform, and up to renormalization effectscitebib.bib75, has an interpretation as a probability density because additional pair production of heavy quarks is power suppressed by kT≪mmuch-less-thansubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚kTll m. (2.102) We see that the total unpolarized heavy-quark TMD FF reduces to a vacuum matrix element of two staple-shaped Wilson-line configurations along the lightlike and timelike direction, respectively. (Recall that we have suppressed transverse gauge links at infinity; the Wilson lines in the interpolating fields for the out states cancel. ) This is reminiscent of the heavy quark-antiquark form factor proposed in ref. citebib.bib20 to extract the TMD soft function on the lattice, but in contrast to that proposal directly relates to a physical observable, i. e. , the total TMD cross section for producing heavy hadrons in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions. We contend that this makes eq. the theoretically simplest TMD observable possible in QCD, since it is entirely given in terms of vacuum matrix elements of Wilson loops.Collins TMD FF: A naive expectation from heavy-quark spin symmetry might be that the Collins FF should be suppressed by 1/m1𝑚1/m because it encodes a correlation between the initial quark transverse polarization vector and the transverse momentum of hadronic final-state radiation. In the case of light quarks, this correlation arises directly from the nonperturbative dynamics of the QCD Lagrangian, as illustrated in (a), but in the heavy-quark case it naively seems to require a suppressed magnetic interaction with the heavy-quark spin. We will now see that this is not the case. As illustrated in (b), the angle between the final-state heavy-quark and light transverse polarization vectors (i. e, the relative phase between their helicity states) determines which hadron in the spin symmetry multiplet is produced, even without a dynamical heavy-quark spin interaction taking place. Reconstructing this information experimentally thus induces a correlation between the heavy-quark and the light spin state. Crucially, spin symmetry ensures that the final-state heavy-quark spin state is identical to the one it was prepared in. The light spin state in turn is in general correlated with the transverse momentum k⟂subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokperp of hadronic final-state radiation, since they both arise from the same nonperturbative dynamics of the light degrees of freedom, leading to a Collins effect at the leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m. To illustrate this, it is again instructive to consider the case of the pseudoscalar meson, sℓ=1/2,sH=0:χ1,H⟂(bT)=14[ρℓ,−+(b⟂)−ρℓ,+−(b⟂)]displaystyle sell=1/2,,sH=0,:quadchi1,Hperp(bT)=frac14bigl[rhoell,-+(bperp)-rhoell,+-(bperp)bigr] (2.103) As expected, the Collins FF in the heavy-quark limit contains information about the strength of the interference, and hence the relative nonperturbative phases, of amplitudes for different light helicities. As a corollary, we conclude that the Collins FF must vanish at leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m when summing over all the hadrons in the spin symmetry multiplet, ∑H∈Mℓχ1,H⟂​(bT)=0. subscript𝐻subscript𝑀ℓsuperscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑇0displaystylesumHin Mellchi1,Hperp(bT)=0,. (2.104) This is immediate to see from the diagonal form of the summed quark spin density matrix or the full correlator in eq. . (2.105)Discussion: The spin symmetry relations in eqs. and are the main results of this section. They hold for all values of bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, which means that they also hold point by point in kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT upon Fourier transform. Furthermore, they are unaffected by renormalization, as we discuss in the next section. This makes them substantially stronger than the known sum rules for relativistic TMD fragmentation functions. For the light-quark Collins function in particular, the Schäfer-Teryaev sum rulecitebib.bib76 has only been rigorously provencitebib.bib77 in the bare case, and requires a sum over all possible hadrons, an integral over zhsubscript𝑧ℎzh, and a weighted integral over kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT. This is in contrast to the novel sum rule in eq. that we have derived for the heavy-quark limit, which only requires summing the Collins function over a subset of hadrons and holds at any value of kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT and zHsubscript𝑧𝐻zH. (We will explicitly extend these spin symmetry relations to kT∼msimilar-tosubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚kTsim m in . ) It thus implies the Schäfer-Teryaev sum rule for heavy quarks as a corollary. We caution that as in the inclusive case, the validity of the spin symmetry relations rests on the assumption that spin symmetry violation is negligible during the entire fragmentation process. (2.157) To evaluate the TMD evolution and the Bessel integrals, we use the numerical implementation of TMD anomalous dimensions, QCD renormalization-group solutions, and double-exponential oscillatory integration in SCETlibcitebib.bib92. Our results for the zHsubscript𝑧𝐻zH-integrated heavy-quark TMD FFs are shown as a function of kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT for different values of the model parameters in . We use αs​(mZ)=0.118​GeVsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑚𝑍0.118GeValphas(mZ)=0.118,GeV as the input value for the strong coupling. We note that due to heavy quark flavor symmetry, the charm and bottom-quark TMD FFs are exactly equal at small kT≪mmuch-less-thansubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚kTll m. In other words, they only depend on the universal Gaussian parameters κHsubscript𝜅𝐻kappaH (for the unpolarized TMD FF), κH⟂subscript𝜅perpendicular-to𝐻absentkappaHperp (for the Collins TMD FF), and the Collins effect strength λH⟂subscript𝜆perpendicular-to𝐻absentlambdaHperp. At large kT∼msimilar-tosubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚kTsim m, the TMD FFs remain independent of the heavy quark mass up to radiative corrections of 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠O(alphas), which we ignore at our LL working order. These plots are thus identical for both flavors we consider. We point out that the Collins function can in general take any sign, as indicated by the yellow band scanning various values of the effect strength λH⟂subscript𝜆perpendicular-to𝐻absentlambdaHperp. The effect of varying the size of higher-power corrections (κHsubscript𝜅𝐻kappaH, κH⟂subscript𝜅perpendicular-to𝐻absentkappaHperp) decreases as kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT increases for both TMD FFs, as expected.

content_sections.3 Polarized heavy-quark TMD PDFs 3.2 Mat: Heavy-quark TMD PDFs are different from their TMD FF counterparts because the heavy quark cannot be part of the initial-state nucleon wave function at the scale μ∼ΛQCDsimilar-to𝜇subscriptΛQCDmusimLambdaQCD at leading power in ΛQCD/msubscriptΛQCD𝑚LambdaQCD/m,14 Power corrections of this kind, which are known as “intrinsic charm” and have received substantial recent interest on the collinear PDF sidecitebib.bib95, bib.bib96, would be an interesting subject to explore in the TMD case in the future. Very recently, the TMD PDFs for charm quarks within ΛcsubscriptΛ𝑐Lambdac baryons, which are leading valence contributions and do not have to be produced from gluons, have been evaluated in a lightfront Hamiltonian model in ref. citebib.bib97; while these are phenomenologically inaccessible, it would be interesting to analyze these valence dynamics in the heavy-quark limit as we did for TMD FFs in . whereas in the fragmentation case the heavy quark is always part of the final-state heavy hadron until its eventual weak decay. This means that heavy quarks must be pair-produced in initial-state gluon splittings at the scale μ∼msimilar-to𝜇𝑚musim m instead. In particular, this means there is at least one perturbative emission with transverse momentum ≳mgreater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent𝑚gtrsim m setting the scale of kT≳mgreater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚kTgtrsim m, while the region of kT≪mmuch-less-thansubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚kTll m can only be populated by several emissions with small net recoil, which is a power-suppressed configuration. In field theory terms, this means that heavy-quark TMD PDFs can be computed by perturbatively matching them onto collinear twist-2 nucleon PDFs in a theory with nℓsubscript𝑛ℓnell light flavors, which are the only nonperturbative piece of information in this case. The matching onto twist-2 collinear PDFs is well developed for light quark and gluon TMDs, with notable results including all unpolarized quark matching coefficients through 𝒪​(αs3)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠3O(alphas3)citebib.bib98, bib.bib99 and results for polarized TMDs through 𝒪​(αs2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2O(alphas2)citebib.bib100, bib.bib101, and many of the following steps are standard, see e. g. citebib.bib3. Likewise, the 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠O(alphas) matching of the unpolarized heavy-quark TMD PDF onto gluon collinear PDFs has been given in refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib47. We nevertheless aim for a self-contained description, giving us the opportunity to point out the ways in which (polarized) heavy-quark TMD PDFs behave differently. The bare light-quark and gluon twist-2 collinear correlators are defined as Φq/Nα​α′​(x)superscriptsubscriptΦ𝑞𝑁𝛼superscript𝛼′𝑥displaystylePhiq/Nalphaalphaprime(x) =∫d​b+4​π​e−i​b+​(x​PN−)/2​⟨N​|ψ¯qα′​(b)​W​(b,0)​ψqα​|​N⟩,absentdsuperscript𝑏4𝜋superscript𝑒isuperscript𝑏𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑁2delimited-⟨⟩𝑁subscriptsuperscript¯𝜓superscript𝛼′𝑞𝑏𝑊𝑏0subscriptsuperscript𝜓𝛼𝑞0𝑁displaystyle=intfracdb+4pie-ib+(xPN-)/2bigNbiglvertbarpsialphaprimeq(b)W(b,0)psialphaqbigrvertNbig,, Φg/Nμ​ν​(x)superscriptsubscriptΦ𝑔𝑁𝜇𝜈𝑥displaystylePhig/Nmunu(x) =∫d​b+4​π​e−i​b+​(x​PN−)/2​⟨N​|F−μ​(b)​W​(b,0)​F−ν​|​N⟩,absentdsuperscript𝑏4𝜋superscript𝑒isuperscript𝑏𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑁2delimited-⟨⟩𝑁superscript𝐹𝜇𝑏𝑊𝑏0superscript𝐹𝜈0𝑁displaystyle=intfracdb+4pie-ib+(xPN-)/2bigNbiglvertF-mu(b)W(b,0)F-nubigrvertNbig,, (3.6) where b≡(0,b+,0)𝑏0superscript𝑏0bequiv(0,b+,0) in this case and W​(b,0)𝑊𝑏0W(b,0) denotes a straight Wilson line segment. The collinear correlators are conventionally decomposed as citebib.bib3 Φq/N​(x>0)subscriptΦ𝑞𝑁𝑥0displaystylePhiq/N(x>0) =fq/N​(x)+gq/N​(x)​SL​γ5+hq/N​(x)​γ5​S̸⟂​n̸4,absentsubscript𝑓𝑞𝑁𝑥subscript𝑔𝑞𝑁𝑥subscript𝑆𝐿subscript𝛾5subscriptℎ𝑞𝑁𝑥subscript𝛾5subscriptitalic-S̸perpendicular-toitalic-n̸4displaystyle=Biglfq/N(x)+gq/N(x),SLgamma5+hq/N(x),gamma5notSperpBigrfracnotn4,, Φg/Nμ​ν​(x>0)superscriptsubscriptΦ𝑔𝑁𝜇𝜈𝑥0displaystylePhig/Nmunu(x>0) =−g⟂μ​ν2​fg/N​(x)+i​ϵ⟂μ​ν2​gg/N​(x)​SL. absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑔perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈2subscript𝑓𝑔𝑁𝑥isuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵperpendicular-to𝜇𝜈2subscript𝑔𝑔𝑁𝑥subscript𝑆𝐿displaystyle=-fracgperpmunu2,fg/N(x)+fraciepsilonperpmunu2,gg/N(x),SL,. (3.7) in terms of the unpolarized (helicity) quark and gluon PDFs fi/Nsubscript𝑓𝑖𝑁fi/N (gi/Nsubscript𝑔𝑖𝑁gi/N) and the transversity quark PDF hq/Nsubscriptℎ𝑞𝑁hq/N. The contribution ∝S⟂proportional-toabsentsubscript𝑆perpendicular-topropto Sperp to the gluon correlator (i. e. , the transversity gluon PDF) vanishes identically for spin-00 and spin-1/2121/2 hadrons in the initial state due to helicity conservationcitebib.bib102. The matching relation between heavy-quark TMD PDFs and twist-2 collinear PDFs holds at the operator level, and constitutes the leading term in the OPE of the former. Taking nucleon matrix elements of the bare operators, the relation for general spin indices reads ΦQ/Nβ​β′​(x,k⟂)superscriptsubscriptΦ𝑄𝑁𝛽superscript𝛽′𝑥subscript𝑘perpendicular-todisplaystylePhiQ/Nbetabetaprime(x,kperp) =∫d​p−p−​CQ/g,μ​νβ​β′​(x​PN−,p−,k⟂,m)​Φg/Nμ​ν​(p−PN−)absentdsuperscript𝑝superscript𝑝superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑄𝑔𝜇𝜈𝛽superscript𝛽′𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑁superscript𝑝subscript𝑘perpendicular-to𝑚superscriptsubscriptΦ𝑔𝑁𝜇𝜈superscript𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑁displaystyle=int!fracdp-p-,CQ/g,munubetabetaprime(xPN-,p-,kperp,m),Phig/NmunuBigl(fracp-PN-Bigr) +∑q∫d​p−p−​CQ/q,α​α′β​β′​(x​PN−,p−,k⟂,m)​Φq/Nα​α′​(p−PN−),subscript𝑞dsuperscript𝑝superscript𝑝superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑄𝑞𝛼superscript𝛼′𝛽superscript𝛽′𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑁superscript𝑝subscript𝑘perpendicular-to𝑚superscriptsubscriptΦ𝑞𝑁𝛼superscript𝛼′superscript𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑁displaystylequad+sumqint!fracdp-p-,CQ/q,alphaalphaprimebetabetaprime(xPN-,p-,kperp,m),Phiq/NalphaalphaprimeBigl(fracp-PN-Bigr),, (3.8) where p−superscript𝑝p- is the lightcone momentum carried by the light parton extracted from the collinear PDF and the sum runs over the nℓsubscript𝑛ℓnell light quark flavors. In pure dimensional regularization, the bare matching coefficients are given by the partonic diagrams CQ/g,μ​νβ​β′​(z​p−,p−,k⟂,m)=,superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑄𝑔𝜇𝜈𝛽superscript𝛽′𝑧superscript𝑝superscript𝑝subscript𝑘perpendicular-to𝑚displaystyle CQ/g,munubetabetaprime(zp-,p-,kperp,m)=raisebox-0.45pt includegraphics[width=110.57327pt]figs/tmdpdfgluonblob. pdf ,, CQ/q,α​α′β​β′​(z​p−,p−,k⟂,m)=,superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑄𝑞𝛼superscript𝛼′𝛽superscript𝛽′𝑧superscript𝑝superscript𝑝subscript𝑘perpendicular-to𝑚displaystyle CQ/q,alphaalphaprimebetabetaprime(zp-,p-,kperp,m)=raisebox-0.45pt includegraphics[width=110.57327pt]figs/tmdpdflightquarkblob. pdf ,, (3.9) where z=x​PN−/p−𝑧𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑁superscript𝑝z=xPN-/p- is the fraction of p−superscript𝑝p- injected into the hard scattering process and we have indicated the heavy quark lines in red. The gray-shaded circles denote the sum of all possible QCD diagrams with these external legs, including gluon attachments to the Wilson lines that are part of the operators indicated by ⊗tensor-productcolor[rgb]1,0,0otimes. We have included the respective lowest-order diagram for illustration. As is standard, matching relations between individual scalar TMD and collinear PDFs follow by inserting eq. into eq. and tracing the resulting Dirac bispinors (…)β​β′superscript…𝛽superscript𝛽′(dots)betabetaprime against the relevant Dirac structures. Flavor conservation in QCD implies that a single fermion line has to connect the external light-quark states in eq. . It follows that contractions with the quark transversity PDF involve an odd number of Dirac matrices on the light-quark line and vanish to all orders, i. e. , flavor conservation and chirality for light quark flavors imply that all terms ∝S⟂proportional-toabsentsubscript𝑆perpendicular-topropto Sperp vanish at twist-2 level in eq. . This is distinct from e. g. the light-quark transversity TMD PDF, which receives a tree-level contribution from the transversity collinear PDF of the same flavor. As in the case of light-quark TMD PDFs, Lorentz covariance further implies that only unpolarized (helicity) collinear PDFs can contribute to the unpolarized and Boer-Mulders (helicity and worm-gearL𝐿L) TMD PDFs, matching the dependence on SLsubscript𝑆𝐿SL in the spin decomposition. These conclusions are not modified by the inclusion of the soft factor, the rapidity renormalization, and the UV renormalization of the TMD PDFs, all of which are orthogonal to the spin structure. They are likewise unaffected by the renormalization of the collinear PDFs, which acts autonomously on the unpolarized and longitudinally polarized sectors. (3.17) As a nontrivial check, we have confirmed that using massive SCET Feynman rulescitebib.bib104 results in the same expressions after performing the spin traces and integrating over the loop momentum. Note that the projection of the 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠O(alphas) twist-2 matching diagram onto the Boer-Mulders function remains zero even for finite quark masses. This is expected because the Boer-Mulders function is odd under time reversalcitebib.bib105, i. e. , it changes sign depending on whether the Wilson lines in the operator point to the future (SIDIS) or the past (Drell-Yan). The diagram in eq. does not yet feature gluon attachments to the Wilson lines that could resolve their direction, and thus its projection onto the Boer-Mulders function has to vanish. content_sections.3 Polarized heavy-quark TMD PDFs 3.4 Con: For ΛQCD≪m≪kTmuch-less-thansubscriptΛQCD𝑚much-less-thansubscript𝑘𝑇LambdaQCDll mll kT, heavy-quark TMD PDFs can be determined using a two-step matchingcitebib.bib47. First, the TMD operators at the scale μ∼kTsimilar-to𝜇subscript𝑘𝑇musim kT are matched onto collinear PDFs in a theory with nℓ+1subscript𝑛ℓ1nell+1 massless quark flavors, which results in the standard massless TMD matching coefficients. In a second step, the nℓ+1subscript𝑛ℓ1nell+1-flavor PDFs are matched onto those in a theory with nℓsubscript𝑛ℓnell flavors at the scale μ∼msimilar-to𝜇𝑚musim m. Subleading-power mass logarithms in amplitudes that require a helicity flip have received much attention in the context of Higgs boson production through a bottom-quark loop, see e. g. refs. citebib.bib107, bib.bib108, bib.bib109, bib.bib110, bib.bib111, and it would be interesting to understand whether the logarithm in eq. might be amenable to similar techniques.

E. g. , we have f1​(x,bT,μ,ζ)subscript𝑓1𝑥subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇𝜁displaystyle f1(x,bT,mu,zeta) =Uq​(μ0,ζ0,μ,ζ)​αs​(μ0)4​π​∫d​zz​CQ/g​(z,bT​m)​fg​(xz,μ0)absentsubscript𝑈𝑞subscript𝜇0subscript𝜁0𝜇𝜁subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝜇04𝜋d𝑧𝑧subscriptsuperscript𝐶1𝑄𝑔𝑧subscript𝑏𝑇𝑚subscript𝑓𝑔𝑥𝑧subscript𝜇0displaystyle=Uq(mu0,zeta0,mu,zeta),fracalphas(mu0)4piint!fracdzz,CQ/g(z,bTm),fgBigl(fracxz,mu0Bigr) (3.26) for the evolved unpolarized heavy-quark TMD PDF, and similarly for the other cases. For the input collinear gluon PDFs we use the NNPDF31nnloas0118 unpolarized proton PDF setcitebib.bib112 together with the NNPDFpol11100 set for the polarized casecitebib.bib113. Our input values for the strong coupling and the quark pole masses were given in . In , we show our numerical results for the heavy quark TMD PDFs for producing a charm or bottom quark from a longitudinally polarized proton as a function of kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT and x𝑥x, respectively. The bottom quark TMD PDFs have a wider peak in kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT compared to the charm because of its larger mass, as can be understood from the fact that the expressions in eq. only depend on m​bT𝑚subscript𝑏𝑇mbT up to RG effects. Note also that the worm-gear L𝐿L function (after including a Jacobian 2​π​kT2𝜋subscript𝑘𝑇2pi kT) is quadratic in the small kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT region with a coefficient proportional to 1/m31superscript𝑚31/m3, whereas the unpolarized and helicity TMD PDFs are linear in kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT. As this approximation is valid to higher kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT in the case of the bottom quark than that of the charm, the bottom-quark TMD PDF has a numerically smaller value over a wide range. As x𝑥x decreases, the unpolarized heavy-quark TMD PDF rises much more rapidly than the polarized ones, as expected from the smaller gluon polarization fraction at smaller x𝑥x. We point out that the unpolarized TMD PDF changes sign at very high x≥0.6𝑥0.6xgeq 0.6, indicating a need for resumming subleading-power threshold logarithms of 1−x1𝑥1-x using e. g. the tools of refs. citebib.bib114.

We may assume that the experimental measurement involves an integral over symmetric ranges in cos⁡θ𝜃costheta such that the forward-backward asymmetry and an associated odd Collins effect in eq. drop out. Crucially, the TMD factorization theorems in eqs. and only assume that the hard scale Q∼za​Q∼zb​Qsimilar-to𝑄subscript𝑧𝑎𝑄similar-tosubscript𝑧𝑏𝑄Qsim zaQsim zbQ is large compared to all other scales, i. e. , all masses and transverse momenta, and therefore hold for both light-quark and heavy-quark fragmentation at za,b∼1similar-tosubscript𝑧𝑎𝑏1za,bsim 1 without modification. In particular, the heavy quarks are approximately massless at the scale μ∼Qsimilar-to𝜇𝑄musim Q at which they are produced, and their polarization states are thus fully entangled. The hard function in eq. could be modified to account for the effect of perturbative spin flips, but this amounts to retaining power corrections in m/Q𝑚𝑄m/Q further suppressed by powers of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. Importantly, this means that a characteristic cos⁡(2​ϕ0)2subscriptitalic-ϕ0cos(2phi0) modulation (the Collins effect) is present both for light and for heavy quarks at leading power and at tree level. In we show the predicted e+​e−→D​D¯​X→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝐷¯𝐷𝑋e+e-to DbarDX or B​B¯​X𝐵¯𝐵𝑋BbarBX cross sections as a function of hadron transverse momentum Pa,Tsubscript𝑃𝑎𝑇Pa,T, and the Collins effect strength Rcos⁡(2​ϕ0)subscript𝑅2subscriptitalic-ϕ0Rcos(2phi0) as a function of qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT. The universality for charm and bottom quarks follows along the same lines as for , and holds as long as the center-of-mass energy is sufficient to produce the quark-antiquark pair in a boosted state. This is the case for charm mesons at typical continuum center-of-mass energies at existing B𝐵B factories, and for both charm and bottom mesons at higher values of Q𝑄Q such as at the Z𝑍Z pole. The Collins effect is smaller at higher center-of-mass energies because χ1,H⟂superscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tochi1,Hperp is linearly suppressed in bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT compared to the unpolarized, which means it predominantly contributes at larger values of bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT where the Sudakov suppression at higher energies tends to be stronger. We show the results of varying κHsubscript𝜅𝐻kappaH (κH⟂superscriptsubscript𝜅𝐻perpendicular-tokappaHperp) for the unpolarized (Collins) TMD FF, and illustrate the variation of λH⟂subscript𝜆perpendicular-to𝐻absentlambdaHperp by the yellow band, exactly as in . Note that the information about the absolute sign of the Collins function is lost in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions, i. e. , for two charge-conjugate hadrons we end up with a positive effect strength for any value of λH⟂=λH¯⟂subscript𝜆perpendicular-to𝐻absentsubscript𝜆perpendicular-to¯𝐻absentlambdaHperp=lambdabarHperp since the effect is proportional to the square of the Collins function. One may nevertheless extract the relative factor between e. g. the D𝐷D and D∗superscript𝐷D* Collins function, which heavy-quark spin symmetry predicts to be exactly minus one, see eq. , by measuring the Collins effect separately for e+​e−→D​D¯​X→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝐷¯𝐷𝑋e+e-to DbarDX and e+​e−→D∗​D¯​X→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscript𝐷¯𝐷𝑋e+e-to D*barDX. Explicitly, our prediction from heavy-quark spin symmetry reads Rcos⁡(2​ϕ0)D​D¯=−13​Rcos⁡(2​ϕ0)D∗​D¯=−13​Rcos⁡(2​ϕ0)D​D¯∗=+19​Rcos⁡(2​ϕ0)D∗​D¯∗superscriptsubscript𝑅2subscriptitalic-ϕ0𝐷¯𝐷13superscriptsubscript𝑅2subscriptitalic-ϕ0superscript𝐷¯𝐷13superscriptsubscript𝑅2subscriptitalic-ϕ0𝐷superscript¯𝐷19superscriptsubscript𝑅2subscriptitalic-ϕ0superscript𝐷superscript¯𝐷displaystyle Rcos(2phi0)DbarD=-frac13Rcos(2phi0)D*barD=-frac13Rcos(2phi0)DbarD*=+frac19Rcos(2phi0)D*barD* (4.6) We point out that for generic 𝒪​(ΛQCD)𝒪subscriptΛQCDO(LambdaQCD) model parameters, the Collins effect strength reaches the several-percent level for continuum open charm production at existing B𝐵B factories, in line with our expectation of an effect strength that is comparable to the light quark case, making a future dedicated measurement (or search) appear very feasible.In e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions, the “intrinsic” heavy-quark Collins effect we analyzed above has been disregarded so far. Note that this effect is in general distinct from the large background contribution of D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD weak decays to e. g. a measurement of the Collins effect on a K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK sample. This contribution is indeed considered in experimental analyses citebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib41, bib.bib42 and subtracted as a background using Monte-Carlo simulations and heavy-quark enriched samples, but cannot be immediately interpreted as a sign of a (nonperturbative) Collins effect since the progenitor D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD pair in this case is not constrained to be near the back-to-back limit by the measurement, meaning that e. g. perturbative gluon emissions can also induce azimuthal correlations on the D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD pair and thus their weak decay products. Ref. citebib.bib44 mentions that it would be possible to look for the intrinsic heavy-quark Collins effect with some further improvements to their analysis, but also incorrectly expects that the Collins effect should be parametrically suppressed by the mass of heavy quarks. The argument sketched in that reference (see beginning of their sectionIV) is that helicity flips should wash out the spin correlation between the heavy quark and the antiquark. This is not the case, as we have argued above: The quarks are approximately massless at the scale μ∼Qsimilar-to𝜇𝑄musim Q at which they are produced, and thus are produced with fully entangled spin states, such that there is no suppression by the mass from physics at this scale. Similarly, in our detailed analysis of the Collins FF at the scale μ≤kT≤m𝜇subscript𝑘𝑇𝑚muleq kTleq m, we find no suppression of the effect by the mass, and the Collins effect in particular is fully allowed by heavy-quark symmetry when accounting for the presence of lightlike Wilson lines. Note that this is not contradictory to the fact that we do find a suppression of the Collins effect by ΛQCD/kTsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑘𝑇LambdaQCD/kT at large kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT, since this suppression is exactly commensurate with the twist suppression of the two Collins functions in the light-quark case, which has been mapped out extensivelycitebib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib43. We conclude that the prospects for a measurement of the intrinsic, nonperturbative heavy-quark Collins effect at B𝐵B factories are even better than anticipated in ref. citebib.bib44.

(4.14) We consider the EIC at beam energies Ee=18​GeVsubscript𝐸𝑒18GeVEe=18,GeV and EN=275​GeVsubscript𝐸𝑁275GeVEN=275,GeV. Any experimental cuts on zH>zcutsubscript𝑧𝐻subscript𝑧cutzH>zcut and the additional prefactor of zH3superscriptsubscript𝑧𝐻3zH3 in eq. are irrelevant at our working order because the heavy quark carries all the energy in all regimes, i. e. , zH=1subscript𝑧𝐻1zH=1 either at leading power in the heavy-quark expansion or at the leading perturbative order, see the comments below eq. . For this estimate we set κH=0subscript𝜅𝐻0kappaH=0 in the unpolarized heavy-quark TMD FF, since the total integral of the cross section up to qTcut≫ΛQCDmuch-greater-thansuperscriptsubscript𝑞𝑇cutsubscriptΛQCDqTcutggLambdaQCD is independent of it up to corrections of 𝒪​(ΛQCD2/qTcut)𝒪superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑇cutO(LambdaQCD2/qTcut)citebib.bib75, and sum over all heavy hadrons containing the heavy quark, exploiting ∑HχH=1subscript𝐻subscript𝜒𝐻1sumHchiH=1. This means that the total rate at which heavy quarks are produced is predicted fully perturbatively, as expected. Our results for the expected total charm and bottom-quark TMD cross sections are given in for Qcut=4​GeVsubscript𝑄cut4GeVQcut=4,GeV and Qcut=10​GeVsubscript𝑄cut10GeVQcut=10,GeV, where higher Qcutsubscript𝑄cutQcut allows for mapping out the TMD region to higher qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT before encountering power corrections, but at the cost of much lower rates. (We have also adjusted qTcutsuperscriptsubscript𝑞𝑇cutqTcut accordingly in each case. ) Scaled to an integrated luminosity of 10​fb−110superscriptfb110,fb-1, we expect a total charm quark sample of 35×10335superscript10335times 103 in the TMD region for the loose cut on Q𝑄Q and in the region x>0.1𝑥0.1x>0.1 where polarization effects are expected to be most pronounced, see , and where a measurement of the sin⁡(2​ϕH)2subscriptitalic-ϕ𝐻sin(2phiH) asymmetry is the most promising. This suggests that even with this limited integrated luminosity, percent-level asymmetries should be statistically resolvable. In we show the results for the unpolarized SIDIS cross section with a D𝐷D (B𝐵B) meson in the final state, and for the two spin asymmetries defined in eq. . Note that the effect of different κHsubscript𝜅𝐻kappaH in the unpolarized TMD fragmentation function is negligible in the cross section and the AL​Lsubscript𝐴𝐿𝐿ALL asymmetry, which as expected are dominated by perturbative physics. The AL​Lsubscript𝐴𝐿𝐿ALL asymmetry is very sizable at ∼30%similar-toabsentpercent30sim 30% at the chosen value of x=0.2𝑥0.2x=0.2. On the other hand, the AU​Lsubscript𝐴𝑈𝐿AUL asymmetry is substantially smaller (1−2%1percent21-2%) for the generic 𝒪​(ΛQCD)𝒪subscriptΛQCDO(LambdaQCD) parameters we picked here due to the smaller value of both h1​L⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝐿perpendicular-toh1Lperp compared to g1​Lsubscript𝑔1𝐿g1L and H1⟂superscriptsubscript𝐻1perpendicular-toH1perp compared to D1subscript𝐷1D1 in most of the contributing TMD region, see figures and and the surrounding discussion. The numerically smaller value of h1​L⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝐿perpendicular-toh1Lperp for bottom quarks discussed around is likewise reflected in the size of the asymmetry for bottom compared to charm quarks. We emphasize that a measurement of AU​Lsubscript𝐴𝑈𝐿AUL, compared to the Collins effect in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions, has the unique benefit of accessing the absolute sign of the heavy-quark Collins function. Resolving this sign should well be possible within the expected statistics at the future EIC. While we leave the study of systematic effects (such as luminosity uncertainties) to future work, we note that the requirements that the established heavy-flavor/gluon distribution program of the EIC places on instrumentation have already been analyzed in depth in ref. citebib.bib27. Among these requirements are secondary vertex reconstruction capabilities and the momentum resolution on soft pions from D𝐷D decays, all of which will also benefit the kind of differential measurements of semi-inclusive heavy-quark fragmentation that we propose here.
content_sections.5 Summary of main results and outlook: In this paper, we have studied the transverse momentum-dependent (TMD) dynamics of bottom or charm quarks with mass m≡mc,mb≫ΛQCDformulae-sequence𝑚subscript𝑚𝑐much-greater-thansubscript𝑚𝑏subscriptΛQCDmequiv mc,mbggLambdaQCD fragmenting into heavy hadrons for the first time. We considered two parametric regimes for the transverse momentum kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT, (a)ΛQCD≲kT≪mless-than-or-similar-tosubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑘𝑇much-less-than𝑚LambdaQCDlesssim kTll m, where the hadron transverse momentum kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT is determined by nonperturbative soft radiation into the final state, and (b)ΛQCD≪m≲kTmuch-less-thansubscriptΛQCD𝑚less-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑘𝑇LambdaQCDll mlesssim kT, where kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT is set by perturbative emissions. We assumed throughout that the heavy quark is produced at a hard scale Q≫m,kTmuch-greater-than𝑄𝑚subscript𝑘𝑇Qgg m,kT, i. e. , it is boosted in the frame of the hard scattering, such that standard TMD factorization applies at the scale Q𝑄Q and only the low-energy TMD matrix elements are modified by the heavy quark dynamics. In both regimes, the dynamics at scales below the heavy quark mass are constrained by heavy-quark symmetry and encoded in novel low-energy matrix elements in boosted Heavy-Quark Effective Theory (bHQET): • We showed that in regime (a), the unpolarized and Collins TMD fragmentation functions (FF) match onto new, universal nonperturbative bHQET matrix elements χ1,H​(kT)subscript𝜒1𝐻subscript𝑘𝑇chi1,H(kT) and χ1,H⟂​(kT)superscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tosubscript𝑘𝑇chi1,Hperp(kT), which we dubbed TMD fragmentation factors. • In regime (b), we made use of the twist expansion for light-quark TMD FFs and combined it with the matching collinear FFs onto bHQET to identify the relevant leading or subleading bHQET matrix elements. • An important new ingredient in this analysis is the unpolarized partonic heavy quark TMD FF d1​Q/Qsubscript𝑑1𝑄𝑄d1,Q/Q, a perturbative Wilson coefficient that appears in our analysis for the first time and that we expect to appear also in other contexts like flavor-tagged energy-energy correlators in the back-to-back limit. • We find that the Collins TMD FF scales as ΛQCD/kTsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑘𝑇LambdaQCD/kT at kT≫ΛQCDmuch-greater-thansubscript𝑘𝑇subscriptΛQCDkTggLambdaQCD — but is not suppressed by the quark mass — and identified the coefficient as a new subleading bHQET matrix element probing gluon correlations within the fragmentation process. • We used heavy-quark spin symmetry to express the TMD fragmentation factors in terms of the underlying spin density matrix of the light hadron constituents. • For the unpolarized TMD FF D1​H/Q​(zH,kT,μ,ζ)subscript𝐷1𝐻𝑄subscript𝑧𝐻subscript𝑘𝑇𝜇𝜁D1,H/Q(zH,kT,mu,zeta), this allowed us to prove the following relations between renormalized TMD FFs within spin symmetry multiplets: D1​H/Q=13​D1​H∗/Q,D1​ΣQ/Q=12​D1​ΣQ∗/Q,D1​H1/Q=35​D1​H2∗/Qformulae-sequencesubscript𝐷1𝐻𝑄13subscript𝐷1superscript𝐻𝑄formulae-sequencesubscript𝐷1subscriptΣ𝑄𝑄12subscript𝐷1superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑄𝑄subscript𝐷1subscript𝐻1𝑄35subscript𝐷1superscriptsubscript𝐻2𝑄displaystyle D1,H/Q=frac13D1,H*/Q,,qquad D1,SigmaQ/Q=frac12D1,SigmaQ*/Q,,qquad D1,H1/Q=frac35D1,H2*/Q (5.1) These relations are a powerful generalization of known results for inclusive heavy-quark fragmentation, demonstrating for the first time that they hold point by point in transverse momentum. • We showed that the Collins function arises from correlations between hadronic radiation into the final state and the transverse polarization of the light constituents, which in turn is correlated with the heavy-quark spin by the experimental reconstruction of e. g. D𝐷D vs. D∗superscript𝐷D* mesons. This new picture of spin correlations in the heavy-quark limit allowed us to prove the following novel sum rule for the renormalized heavy-quark Collins function, which holds up to corrections of 𝒪​(ΛQCD/m)𝒪subscriptΛQCD𝑚O(LambdaQCD/m) and up to radiative corrections in αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas at the scale μ∼msimilar-to𝜇𝑚musim m, and likely beyond, ∑H∈MℓH1​H/Q⟂​(zH,kT,μ,ζ)=0,subscript𝐻subscript𝑀ℓsuperscriptsubscript𝐻1𝐻𝑄perpendicular-tosubscript𝑧𝐻subscript𝑘𝑇𝜇𝜁0displaystylesumHin MellH1,H/Qperp(zH,kT,mu,zeta)=0,, (5.2) where Mℓ=ΛQ,H,H∗,ΣQ,ΣQ∗,H1,H2∗,…subscript𝑀ℓsubscriptΛ𝑄𝐻superscript𝐻subscriptΣ𝑄superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑄subscript𝐻1superscriptsubscript𝐻2…Mell=LambdaQ,H,H*,SigmaQ,SigmaQ*,H1,H2*,dots is a heavy-quark spin symmetry multiplet specified by the total angular momentum and flavor content of the light hadron constituents. To extend our analysis to the possible phenomenology at the future Electron-Ion Collider (EIC), we also considered the production of polarized heavy quarks from a polarized nucleon, which is encoded in all-order matching relations between heavy-quark TMD PDFs and twist-2 collinear light-parton PDFs: • We find that terms proportional to the transverse nucleon polarization vanish for heavy quarks at twist-2 to all orders in the strong coupling due to chirality and flavor conservation in the light-quark sector. • In contrast to the light-quark case, transverse quark polarization states are populated from unpolarized and linearly polarized nucleons because the quark mass breaks chirality. • We find nontrivial matching coefficients at 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠O(alphas) for the heavy-quark worm-gear L𝐿L and helicity TMD PDFs onto the gluon helicity collinear PDF, both of which we computed explicitly for the first time. We anticipate that the heavy-quark Boer-Mulders function will receive a contribution from the twist-2 collinear gluon PDF starting at 𝒪​(αs2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2O(alphas2), where it becomes allowed by time-reversal invariance. Combining the standard TMD factorization theorems for e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- to hadrons and SIDIS with simple numerical models for the new nonperturbative functions we identified, we provided predictions for unpolarized heavy-quark TMD cross sections, the Collins effect strength for heavy quarks at e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- colliders (and in particular for c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc continuum production at current B𝐵B factories), as well as for the relevant spin asymmetries at the future EIC: • We find that a measurement of the intrinsic heavy-quark Collins effect is well within reach of existing B𝐵B factories, and is motivated by the rich nonperturbative structure of the heavy-quark Collins function that our analysis revealed. • The fact that transversely polarized heavy quarks are produced from linearly polarized nucleons at a significant rate, as encoded in the worm-gear L𝐿L matching coefficient, in addition provides a clean avenue for probing the heavy-quark Collins functions in heavy-quark SIDIS at the future EIC, including its absolute sign. The theoretical framework we developed in this paper paves the way for many promising future applications: • While we only considered the case of unpolarized heavy hadrons in this work, an immediate next application of our framework are polarized vector mesons or baryons containing heavy quarks. This gives access to a larger set of transverse-momentum dependent polarized fragmentation functionscitebib.bib115, bib.bib119, bib.bib120 which in the heavy-quark case resolve the light spin density matrix in even greater detail and obey additional sum rules. • Another promising prospect is to consider heavy-quark TMD fragmentation within jets, which makes its rich physics accessible in hadron collisions. This extension is in fact straightforward because our results for the heavy-quark TMD FFs hold independent of the factorization theorem they appear in. This makes it possible to insert them into the hadron-in-jet frameworks of refs. citebib.bib121, bib.bib122 in a plug-and-play fashion as long as Q∼pTjet​R≫m,kTformulae-sequencesimilar-to𝑄superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑇jet𝑅much-greater-than𝑚subscript𝑘𝑇Qsim pTjetRgg m,kT. Yet another possibility, which could mitigate the effect of nonglobal logarithms that can become nonperturbative in our regime of interest, would be to apply grooming to the jet and study the hadron transverse momentum spectrum with respect to the groomed jet axiscitebib.bib123, bib.bib58, see also . We look forward to the attendant phenomenology, which may in addition serve as a vacuum baseline for TMD interactions of open charm and bottom quarks with the quark-gluon plasma in heavy-ion collisions. • Other natural extensions are higher-order calculations of the various new partonic matching coefficients we introduced in this paper, which will reduce the perturbative uncertainties on the lowest-order theory predictions we provided here. This will also involve analyzing the renormalon structure and optimizing the choice of quark mass scheme. In addition, one could consider the matching onto subleading bHQET fragmentation matrix elements (for TMD FFs) or onto twist-3 collinear PDFs (for TMD PDFs, extending the work of ref. citebib.bib124 to the massive case), which would make it possible to interpret phenomenological extractions in terms of higher-point correlation functions. Higher-order resummed predictions for heavy-quark TMD spectra then immediately follow from our factorization results by solving the attendant renormalization group equations, and will serve as powerful, highly differential benchmarks of the heavy-quark physics encoded in present and future parton showers, including their interface with hadronization models, on which our field-theory analysis of the nonperturbative dynamics places rigorous constraints. In conclusion, our analysis reveals that a wealth of information on the all-order and nonperturbative structure of QCD resides in the transverse momentum dependence of heavy-quark fragmentation. An experimental exploration of this new subfield of TMD physics is in immediate reach of existing B𝐵B factories and will be an exciting addition to the planned heavy-flavor physics program of the future EIC.

sorted_papers.title:
The $\Lambda_c\to \Lambda\, l^+\nu_\ell$ weak decay including new
  physics
sorted_papers.abstract:
We investigate the $\Lambda_c \to \Lambda \ell^{+} \nu_\ell$ decay with a
focus on potential new physics (NP) effects in the $\ell = \mu$ channel. We
employ an effective Hamiltonian within the framework of the Standard Model
Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) to consider generalized dimension-6 semileptonic
$c\to s$ operators of scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector and tensor
types. We rely on Lattice QCD (LQCD) for the hadronic transition form factors,
using heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) to determine those that have not yet
been obtained on the lattice. Uncertainties due to the truncation of the NP
Hamiltonian and different implementations of HQSS are taken into account. As a
result, we unravel the NP discovery potential of the $\Lambda_c\to \Lambda$
semileptonic decay in different observables. Our findings indicate high
sensitivity to NP in lepton flavour universality ratios, probing multi-TeV
scales in some cases. On the theoretical side, we identify LQCD uncertainties
in axial and vector form factors as critical for improving NP sensitivity,
alongside better SMEFT uncertainty estimations.

sections.3: Dutta, Λb→(Λc,p)​τ​ν→subscriptΛ𝑏subscriptΛ𝑐𝑝𝜏𝜈\Lambda_{b}\to(\Lambda_{c},\,p)\,\tau\,\nu decays within standard model and beyond, Phys
sections.23: Meinel, Λc→Λ​l+​νl→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝑙subscript𝜈𝑙\Lambda_{c}\to\Lambda l^{+}\nu_{l} form factors and decay rates from lattice QCD with physical quark masses, Phys
sections.30: Meinel, Λc→Λ​l+​νl→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝑙subscript𝜈𝑙\Lambda_{c}\to\Lambda l^{+}\nu_{l} form factors and decay rates from lattice QCD with physical quark masses, Phys

sections.39: Meinel, Λc→Λ​l+​νl→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝑙subscript𝜈𝑙\Lambda_{c}\to\Lambda l^{+}\nu_{l} form factors and decay rates from lattice QCD with physical quark masses, Phys

qualitative: Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) is a theoretical framework used to relate the form factors of weak decays involving heavy quarks, such as the Λc→Λ decay, by exploiting the approximate symmetry when the heavy quark mass is much larger than the QCD scale.
HQSS allows the determination of unknown hadronic transition form factors in the Λc→Λ semileptonic decay by expressing them in terms of form factors that have already been computed via lattice QCD.

The symmetry arises in the limit of infinite heavy quark mass, where spin-dependent interactions become negligible, leading to degeneracy in the spin states of the heavy quark and its hadronic bound states.

In the context of this paper, HQSS is used to constrain the axial and vector form factors that are not yet available from lattice QCD simulations, thereby improving the theoretical precision of NP sensitivity studies.
Different implementations of HQSS introduce theoretical uncertainties, which are explicitly taken into account in the analysis to assess the robustness of the NP discovery potential.
quantitative: The paper uses HQSS to relate form factors in the Λc→Λ decay, particularly for axial and vector currents, though no specific numerical values for form factors are provided in the text.
The symmetry is applied under the assumption that the heavy quark mass is much larger than ΛQCD, i.e., m_c ≫ ΛQCD, which justifies the validity of the symmetry in the Λc system.

The form factors are parametrized in terms of dimensionless functions that are constrained by HQSS, such as f_1, f_2, g_1, g_2, etc., though their exact expressions are not given in the provided text.

Uncertainties from different HQSS implementations are quantified as part of the theoretical error budget, but no specific numerical uncertainty values are reported in the abstract or sections provided.

- C2：方法中应用HQSS — 满足。证据：“rely on Lattice QCD (LQCD) ... using HQSS” 对比 “HQSS”

content_sections.I Introduction: Hadron weak decays probe the flavour structure of matter, where unknown Beyond Standard Model (BSM) effects would manifest as differences between the SM prediction and precise measurements. The reader can consult Refs. citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3 for comprehensive reviews in both light and heavy quark sectors. In recent years, experimental hints to lepton flavour universality (LFU) violation observed in b→c​ℓ​ν¯ℓ→𝑏𝑐ℓsubscript¯𝜈ℓbto cellbarnuell with different hadrons in the initial and final states have received considerable attention. Theoretical studies and experimental searches have also been extended to the weak decays of c𝑐c and s𝑠s quarks. Although, for some quantities, the deviations from the SM have become smaller (a recent update can be found in Ref. citebib.bib4), the theoretical strategies developed to identify and predict promising observables are valuable tools to keep putting the SM under scrutiny. Baryon decays are complementary to meson ones, providing a different, richer, sensitivity to new physics (NP), particularly when the study of decays rates is generalized to consider angular distributions and asymmetries. The Λb→Λc​ℓ−​ν¯ℓ→subscriptΛ𝑏subscriptΛ𝑐superscriptℓsubscript¯𝜈ℓLambdabtoLambdac,ell-barnuell decay has been extensively investigated in the SM and beyondcitebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31. Some studies considered Λb→p​ℓ−​ν¯ℓ→subscriptΛ𝑏𝑝superscriptℓsubscript¯𝜈ℓLambdabto p,ell-barnuell as wellcitebib.bib5, bib.bib10, bib.bib32. In most cases, lattice QCD (LQCD) input has been used but quark modelcitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, heavy quark effective theorycitebib.bib38 and sum rulecitebib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42 calculations have also been performed. The ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab inclusive semileptonic decays were examined in Ref. citebib.bib43. The transitions from ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab to Λc∗superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑐Lambdac* excited states were considered in Refs. citebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50. The Λc→Λ​ℓ+​νℓ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscriptℓsubscript𝜈ℓLambdactoLambda,ell+nuell decay was also discussed in Ref. citebib.bib35, bib.bib25. In the light-quark sector, the NP discovery potential of ΛΛLambda, ΣΣSigma and ΞΞXi semileptonic decays has been explored in Ref. citebib.bib51. From the perspective of BSM searches, rare Σ→p​ℓ​ℓ→Σ𝑝ℓℓSigmato pellell decays are also interestingcitebib.bib52, bib.bib53. Asymmetries in the angular distributions of Σ0→Λ​γ→superscriptΣ0Λ𝛾Sigma0toLambdagamma compared to the corresponding ones with antiparticles would give access to new sources of CP violationcitebib.bib54. New experimental results are the driving force for these theory developments. The study of hadron decays, including hyperon ones, proceeds at BESIII, Belle II and LHCb. The experimental program of LHCb and Belle II encompasses a variety of b→s→𝑏𝑠bto s and b→c→𝑏𝑐bto c processes. BESIII, operating in a regime where Λc+​Λ¯c−superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑐superscriptsubscript¯Λ𝑐Lambdac+barLambdac- pairs are produced in the exclusive two-body channel with no additional particles, has recently performed updated measurements of Λc→Λ​ℓ+​νℓ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscriptℓsubscript𝜈ℓLambdactoLambda,ell+,nuell decays with ℓ=e,μℓ𝑒𝜇ell=e,,mucitebib.bib55, bib.bib56. We study the impact of NP contributions to the Λc→Λ​ℓ+​νℓ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscriptℓsubscript𝜈ℓLambdactoLambda,ell+,nuell decay, relying on the general framework of Ref. citebib.bib21 for general q→q′→𝑞superscript𝑞′qto qprime charged-current transitions involving left-handed neutrinos. We consider scalar, pseudo-scalar and right-handed tensor NP interactions, as well as left- and right-handed corrections to the SM, with interaction strengths given by Wilson coefficients. The effective Hamiltonian is a low-energy realization of the Standard Model Effective Field Theory (SMEFT)citebib.bib57, bib.bib58. The QCD input can be parametrized in terms of hadronic structure functions built from matrix elements of the operators involved. These structure functions are written in terms of transition form factors, which depend on the masses of the initial and final particles and on the invariant mass of the outgoing lepton pair. These form factors are directly taken from lattice QCD determinations, when possible. Otherwise we resort to approximations to express the unknown form factors in terms of known ones. In line with the standard hypothesis of flavour hierarchy of NP, we assume that ℓ=eℓ𝑒ell=e is well described by the SM and focus on the ℓ=μℓ𝜇ell=mu channel. The fact that (pseudo)scalar and tensor NP contributions are 𝒪​(mℓ)𝒪subscript𝑚ℓO(mell) and therefore suppressed in the e𝑒e channel further reinforces the assumption. In Sec. we first present the effective Hamiltonian, define the observables under consideration, the QCD input, and present the NP contributions and their constrains. The sensitivity to BSM effects is explored in Sec. for LFU ratios and in Ref. for the coefficients in the angular decomposition of the decay rate. The conclusions of our study are summarised in Sec. .
content_sections.II The Λc→Λ​ℓ+​νℓ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscri: To investigate the Λc→Λ→subscriptΛ𝑐ΛLambdactoLambda semileptonic decay we rely on the most general low-energy dimension-six effective Hamiltonian for the c→s​ℓ+​νℓ→𝑐𝑠superscriptℓsubscript𝜈ℓcto s,ell+,nuell transition, considering only left-handed neutrinos and lepton-flavour conserving transitions (see for instance Ref. citebib.bib59) Heffsubscript𝐻effdisplaystyle Hrm eff =displaystyle= 4​GF2Vc​s[(1+ϵLℓ)(s¯γαPLc)(ν¯ℓPRγαℓ)+ϵRℓ(s¯γαPRc)(ν¯ℓPRγαℓ)displaystylefrac4GFsqrt2VcsBig[(1+epsilonellL),(barsgammaalphaPLc) (barnuellPRgammaalphaell)+epsilonellR,(barsgammaalphaPRc) (barnuellPRgammaalphaell) with PL,R=(1∓γ5)/2subscript𝑃𝐿𝑅minus-or-plus1subscript𝛾52PL,R=(1mpgamma5)/2 denoting the projectors onto left and right chiralities, respectively. GFsubscript𝐺𝐹GF is the Fermi decay constant and Vc​ssubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠Vcs the pertinent Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix element. In the domain of applicability of SMEFT, where the NP scale ΛΛLambda is much higher than the electroweak one, the strength of NP terms is determined by flavour (ℓℓell) dependent Wilson coefficients ϵXℓsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵℓ𝑋epsilonellX, which are suppressed by v2/Λ2superscript𝑣2superscriptΛ2v2/Lambda2, where v𝑣v is the Higgs vacuum expectation valuecitebib.bib57. Although these Wilson coefficients are in general complex, we adopt the CP-conserving limit and treat them as real in the following. Sub-index X=L𝑋𝐿X=L, R𝑅R, S𝑆S, P𝑃P, T𝑇T denotes the left-handed, right-handed, scalar, pseudoscalar and tensor structure of the corresponding c→s→𝑐𝑠cto s transition operators1Notice that the Wilson coefficient for the term proportional to the left-handed quark current is denoted ϵLℓsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵℓ𝐿epsilonellL differing with the notation of Ref. citebib.bib59 where it is represented as ϵVℓsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵℓ𝑉epsilonellV. . Note that, for left-handed neutrinos, a tensor operator with quark chirality PLsubscript𝑃𝐿PL instead of PRsubscript𝑃𝑅PR vanishes identically, as can be shown with the help of Dirac algebra.

For the Λc+​(p)→Λ​(p′)​ℓ+​(k′)​νl​(k)→superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑐𝑝Λsuperscript𝑝′superscriptℓsuperscript𝑘′subscript𝜈𝑙𝑘Lambdac+(p)rightarrowLambda(pprime),ell+(kprime),nul(k) decay, the double-differential branching ratio ℬ≡Γ/ΓtotℬΓsubscriptΓtotBequivGamma/Gammatexttot, in terms of q2=(k+k′)2superscript𝑞2superscript𝑘superscript𝑘′2q2=(k+kprime)2 and the angle θ𝜃theta between the ΛΛLambda and ℓ+superscriptℓell+ three-momenta in the leptonic rest frame, where qμ=(q2,0→)superscript𝑞𝜇superscript𝑞2→0qmu=(sqrtq2,vec0), can be cast ascitebib.bib60, bib.bib59, bib.bib21 d2​ℬd​q2​d​cos⁡θ=|Vc​s|2​[c0ℓ​(q2)+c1ℓ​(q2)​cos⁡θ+c2ℓ​(q2)​cos2⁡θ],superscriptd2ℬdsuperscript𝑞2d𝜃superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠2delimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑐ℓ0superscript𝑞2subscriptsuperscript𝑐ℓ1superscript𝑞2𝜃subscriptsuperscript𝑐ℓ2superscript𝑞2superscript2𝜃fracrm d2Brm dq2rm dcostheta=|Vcs|2left[cell0(q2)+cell1(q2)costheta+cell2(q2)cos2thetaright],, where |Vc​s|2superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠2|Vcs|2 has been factorised for convenience. The full ΛcsubscriptΛ𝑐Lambdac width Γtot=(3.252±0.050)×10−12subscriptΓtotplus-or-minus3.2520.050superscript1012Gammatexttot=(3.252pm 0.050)times 10-12GeV has been obtained from its mean life τ=(202.4±3.1)×10−15𝜏plus-or-minus202.43.1superscript1015tau=(202.4pm 3.1)times 10-15scitebib.bib61. The functions ci​(q2)subscript𝑐𝑖superscript𝑞2ci(q2) entirely characterise the decay and can be extracted from experimentally measurable quantities. For example, as shown in Eqs. -, they can be obtained from the single-differential decay width d​Γ/d​q2dΓdsuperscript𝑞2rm dGamma/rm dq2, the forward-backward asymmetry, 𝒜FBsubscript𝒜FBArmFB, and the convexity, 𝒜π/3subscript𝒜𝜋3Api/3. Our analysis of NP contributions is performed in terms of c0,1,2ℓ​(q2)subscriptsuperscript𝑐ℓ012superscript𝑞2cell0,1,2(q2). We take advantage of the general framework developed in Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib23 for the study of hadron semileptonic decays in the presence of NP. Appendix discloses how the formalism derived in these references is adapted to the present case. In order to reduce the theoretical and experimental uncertainties, it is useful to consider LFU ratios. In our case Ri≡ci​(mμ,q2,ϵμ)ci​(me,q2,ϵe)=ciμ​(q2,ϵμ)cie​SM​(q2)+𝒪​(ϵe)​≃⏟if​ϵe≪ϵμ​1+𝒪​(mμ2/x2)+𝒪​(ϵμ),subscript𝑅𝑖subscript𝑐𝑖subscript𝑚𝜇superscript𝑞2superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇subscript𝑐𝑖subscript𝑚𝑒superscript𝑞2superscriptitalic-ϵ𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝜇superscript𝑞2superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝑒SMsuperscript𝑞2𝒪superscriptitalic-ϵ𝑒subscript⏟similar-to-or-equalsmuch-less-thanifsuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝑒superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇1𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜇2superscript𝑥2𝒪superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇Riequivfracci(mmu,q2,epsilonmu)ci(me,q2,epsilone)=fraccimu(q2,epsilonmu)ciermSM(q2)+O(epsilone)underbracesimeqtextif epsilonellepsilonmu1+O(mmu2/x2)+O(epsilonmu) , with x2=q2,mΛ2,mΛc2superscript𝑥2superscript𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑚Λ2superscriptsubscript𝑚subscriptΛ𝑐2x2=q2,mLambda2,mLambdac2. The common assumption ϵe≪ϵμmuch-less-thansuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝑒superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇epsilonellepsilonmu arises, for example, in many NP models which explain the fermion mass hierarchycitebib.bib62, bib.bib63. In these ratios, not only VCKMsubscript𝑉CKMVrm CKM cancels out but also an important advantage resides in the fact that in the SM the deviation from unity enters at 𝒪​(mμ2/x2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜇2superscript𝑥2O(mmu2/x2) and so does the SM uncertainty. By using ratios a significant improvement in the sensitivity to NP is hence achieved, as compared to cisubscript𝑐𝑖ci values themselves, where the SM uncertainties enter without any suppression.

content_sections.III Sensitivity to new physics in the μ𝜇: We have posed ourselves the question of how large should a new physics contribution be in order to be experimentally identifiable, given the QCD uncertainties. We focus on the μ𝜇mu channel, under the assumption ϵμ≫ϵe≃0much-greater-thansuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇superscriptitalic-ϵ𝑒similar-to-or-equals0epsilonmuggepsilonesimeq 0, and study ratios to reduce the uncertainties. We have defined the signals to which we are sensitive as the ones that do not overlap with the SM prediction within 1​σ1𝜎1sigma errors. In other words, if a Wilson coefficient has a given true value, ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX, we would be sensitive to it at a certain q2superscript𝑞2q2 only if its magnitude is large enough to completely separate the 1​σ1𝜎1sigma NP band from the SM one. This condition has some degree of arbitrariness and would still be insufficient to claim a discovery in an experiment but serves the purpose of getting an idea of the NP discovery potential of each of the LFU ratios, Risubscript𝑅𝑖Ri, as functions of q2superscript𝑞2q2. We define ϵXsenssuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋sensepsilonXrmsens as the minimum, in magnitude, ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX value which satisfies this condition. We evaluate them one by one, setting the rest of the Wilson coefficients to zero. Recalling the notation of Eq. we define ϵXsenssuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋sensepsilonXrmsens as the one that satisfies |Ri​(ϵX)−RiSM|>Δ​Ri​(ϵX)+Δ​RiSMsubscript𝑅𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖SMΔsubscript𝑅𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖SMabsolutevalueRi(epsilonX)-RirmSM>Delta Ri(epsilonX)+Delta RirmSM with a minimal |ϵX|subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋|epsilonX|. Here Δ​RiS​MΔsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖𝑆𝑀Delta RiSM and Δ​Ri​(ϵX)Δsubscript𝑅𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋Delta Ri(epsilonX) denote the 1​σ1𝜎1sigma errors of RiSMsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖SMRirmSM and Ri​(ϵX)subscript𝑅𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋Ri(epsilonX), which includes NP. On the one hand, the larger the ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX, the more promising the observable would be to detect a given NP signal. On the other hand, the SMEFT calculation has a worse convergence for larger ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX. We take this into account by introducing a SMEFT truncation error within Δ​Ri​(ϵX)Δsubscript𝑅𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋Delta Ri(epsilonX), which is defined below. We explore the condition of Eq. for positive and negative ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX separately. In general we do not obtain the same absolute value in both cases due to quadratic corrections. Quadratic terms in SMEFT are briefly discussed in Ref. citebib.bib68, while the theoretical uncertainty of SMEFT is addressed in Sect.2.7.6 of Ref. citebib.bib69. According to this reference, to properly account for the truncation error one should calculate to one-loop order. It is also argued that an estimate can be given without such a computation, but including Wilson coefficients of the dimension-eight Lagrangian (Eq. (2.79) therein). Such studies are beyond the scope of the present work. We content ourselves with a simpler approach, relying on a common truncation error estimate given by the difference between linearization and quadratization of the matrix element squared, and define the following uncertainty for cisubscript𝑐𝑖ci, Eq. Δ​ciSMEFT​(ϵX)=ϵX2​max⁡|ciSM|,|ciX​l|,|ciX​q|. Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖SMEFTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋2superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖SMsubscriptsuperscript𝑐𝑋l𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝑐𝑋q𝑖Delta citextSMEFT(epsilonX)=epsilonX2max|cirmSM|,|cXrmli|,|cXrmqi|,. It estimates the size of the unaccounted 𝒪​(ϵ2)𝒪superscriptitalic-ϵ2O(epsilon2) contributions, only with information from ℒSMsubscriptℒSMLrmSM and the NP dimension-six Lagrangian ℒ6subscriptℒ6L6. To simplify the notation, index ℓ=μℓ𝜇ell=mu has been omitted throughout this section. Correspondingly, Δ​RiSMEFT​(ϵX)=Δ​ciSMEFT​(ϵX)cie​SM. Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖SMEFTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖SMEFTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝑒SMDelta RitextSMEFT(epsilonX)=fracDelta citextSMEFT(epsilonX)cierm SM . We consider this a conservative estimation of the truncation error. In order to obtain ϵXsenssuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋sensepsilonXrmsens from Eq. we sum in quadratures the error propagated from the LQCD vector and axial form factors, Δ​RiLQCDΔsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖LQCDDelta RirmLQCD, with the truncation one, yielding (Δ​Ri​(ϵX))2=(Δ​RiLQCD​(ϵX))2+(Δ​RiSMEFT​(ϵX))2. superscriptΔsubscript𝑅𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋2superscriptΔsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖LQCDsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋2superscriptΔsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖SMEFTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋2left(Delta Ri(epsilonX)right)2=left(Delta RirmLQCD(epsilonX)right)2+left(Delta RitextSMEFT(epsilonX)right)2 . In addition, the HQSS uncertainty in ciS,P,Tsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝑆𝑃𝑇ciS,P,T is taken into account by covering all three versions of the form factors introduced in Sec. . In this case, this reduces to taking the least favourable choice, namely the HQSS prescription associated with the minimum perturbation to the SM, maximising therefore |ϵXsens|superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋sensabsolutevalueepsilonXrmsens. First of all, we study the ratio of integrated widths, i. e. we introduce NP contributions in the numerator of Eq. . In this observable, |ϵLsens|=6.3×10−4superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿sens6.3superscript104absolutevalueepsilonLrmsens=6.3times 10-4, |ϵRsens|=2.1×10−3superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅sens2.1superscript103absolutevalueepsilonRrmsens=2.1times 10-3, |ϵTsens|=8.0×10−4superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇sens8.0superscript104absolutevalueepsilonTrmsens=8.0times 10-4, |ϵSsens|=8.9×10−3superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑆sens8.9superscript103absolutevalueepsilonSrmsens=8.9times 10-3, but we are not sensitive to ϵPsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑃epsilonP. This is a very good sensitivity, beyond the multi-TeV scale. Indeed, the ϵitalic-ϵepsilon value corresponding approximately to a NP scale ΛΛLambda of 1 TeV is estimated by a naive power counting (see Sec. 2.1 of Ref. citebib.bib69), to be |ϵ|=v2/Λ2≃0.0625italic-ϵsuperscript𝑣2superscriptΛ2similar-to-or-equals0.0625absolutevalueepsilon=v2/Lambda2simeq 0.0625, with v≈250𝑣250vapprox 250GeV. Next, we analyse the LFU ratios defined in Eq. . The first thing to notice is that the SM R2subscript𝑅2R2 is independent of the form factors. Therefore Δ​R2SM=0Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑅2SM0Delta R2rm SM=0. Applying the criterion of Eq. , we are sensitive to arbitrarily small NP ϵL,R,Tsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿𝑅𝑇epsilonL,R,T, which are the ones that contribute to c2subscript𝑐2c2. This has to be understood as a result of neglecting 𝒪​(ϵ2)𝒪superscriptitalic-ϵ2O(epsilon2) (and disregarding the experimental uncertainty). Nevertheless, this result clearly indicates that R2subscript𝑅2R2 is a very sensitive observable to NP contributions. The drawback is that it might be difficult to measure, because c2subscript𝑐2c2 is related to the angular asymmetry 𝒜π/3subscript𝒜𝜋3Api/3, Eq. . We have also observed that R0subscript𝑅0R0, which is dominated by the ratio of decay widths, is more sensitive to NP than R1subscript𝑅1R1. In consequence we put the focus on R0subscript𝑅0R0 in the following. In Fig. we study the sensitivity of R0subscript𝑅0R0 to NP signals of different chirality, ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX, as a function of q2superscript𝑞2q2. The ϵXsens​(q2)superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋senssuperscript𝑞2epsilonXrmsens(q2) are reported; they set the boundaries of the regions of sensitivity. Red lines denote the Wilson coefficient values corresponding to a NP scale of 2 (dashed) and 10TeV (solid). The plots reveal an excellent sensitivity to ϵLsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿epsilonL and ϵTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇epsilonT (upper panels). For ϵRsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅epsilonR, the sensitivity is higher in the region of medium to high q2superscript𝑞2q2 (lower left panel). We are also sensitive to quite small ϵSsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑆epsilonS (lower right panel). The only current to which this ratio is not sensitive is the pseudoscalar one. In this case the ϵPsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑃epsilonP signal is too small to be discerned from the SM, taking into account the 𝒪​(ϵP2)𝒪superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑃2O(epsilonP2) theoretical error. Finally, from our analysis we conclude that the main obstacle to resolve a NP signal from the theoretical point of view is the LQCD error of the (axial)vector form factors, prevailing over the HQSS errors that accompany some NP contributions, and the SMEFT uncertainty, which can be sizeable for relatively large ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX. One may also wonder about the experimental uncertainty and therefore the real sensitivity in practice. At present, the experimental error is much larger than the theoretical one, at least in observables where SM uncertainties cancel. To illustrate this, we consider the integrated ratio R𝑅R (whose SM value is given in Eq. ), setting all the ϵX≠L=0subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋𝐿0epsilonXneq L=0 and accounting for the theoretical uncertainties. For instance, to be sensitive to ϵL=1.2×10−2subscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿1.2superscript102epsilonL=1.2times 10-2 as the central value extracted in Ref. citebib.bib60, one would need the experimental error to be reduced to 10% of its current size5Recall that we set all the ϵX≠L=0subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋𝐿0epsilonXneq L=0 and account for the theoretical uncertainties. . In addition, as discussed in Sec. , the tensions that arise in the description of Λc→Λ​e+​νe→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝑒subscript𝜈𝑒LambdactoLambda e+nue with SM LQCD input compromise the extraction of ϵXμsuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋𝜇epsilonXmu from Λc→Λ​μ+​νμ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝜇subscript𝜈𝜇LambdactoLambdamu+numu data alone. On the other hand, recalling the aforementioned 𝒪​(mμ2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜇2O(mmu2) suppression of the form factor uncertainty in the SM contribution to LFU ratios, we find that these ratios are more reliable in order to look for NP deviations, because systematic differences in the SM form factor largely cancel out. In Fig. we report the LFU ratio for the differential decay width, defined in analogy with Eq. as RΓ​(ϵXμ)=d​Γ/d​q2​(Λc→Λ​μ+​νμ,ϵXμ)d​Γ/d​q2​SM​(Λc→Λ​e+​νe),subscript𝑅Γsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝑋𝑑Γ𝑑superscript𝑞2→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝜇subscript𝜈𝜇subscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝑋𝑑Γ𝑑superscript𝑞2SM→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝑒subscript𝜈𝑒RGamma(epsilonmuX)=fracdGamma/dq2(LambdactoLambdamu+numu,epsilonmuX)dGamma/dq2,rm SM(LambdactoLambda e+nue) , compared to the corresponding experimental result from BESIIIcitebib.bib56. The theoretical result includes a black 1σ𝜎sigma SM band from LQCD and a red one accounting for the allowed range in the Wilson coefficients from Ref. citebib.bib60. In line with the previous comments about the ratio of integrated widths, the present experimental uncertainty is still insufficient to put competitive bounds on the ϵμsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇epsilonmu Wilson coefficients.
content_sections.IV Sensitivity to new physics in the μ𝜇\: Here we report an analysis similar to the one performed for LFU ratios but at the level of the ciμ​(q2)subscriptsuperscript𝑐𝜇𝑖superscript𝑞2cmui(q2) functions. It is free of assumptions on the flavour hierarchy of Wilson coefficients. Furthermore, ciμ​(q2)subscriptsuperscript𝑐𝜇𝑖superscript𝑞2cmui(q2) would not receive any contribution from the statistical experimental error in the Λc→Λ​e+​νe→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝑒subscript𝜈𝑒LambdactoLambda e+nue decay. Unfortunately, the sensitivity is considerably reduced in comparison with the one expected for the LFU ratios explored in the previous section. We present only the cases where the NP perturbation was found to be relatively large in Sec. (see Fig. ). In consequence, we do not take into account (pseudo)scalar terms. The left-handed contributions to c0,1,2subscript𝑐012c0,1,2 are studied in Fig. (a)-(c). In this case the truncation error is governed by ϵL2​ciL​l=2​ϵL2​ciSMsuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿2superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝐿l2superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿2superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖SMepsilonL2ciLrml=2epsilonL2cirmSM and the convergence is good, at least from the perspective of the uncertainty that we have defined. The error is dominated by the one of the LQCD vector form factors, even for high values of ϵLsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿epsilonL. The plots show that the study of c0,1,2subscript𝑐012c0,1,2 for the present process is mostly insensitive to the ϵLsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝐿epsilonL range constrained by pseudoscalar meson decays in Ref. citebib.bib60. The right-handed contributions are in general modest, being more prominent in c0subscript𝑐0c0 than in c1subscript𝑐1c1 and c2subscript𝑐2c2. We show the sensitivity to ϵRsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅epsilonR in c0subscript𝑐0c0 in Fig. (d). It is much poorer than in the other displayed cases. Indeed this observable is only sensitive to quite low ΛΛLambda (below the TeV) in the naive power counting estimation6Approaching the electroweak scale, less NP models survive that have not been ruled out empirically. . At low q2superscript𝑞2q2, c0subscript𝑐0c0 is not sensitive to ϵRsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅epsilonR at all. In other words, the condition in Eq. is not satisfied. Actually, due to the small magnitude of c0Rsuperscriptsubscript𝑐0𝑅c0R at low q2superscript𝑞2q2, ϵRsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅epsilonR needs to be large to be distinguishable. However, at such ϵRsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅epsilonR values, the truncation error becomes large enough to make Eq. impossible to satisfy. The difference between positive and negative ϵRsenssuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅sensepsilonRrmsens is caused by the ϵR2​c0R​qsuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅2superscriptsubscript𝑐0𝑅qepsilonR2c0Rrmq term, which is important at high ϵRsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅epsilonR. A similar scenario is encountered for the tensor contribution to c0subscript𝑐0c0 studied in Fig. (e). Now the decay under study is not sensitive to negative ϵTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇epsilonT. This is due to the twofold impact of the quadratic term in Eq. , which is relatively large: not only does it notably increase Δ​c0​(ϵT)Δsubscript𝑐0subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇Delta c0(epsilonT), it also conspires to cancel the linear contribution to c0​(ϵT)subscript𝑐0subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇c0(epsilonT), having the opposite sign. As argued in the previous section, the quadratic contribution is not complete. Hence, the fact that ϵR,T2superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑅𝑇2epsilonR,T2 are large implies that our sensitivity estimates are less solid in these cases. This is partially accounted by the truncation error. In all the other ciXsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝑋ciX analysed in Fig. , Δ​ciSMEFTΔsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖SMEFTDelta cirmSMEFT is negligible for low values of ϵXsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋epsilonX (ϵX≤0.1subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑋0.1epsilonXleq 0.1). The most promising analysis turns out to be the study of ϵTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇epsilonT in c1subscript𝑐1c1, Fig. (f). As anticipated in Sec. , the maximum in the tensor contribution coincides with a SM minimum (Fig. (b)). This leads to a good sensitivity in the low-medium q2superscript𝑞2q2 region as can be seen in Fig. (f). The quadratic coefficient c1T​qsuperscriptsubscript𝑐1𝑇qc1Trmq is small and the truncation error is negligible (it is given by ϵT2​c1T​lsuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑐1𝑇lepsilonT2c1Trml). The critical uncertainty in this scenario is actually the one of the LQCD vector and axial form factors Δ​c1LQCDΔsuperscriptsubscript𝑐1LQCDDelta c1rmLQCD. In this case, the Λc→Λ​μ+​νμ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝜇subscript𝜈𝜇LambdactoLambdamu+numu decay is sensitive to a part of the band allowed by pseudoscalar meson decayscitebib.bib60 without resorting to ratios. Finally, the tensor contribution to c2subscript𝑐2c2 is not discussed because there is no linear term in ϵTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇epsilonT. As a final remark, we stress that the key uncertainties to asses in order to gain sensitivity to NP with the ΛcsubscriptΛ𝑐Lambdac semileptonic decay are the ones of the LQCD vector and axial form factors because they appear in the leading SM term. In the case of the tensor contribution to c0subscript𝑐0c0, this error competes with the truncation one. In this sense, a more precise estimate of the SMEFT uncertainty, taking into account all terms at 𝒪​(1/Λ4)𝒪1superscriptΛ4O(1/Lambda4) would be very useful as well. In addition, it is clear that a reliable determination of the truncation error would provide more consistency to the analysis in general, including the left and right-handed contributions. In the tensor term the HQSS uncertainty is not so critical, since it is part of the NP correction (hence it is multiplied by ϵTsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑇epsilonT) and is therefore small.
content_sections.V Conclusions and outlook: We have studied the Λc→Λ​ℓ+​νℓ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscriptℓsubscript𝜈ℓLambdactoLambdaell+nuell decay, investigating the impact of NP currents in the ℓ=μℓ𝜇ell=mu channel. To accomplish this task, an effective Hamiltonian for c→s​ℓ+​νℓ→𝑐𝑠superscriptℓsubscript𝜈ℓcto sell+nuell, based on SMEFT has been employed. We have also relied on the LQCD results for the axial and vector Λc→Λ→subscriptΛ𝑐ΛLambdactoLambda form factors obtained in Ref. citebib.bib64. The form factors required to describe the Λc→Λ→subscriptΛ𝑐ΛLambdactoLambda transitions driven by scalar, pseudoscalar and tensor operators have been expressed in terms of the SM axial and vector ones by taking advantage of the large c𝑐c-quark mass. Differences arising from alternative approximations to implement this connection among form factors are treated as a theoretical uncertainty. The NP analyses are performed in terms of the ci​(q2)subscript𝑐𝑖superscript𝑞2ci(q2) functions that drive the dependence on the angle between the ΛΛLambda and the μ+superscript𝜇mu+ in the leptonic rest frame. We have first obtained the q2superscript𝑞2q2 shapes of the NP contributions to cisubscript𝑐𝑖ci, with their QCD uncertainty bands. Lepton flavour universality ratios received special attention. In the first place, owing to the LQCD form factor input, a SM ratio Γ​(Λc→Λ​μ+​νμ)/Γ​(Λc→Λ​e+​νe)=0.96884​statLQCDΓ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝜇subscript𝜈𝜇Γ→subscriptΛ𝑐Λsuperscript𝑒subscript𝜈𝑒0.96884subscriptsuperscript61LQCDstatGamma(LambdactoLambdamu+numu)/Gamma(LambdactoLambda e+nue)=0.96884rm LQCDrmstat is obtained. LFU ratios are particularly advantageous in the search for NP due to the 𝒪​(mμ2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜇2O(mmu2) suppression of the SM uncertainty. This is apparent in the reported Ri=ciμ/ciesubscript𝑅𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝑒Ri=cimu/cie, i=0,1,2𝑖012i=0,1,2, ratios. We have then studied the theoretical sensitivity to a potential NP current of a certain chirality in the μ𝜇mu channel, accounting also for the theoretical uncertainty in the SMEFT truncation. Assuming a ϵμ≫ϵemuch-greater-thansuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇superscriptitalic-ϵ𝑒epsilonmuggepsilone hierarchy, we have analysed different LFU ratios. In the ratio of integrated widths the sensitivity is already very high, probing the multi-TeV scale, except for the pseudoscalar interaction. However, to realise this theoretical expectation, the experimental uncertainty should be reduced as detailed in the work. In addition, we have shown that the ratios R0subscript𝑅0R0 and R2subscript𝑅2R2 have an excellent sensitivity to NP of any type except the pseudoscalar one. Finally, an analogous sensitivity analysis has been performed on the ciμsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝑖𝜇cimu observables themselves, which are free of the flavour hierarchy assumption. In this case the sensitivity varies notably depending on the NP chirality. In particular, the NP tensor contribution to c1subscript𝑐1c1 displays a promising discovery potential. We have identified the LQCD uncertainty of the axial and vector form factors as the most important one when it comes to improving the sensitivity to NP. Besides, we have also seen that a better estimation of the SMEFT uncertainty would substantially improve the precision of this kind of analysis. As a future development, the inclusion of right-handed neutrinos in the analysis would be interesting, since they are present in a large variety of NP models and can be implemented in a straightforward way within the framework established in Refs. citebib.bib21 and citebib.bib23.
