Therefore, relations (LABEL:Zbs3) entail two important messages2Because of the coupled-channel transitions, the physical Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s appear to be dynamical mixtures of the basis states introduced in Eq. . Thus, these messages should be regarded as being originated from the strict HQSS. • in the strict HQSS limit both Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states carry spin-0 and spin-1 components with equal weight and • the spin-0 and spin-1 components appear with different relative signs in the two states. Importantly, as demonstrated in Ref. citebib.bib21, the above nontrivial interference pattern is indeed consistent with the data, where the two Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb peaks appear to be equally strong in the decay chains Υ​Υ10860displaystyleUpsilon →→displaystyleto π​Zb(′)→π​π​Υ​(n​S),n=1,2,3,formulae-sequence→𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆𝑛123displaystylepi Zb(prime)topipiUpsilon(nS),quad n=1,2,3, Υ​Υ10860displaystyleUpsilon →→displaystyleto π​Zb(′)→π​π​hb​(m​P),m=1,2,formulae-sequence→𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃𝑚12displaystylepi Zb(prime)topipi hb(mP),quad m=1,2, with a constructive interference in the former and a destructive interference in the latter case. This underlines the fact that, although in general coupled-channel transitions mix the basis states , nevertheless, both wave functions of the physical Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s still contain the Sb​b¯=0subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏0Sbbarb=0 and Sb​b¯=1subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏1Sbbarb=1 components in comparable shares. The same pattern emerges in the tetraquark picture proposed in Ref. citebib.bib25, where the driving subclusters are compact diquarks and anti-diquarks. In contrast to the two scenarios just sketched, it should be stressed that without sizable heavy-quark spin-0 and spin-1 clusters within the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states the observed pattern is unexplained and appears to be very unnatural. In the limit of the heavy quark mass going to infinity, the heavy-quark spin needs to be conserved. It is thus interesting to investigate, how this limit is reached in the scenario outlined above. It turns out that what controls the importance of the hbsubscriptℎ𝑏hb final states is the ratio of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s mass splitting, which violates HQSS and thus vanishes in the heavy quark limit, and the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s widths, which survive the heavy quark limit. This will be discussed in detail below.

content_sections.IV Inclusion of the pion interaction in: In the previous section it was demonstrated how the explicit HQSS breaking due to the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb mass splitting could be balanced by their narrow widths to provide similar branching fractions for the reactions with and without the heavy-quark spin flip. Here we discuss consequences of yet another peculiar property of the HQSS violation in the reaction Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopipi hb(mP) as compared with the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final states. Namely, the tree level amplitude of the reaction Υ​→π​π​Υ​(n​S)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆UpsilontopipiUpsilon(nS) admits two momentum-dependent chiral contact terms (with two derivatives of the pion fields), which parameterize a short range interaction in the system, already in the strict heavy-quark limitcitebib.bib30, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24. These contact terms can be matched with two subtraction constants needed to include the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in this reaction. In contrast, the leading contact interactions in the decays Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopipi hb(mP), while having the same chiral structure, violate HQSS and therefore should be strongly suppressed relative to those for the transitions that conserve the heavy quark spin. In what follows, we discuss how this fact can be reconciled with the fact that in the individual reactions Υ​→π​Zb→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋subscript𝑍𝑏→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zbtopipi hb(mP) and Υ​→π​Zb′→π​π​hb​(m​P),→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zbprimetopipi hb(mP), the same number of subtractions is required to render the dispersive integrals finite as in the channel with the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final state. Specifically, we demonstrate that the destructive interference of the two amplitudes in the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb channel (as opposed to the constructive interference for the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final state) provides a better convergence of the resulting dispersive integral, so that a smaller number of subtractions is required. To bring a quantitative twist to this discussion, we employ the coupled-channel EFT-based approach developed for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states in Refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib31 with the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI for the transition Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopipi hb(mP) to demonstrate that its role in this channel is indeed suppressed, in contrast to the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) final states citebib.bib24.

In this section we employ the dispersive approach developed in the previous section to generalise the analysis of the experimental data on the dipion transitions Υ​→π+​π−​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860superscript𝜋superscript𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi+pi-hb(mP) with m=1,2𝑚12m=1,2 performed in Ref. citebib.bib28. In particular, we check against the data that the effect of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI is small for a counter term with a strength in line with expectations from heavy quark spin symmetry. In order to proceed, we stick to the technique of the spectral function previously employed in Ref. citebib.bib24 to write M0hb;L​(s)=∫μmin2μmax2𝑑μ2​ρ​(μ2)​M0,stablehb;L​(s,μ),superscriptsubscript𝑀0subscriptℎ𝑏𝐿𝑠superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜇2minsubscriptsuperscript𝜇2maxdifferential-dsuperscript𝜇2𝜌superscript𝜇2superscriptsubscript𝑀0stablesubscriptℎ𝑏𝐿𝑠𝜇M0hb;L(s)=intmu2rm minmu2rm maxdmu2rho(mu2)M0,textstablehb;L(s,mu), where the amplitude M0,stablehb;Lsuperscriptsubscript𝑀0stablesubscriptℎ𝑏𝐿M0,textstablehb;L for stable Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s was defined in the previous section while the integration limits are set as in Ref. citebib.bib24. The spectral density function ρ​(μ2)𝜌superscript𝜇2rho(mu2) can be evaluated as ρ​(μ2)=−1π​Im​U​(μ2),𝜌superscript𝜇21𝜋Im𝑈superscript𝜇2rho(mu2)=-frac1pimboxIm,U(mu2), with U​(μ2)𝑈superscript𝜇2U(mu2) referring to the production amplitude for Υ​→π+​π−​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860superscript𝜋superscript𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi+pi-hb(mP) built in Ref. citebib.bib28 (fit A) via the B∗​B(∗)superscript𝐵superscript𝐵B*B(*)-meson loops but without considering the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI4The function U​(μ2)𝑈superscript𝜇2U(mu2) contains also the effect of the finite width of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states discussed above. . Furthermore, since the latter effect is expected to be small, we do not refit the data but employ all the parameters found in Ref. citebib.bib28 from the fit to the data and only check how the line shapes change upon including the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI and varying the HQSS-breaking constant within an interval consistent with naturalness. In particular, in Fig. we demonstrate the modification of the line shapes in the channels π+​π−​hb​(m​P)superscript𝜋superscript𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃pi+pi-hb(mP) with m=1,2𝑚12m=1,2 when the coefficient c𝑐c in Eq. varies within a natural interval. For definiteness and keeping in mind that the values of c𝑐c consistent with naturalness should be |c|∼1similar-to𝑐1|c|sim 1, we choose a conservative interval |c|⩽10𝑐10|c|leqslant 10. Comparing the red band, which shows the spread with the parameter c𝑐c of the line shapes with the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi FSI included, and the black solid curve, which represents the fit built in Ref. citebib.bib28, one can conclude that, in agreement with the arguments provided in this section, the modification of the line shapes due to the inclusion of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi interaction in the final state is marginal. To be more quantitative, in terms of the χ2/Ndofsuperscript𝜒2subscript𝑁dofchi2/Nrm dof this corresponds to a spread from 1.21 to 1.36 as compared with the value 1.29 obtained for the fit A from Ref. citebib.bib28. Moreover, if c𝑐c is allowed to vary freely, the best fit to the data yields c​(1​P)=24𝑐1𝑃24c(1P)=24 and c​(2​P)=−24𝑐2𝑃24c(2P)=-24 with the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2/dof =1.16, which is only marginally smaller than the lower bound within the natural interval (the red band in Fig. ). However, such values of the parameter c𝑐c returned by the best fit are formally somewhat larger than what we regard as natural. We note also, as a disclaimer, that the fit may use the freedom of varying c𝑐c to partly compensate for other approximations made in the contact fit A of Ref. citebib.bib28, for example, the effect of the neglected one-pion exchange. Since the resulting effect for the hbsubscriptℎ𝑏hb final states is small, we do not dwell on this any further in this work. Although the inclusion of the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in an EFT approach with non-perturbative pions (see, e. g. , Ref. ) is technically more demanding because of the additional left-hand cut introduced in this way, this would be an important step in the future more quantitative studies.
content_sections.V Summary: In this work we investigate the consequences of heavy-quark spin symmetry and the pattern of its breaking for the measured decays Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​Υ​(n​S)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipiUpsilon(nS) (n=1,2,3𝑛123n=1,2,3) and Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipi hb(mP) (m=1,2𝑚12m=1,2). These decays appear to have nearly equal probabilities in spite of the fact that a direct transition from Sb​b¯=1subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏1Sbbarb=1 Υ​Υ10860Upsilon to Sb​b¯=0subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏0Sbbarb=0 hb​(m​P)subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃hb(mP) changes heavy-quark spin and therefore violates HQSS. As argued in Ref. citebib.bib21, this can be naturally understood in the molecular scenario for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states, because in this case Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb appear as orthogonal combinations of the Sb​b¯=0subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏0Sbbarb=0 and Sb​b¯=1subscript𝑆𝑏¯𝑏1Sbbarb=1 components of equal strength. On the other hand, the interference between the contributions of the Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb to the decay amplitude for a π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final state is destructive which results in a vanishing signal in the strict HQSS limit. In contrast to this, the signal for a π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon final state is fully in line with HQSS due to a constructive interference between the two Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states. Thus, in order to have signals of a similar strength in the π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon and π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final states, HQSS in the latter channel must be violated (mz′−mz≠0subscript𝑚superscript𝑧′subscript𝑚𝑧0mzprime-mzneq 0), and this violation should be balanced by another quantity with dimension of a mass of a similar (small) size. We demonstrate that this balance is provided by the small widths of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states ΓzsubscriptΓ𝑧Gammaz. As a consequence, the transition amplitude to the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final state strongly depends on the ratio of these two scales, r=(mz′−mz)/Γz𝑟superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑧′subscript𝑚𝑧subscriptΓ𝑧r=(mzprime-mz)/Gammaz, as given in Eqs. and . Remarkably, while the ratio r𝑟r vanishes in the strict HQSS limit, its value rphys≈3subscript𝑟phys3rrm physapprox 3 obtained for the physical masses of the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s corresponds to the dynamical regime which is already quite close to the opposite limit of r→∞→𝑟rtoinfty — in this limit the absolute values of the transition amplitudes to the π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon and π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb final states are shown to have exactly the same peak positions and strengths. Also, we pinpoint a crucial difference related to HQSS in the role played by the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in the dipion transitions Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​Υ​(n​S)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipiUpsilon(nS) and Υ​→π​Zb(′)→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′→𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilontopi Zb(prime)topipi hb(mP). We demonstrate how the absence of the direct short-range HQSS-conserving operators in the bottomonium transitions Υ​→π​π​hb​(m​P)→Υ10860𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃Upsilon topipi hb(mP) can be reconciled with a subtraction in the dispersive integral if the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI is taken into account. In particular, it is shown that when both Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states are included simultaneously, their leading contributions to the total amplitude cancel against each other. As a result, the dispersive integrals in the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb channels demonstrate a better convergence than those in the π​π​Υ𝜋𝜋ΥpipiUpsilon channels and the remaining subtraction constants in the π​π​hb𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏pipi hb amplitudes are indeed suppressed by HQSS. We have verified that, in agreement with natural expectations, implementing this scheme to take into account the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI does not result in any appreciable change in the fitted line shapes in the π​π​hb​(1​P)𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏1𝑃pipi hb(1P) and π​π​hb​(2​P)𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑏2𝑃pipi hb(2P) channels (contrary to the crucial role played by the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in the π​π​Υ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋Υ𝑛𝑆pipiUpsilon(nS) channels), so that neglecting the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI in the previous analysis of Ref. citebib.bib28, bib.bib31 is indeed justified, at least at the present level of accuracy dictated by the quality of the existing data. Meanwhile, our findings establish a way to improve on such an analysis when/if such improvements are finally called for by the experiment. As a final remark, we notice that while the numerical analysis performed in this work relies on a particular fit to the data built in the framework of the molecular approach for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s, the qualitative arguments presented in this paper are solely based on the interplay of different scales (the mass splitting mz′−mzsubscript𝑚superscript𝑧′subscript𝑚𝑧mzprime-mz and the width ΓzsubscriptΓ𝑧Gammaz in the first place) and therefore can equally apply to any approach to the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb’s which is consistent with HQSS and its leading breaking effects, in particular, to the tetraquark model. Our findings concerning the role played by the π​π/K​K¯𝜋𝜋𝐾¯𝐾pipi/KbarK FSI should be also important for understanding analogous charmonium transitions to the final states π​π​ψ​(n​S)𝜋𝜋𝜓𝑛𝑆pipipsi(nS) and π​π​hc​(m​P)𝜋𝜋subscriptℎ𝑐𝑚𝑃pipi hc(mP) via the Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zc and Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐4020Zc states, though the effects of the HQSS violation in the charmonium sector are expected to be larger. Acknowledgements. The authors thank Saša Prelovšek for a fruitful discussion that triggered this project. This work is supported in part by the NSFC and the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG) through the funds provided to the Sino-German Collaborative Research Center “Symmetries and the Emergence of Structure in QCD” (NSFC Grant No. 11621131001, DFG Grant No. CRC110) and by BMBF (contract No. 05P21PCFP1). A. N. is supported by the Slovenian Research Agency (research core Funding No. P1-0035).

all_papers.title: Heavy-Quark spin symmetry breaking in the Born-Oppenheimer approximation
all_papers.abstract:
Heavy-quark spin symmetry is explicitly broken by the mass splitting between
a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson and its vector partner. This fact plays a
pivotal role in the physics of states whose mass lies close to the threshold of
an open-flavor meson pair, like $X(3872)$. We show that this source of
heavy-quark spin symmetry breaking can be systematically included within the
diabatic representation of the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. We verify that
including all the appropriate coupled channels guarantees conservation of total
angular momentum, parity, and charge-conjugation parity. This marks a
fundamental step towards a unified, first-principles study of quarkonia and
meson molecules with hidden flavor.

sections.19: Lichten, Resonant charge exchange in atomic collisions, Phys
sections.20: Wigner, Group Theory and its Application to the Quantum Mechanics of Atomic Spectra, Academic Press, New York (1959).
sections.22: Voloshin, Heavy quark spin symmetry breaking in near-threshold JP​C=1−−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJ^{PC}=1^{--} quarkonium-like resonances, Phys

qualitative: Heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) is explicitly broken by the mass splitting between a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson (e.g., B) and its vector partner (e.g., B*).
This HQSS breaking plays a crucial role in the physics of states near open-flavor meson pair thresholds, such as the X(3872).

In the Born-Oppenheimer (BO) approximation, HQSS is typically assumed in lattice QCD simulations with static quarks, but this assumption becomes unreliable when considering coupled quark-antiquark and di-meson channels near thresholds.
The breaking of HQSS leads to spin-dependent splitting of di-meson thresholds and modifies the transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations.
The diabatic representation of the BO approximation allows for a systematic and consistent inclusion of HQSS breaking by constructing a spin-dependent interaction matrix.
The inclusion of heavy quark kinetic energies promotes the cylindrical symmetry group D∞h ⊗ CP to the full O(3) ⊗ CP symmetry of QCD, reflecting a more complete description of the system.
The formalism enables a unified, first-principles study of quarkonia and meson molecules with hidden flavor, particularly in the presence of string breaking and spin effects.
quantitative: The D*–D mass splitting is approximately three times larger than the B*–B mass splitting.
The transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations are determined from Dirac algebra and form a matrix of coupling coefficients.
The interaction matrix in the diabatic representation depends explicitly on the spin configuration of the heavy quark-antiquark pair.
Linear combinations of matrix elements, weighted by coefficients from angular momentum theory, yield the potential matrix entries used in the Schrödinger equation.
The master Schrödinger equation in the diabatic BO framework includes both quark-antiquark and di-meson channels with spin-dependent couplings and threshold splittings.

content_sections.1 Introduction: In the Born-Oppenheimer (BO) approximation for QCD, the spectrum of a heavy quark-antiquark system is determined by potentials which are numerically accessible in lattice QCD citebib.bib1. Because of this feature, the BO picture is one of the most promising tools for the study of conventional and unconventional heavy quark-antiquark states from first principles; see, for instance, refs. citebib.bib2, bib.bib3 and references therein. The idea of using lattice QCD calculations of the BO potentials that include the effects of string breaking to precisely determine the spectrum and decays of heavy quark-antiquark systems has been around for quite some time citebib.bib4. Although direct lattice measurements of string breaking have been available for many years citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, achieving such a description remains a current theoretical challenge. Until recently, the main difficulty consisted in overcoming the single-channel approximation traditionally associated with BO citebib.bib2, as string breaking couples quark-antiquark and di-meson channels. Significant theoretical progress in the application of BO with coupled channels has been reported. This includes, for instance, calculations of the bottomonium spectrum in BO using the first calculation of string breaking in lattice QCD citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, phenomenological studies of quarkoniumlike mesons and open-flavor di-meson scattering in the diabatic representation of BO citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, analyses of exotic heavy hadrons in the diabatic representation of the diquark model citebib.bib11, and calculations of threshold effects in a BO effective field theory citebib.bib12. Nevertheless, there is yet another constraint customarily linked with BO that must be removed in order to carry out precise, ab initio studies of heavy quark-antiquark states: heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS). Current BO calculations of the bottomonium spectrum with string breaking citebib.bib7, bib.bib8 rely on HQSS, since it is realized for lattice QCD simulations with static quarks. However, because of the mass splitting between a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson B𝐵B and its vector partner B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast, HQSS is broken by the mixing of quarkoniumlike resonances with di-meson pairs. In fact, this HQSS-breaking effect has been used to explain the hadronic decays of Υ​(4​S)Υ4𝑆Upsilon(4S) and Υ​Υ10860Upsilon; see, for instance, refs. citebib.bib13, bib.bib14 and references therein. In light of this, one could question the reliability of HQSS-based calculations of coupled quark-antiquark and di-meson channels, especially for energies close to a meson-pair threshold. The situation is even more problematic in the charmoniumlike sector, with a D∗superscript𝐷∗Dast-D𝐷D splitting three times bigger than the B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast-B𝐵B splitting. Phenomenological analyses citebib.bib15 indicate that the precise location of the di-meson thresholds may be decisive for the understanding of exotic states like X​𝑋3872X. The problem of HQSS breaking in the BO approximation has been addressed in the case of u​d​b¯​b¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑏¯𝑏udbarbbarb tetraquarks by including the B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast-B𝐵B mass splitting and solving the resulting Schrödinger equations for the coupled BO channels citebib.bib16. However, the u​d​b¯​b¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑏¯𝑏udbarbbarb tetraquark case is made somewhat simpler by the absence of any string breaking, hence the formalism described in ref. citebib.bib16 is not easily extended to other situations where string breaking plays a prominent role. In this work, we show that HQSS breaking can be systematically and consistently included in the diabatic representation of the BO approximation. The effect of HQSS breaking is to split the thresholds and replace the single string-breaking transition rate from lattice QCD by a matrix of transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations, with coefficients determined from Dirac algebra. The quark-antiquark static energy, di-meson thresholds, and the transition rates form an interaction matrix that depends on the spin configuration of the heavy sources. Linear combinations of the interaction matrix elements for different spin configurations, with coefficients determined from angular momentum theory, form the entries of a potential matrix from which the spectrum of the heavy quark-antiquark system can be calculated. As a byproduct, we verify that the cylindrical symmetry group of a static quark-antiquark pair, D∞​h⊗C​Ptensor-productsubscript𝐷ℎ𝐶𝑃Dinfty hotimes CP, gets naturally promoted to the full O​⊗Ctensor-product𝑂3𝐶Ootimes C symmetry group of QCD when the kinetic energies of the heavy quarks are introduced. This result constitutes a substantial amendment to the previous phenomenological analyses in refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib10 by determining the matrix of coupling coefficients that in these studies was arbitrarily fixed ad hoc. It provides a foothold for a detailed, unified study of heavy quark-antiquark systems from ab initio lattice QCD simulations with static quarks. The contents of this paper are organized as follows. In section, we thoroughly review the static approximation for a heavy quark-antiquark pair. In section, we calculate the transition rates between quark-antiquark and the various di-meson configurations in terms of the string-breaking transition rate. In section, we include violation of HQSS by threshold spin splittings. In section, we introduce the kinetic energies of the heavy quarks in the diabatic representation of BO and write down the master Schrödinger equation with quark-antiquark and di-meson channels. Finally, in section, we summarize our findings and indicate possible future developments.
content_sections.2 Static approximation: For the system of a heavy quark-antiquark pair with strong interactions, the time scale for the motion of the heavy quarks, dictated by the heavy-quark mass mQsubscript𝑚𝑄mQ, is considerably larger than the time scale for the evolution of the gluon and light-quark fields, dictated by the nonperturbative QCD energy scale ΛQCDsubscriptΛQCDLambdatextupQCD. Because of this sharp separation of the time scales involved, it makes sense to expand the Hamiltonian of the heavy quark-antiquark system in powers of ΛQCD/mQsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑄LambdatextupQCD/mQ. The leading order contribution in this expansion corresponds to the infinite mass limit for the heavy quarks, mQ→∞→subscript𝑚𝑄mQtoinfty. In this limit, the heavy quark-antiquark pair has no motion and acts as a static source for the light QCD fields. The energy levels of the dynamical fields, that is, gluons and light quarks, are not affected by the spins of the static quarks. This decoupling of the heavy-quark spins is referred to as HQSS. Hence, let us begin by considering QCD with static quark (Q𝑄Q) and antiquark (Q¯¯𝑄barQ) sources at the positions +12​𝒓12𝒓+frac12r and −12​𝒓12𝒓-frac12r. In this static approximation, 𝒓𝒓r is a constant parameter. The dynamical fields are gluons and light quarks (q𝑞q) and antiquarks (q¯¯𝑞barq). The relevant symmetry group of QCD is O​⊗Ctensor-product𝑂3𝐶Ootimes C, where O​𝑂3O is the group formed by three-dimensional rotations and the parity transformation P𝑃P, and C𝐶C is the charge conjugation. However, the static quarks break the symmetry down to the cylindrical subgroup D∞​h⊗C​Ptensor-productsubscript𝐷ℎ𝐶𝑃Dinfty hotimes CP, which consists of: • rotations around the 𝒓bold-𝒓hatr axis; • a reflection ℛℛR through a plane containing 𝒓𝒓r; • the combined transformation C​P𝐶𝑃CP. There are several terms in the total angular momentum 𝑱𝑱J of a heavy quark-antiquark system. We denote the light Q​C​D𝑄𝐶𝐷QCD angular momentum of the dynamical fields by 𝑱lightsubscript𝑱lightJtextuplight. The total spin of the heavy quarks is 𝑺Q​Q¯subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄SQbarQ. The orbital angular momentum of the sources is 𝑳𝑳L. The total angular momentum is then 𝑱=𝑳+𝑺Q​Q¯+𝑱light. 𝑱𝑳subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄subscript𝑱lightJ=L+SQbarQ+Jtextuplight. It is also convenient to introduce the static spin defined by 𝑺=𝑺Q​Q¯+𝑱light. 𝑺subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄subscript𝑱lightS=SQbarQ+Jtextuplight. In the case λ=0𝜆0lambda=0, the value of ϵitalic-ϵepsilon is completely determined by the static spin s𝑠s, the intrinsic parity 𝔭𝔭mathfrakp of the source, and the light BO quantum numbers Jlightsubscript𝐽lightJtextuplight and ϵlightsubscriptitalic-ϵlightepsilontextuplight as ϵ=ϵlight​𝔭​(−1)Jlight−sitalic-ϵsubscriptitalic-ϵlight𝔭superscript1subscript𝐽light𝑠epsilon=epsilontextuplightmathfrakp(-1)Jtextuplight-s (see appendix), making this quantum number redundant. Thus, we label the configurations by the static spin projection λ𝜆lambda with sign and η𝜂eta, omitting ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. We shall denote these static BO quantum numbers that also take into account the spin of the heavy quarks by ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta, such as 0gsubscript0𝑔0g, 0usubscript0𝑢0u, ±1gplus-or-minussubscript1𝑔pm 1g, ±1uplus-or-minussubscript1𝑢pm 1u, ±2gplus-or-minussubscript2𝑔pm 2g, ±2uplus-or-minussubscript2𝑢pm 2u, and so on. In this paper, we shall concentrate on light QCD configurations with BO quantum numbers Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+, which can be produced by a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source and by di-meson sources. For a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source, that is, the product of Q𝑄Q and Q¯¯𝑄barQ fields connected by a gauge transporter, Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ corresponds to Jlight=0subscript𝐽light0Jtextuplight=0, so that 𝑺=𝑺Q​Q¯𝑺subscript𝑺𝑄¯𝑄S=SQbarQ. For a di-meson source, that is, a product of Q​q¯𝑄¯𝑞Qbarq and q​Q¯𝑞¯𝑄qbarQ operators, 𝑺𝑺S coincides with the total spin of the di-meson pair. So, for Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and di-meson sources producing light Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ configurations, s𝑠s and λ𝜆lambda are just total spin quantum numbers. Note, however, that for a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source the individual spin quantum numbers are 1212frac12, while for a di-meson source the individual spin quantum numbers are integers. In theory, a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source generates all Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ light QCD configurations, labeled by n=1,2,3,…𝑛123…n=1,2,3,dots in increasing energy levels. However, at any given Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ distance r=|𝒓|𝑟𝒓r=lvertrrvert, many of these light QCD configurations will have a very small overlap with the quark-antiquark source, making it difficult to detect them in lattice QCD simulations with finite numerical precision. A practical workaround is to consider the correlation between quark-antiquark and di-meson sources. Following the calculations of the Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ BO potentials with string breaking in lattice QCD citebib.bib5, we truncate the operator expansion by considering only di-meson sources for negative-parity mesons, which yields a good description of the ground n=1𝑛1n=1 and the first excited n=2𝑛2n=2 Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ BO configurations.1In the case of string breaking by light and strange quarks, one can get a good description of the first three configurations by including also di-meson sources for strange negative-parity mesons citebib.bib6. Here we do not consider di-meson sources for positive-parity mesons, which are presumably required for the calculation of excited Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ light QCD configurations with larger n𝑛n. Neither do we include configurations with light BO quantum numbers ΠusubscriptΠ𝑢Piu, Σu−superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑢Sigmau-, etc. , also referred to as hybrid configurations. Hence, the current analysis is restricted to energies well below the lowest threshold for the production of a positive-parity meson (B​B¯1𝐵subscript¯𝐵1BbarB1) and the energy of the lowest quarkonium hybrid meson. content_sections.3 String breaking and transition rates: In lattice QCD, string breaking is manifested explicitly as a nonvanishing transition rate between states created by quark-antiquark and di-meson sources. Lattice QCD has been used to calculate the transition rate between Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ states created by Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB sources in a 0gsubscript0𝑔0g static BO configuration citebib.bib5. In this section, we express the transition rates between Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) sources for different ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta in terms of the string-breaking transition rate g𝑔g between a gluonic string with light BO quantum numbers Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ and a light quark-antiquark pair within the same configuration. Any di-meson operator can be factorized into products of light-quark operators and heavy-quark operators using the Fierz identity, q​q¯=−14​[(q¯​q)+(q¯​γμ​q)​γμ+12​(q¯​σμ​ν​q)​σμ​ν−(q¯​γμ​γ5​q)​γμ​γ5+(q¯​γ5​q)​γ5]𝑞¯𝑞14delimited-¯𝑞𝑞¯𝑞superscript𝛾𝜇𝑞subscript𝛾𝜇12¯𝑞superscript𝜎𝜇𝜈𝑞subscript𝜎𝜇𝜈¯𝑞superscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾5𝑞subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾5¯𝑞subscript𝛾5𝑞subscript𝛾5qbarq=-frac14biggl[(barqq)+(barqgammamuq)gammamu+frac12(barqsigmamunuq)sigmamunu-(barqgammamugamma5q)gammamugamma5+(barqgamma5q)gamma5biggr] where the overall minus sign comes from the anticommutation of fermionic operators. Specifically, the di-meson sources in eqs. - that create static BO configurations 0gsubscript0𝑔0g become ℬ0,0=subscriptℬ00absentdisplaystyleB0,0= 12[(q¯P−γ+q)(Q¯γ−Q)+(q¯P−γ−q)(Q¯γ+Q)−(q¯P−γ3q)(Q¯γ3Q)displaystylefrac12bigl[(barqP-gamma+q)(barQgamma-Q)+(barqP-gamma-q)(barQgamma+Q)-(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgamma3Q) −(q¯P−γ5q)(Q¯γ5Q)],displaystylehphantomfrac12bigl[-(barqP-gamma5q)(barQgamma5Q)bigr], (4a) ℬ1,0∗g=subscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent𝑔10absentdisplaystyleBast g1,0= 12​[(q¯​P−​γ+​q)​(Q¯​γ−​Q)−(q¯​P−​γ−​q)​(Q¯​γ+​Q)],12delimited-¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾𝑄¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾𝑄displaystylefrac1sqrt2bigl[(barqP-gamma+q)(barQgamma-Q)-(barqP-gamma-q)(barQgamma+Q)bigr], (4b) ℬ0,0∗∗=subscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent∗00absentdisplaystyleBastast0,0= 12​3[(q¯P−γ+q)(Q¯γ−Q)+(q¯P−γ−q)(Q¯γ+Q)−(q¯P−γ3q)(Q¯γ3Q)displaystylefrac12sqrt3bigl[(barqP-gamma+q)(barQgamma-Q)+(barqP-gamma-q)(barQgamma+Q)-(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgamma3Q) +3(q¯P−γ5q)(Q¯γ5Q)],displaystylehphantomfrac12sqrt3bigl[+3(barqP-gamma5q)(barQgamma5Q)bigr], (4c) ℬ2,0∗∗=subscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent∗20absentdisplaystyleBastast2,0= −16​[(q¯​P−​γ+​q)​(Q¯​γ−​Q)+(q¯​P−​γ−​q)​(Q¯​γ+​Q)+2​(q¯​P−​γ3​q)​(Q¯​γ3​Q)].16delimited-¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾𝑄¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾𝑄2¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄displaystyle-frac1sqrt6bigl[(barqP-gamma+q)(barQgamma-Q)+(barqP-gamma-q)(barQgamma+Q)+2(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgamma3Q)bigr]. (4d) HQSS implies that the Q𝑄Q and Q¯¯𝑄barQ spins are conserved. The 4 operators Q¯​γ3​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄barQgamma3Q, Q¯​γ+​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾𝑄barQgamma+Q, Q¯​γ−​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾𝑄barQgamma-Q, and Q¯​γ5​Q¯𝑄subscript𝛾5𝑄barQgamma5Q in eqs. correspond to 4 independent Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ spin states. In the quark-antiquark source 𝒬1,0subscript𝒬10Q1,0 in eq. , the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ spin state is given by the operator Q¯​γ3​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄barQgamma3Q. Hence, the static correlation function between 𝒬1,0subscript𝒬10Q1,0 and any of the di-meson sources in eqs. proceeds exclusively through the terms in the latter that are proportional to Q¯​γ3​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄barQgamma3Q, ⟨0|ℬs,0(∗∗)​𝒯τ​𝒬1,0†|0⟩∝⟨0|(q¯​P−​γ3​q)​(Q¯​γ3​Q)​𝒯τ​(Q¯​γ3​𝒲​Q)†|0⟩proportional-toquantum-operator-product0superscriptsubscriptℬ𝑠0∗absent∗subscript𝒯𝜏superscriptsubscript𝒬10†0quantum-operator-product0¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄subscript𝒯𝜏superscript¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝒲𝑄†0braket0Bs,0(astast)TtauQ1,0dagger0proptobraket0(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgamma3Q)Ttau(barQgamma3WQ)dagger0 where ℬs,0(∗∗)superscriptsubscriptℬ𝑠0∗absent∗Bs,0(astast) is a shorthand for any of the di-meson sources in eqs. and 𝒯τsubscript𝒯𝜏Ttau is the Euclidean time-evolution operator from time 00 to τ𝜏tau. Factoring out the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ spin given by Q¯​γ3​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄barQgamma3Q, the correlator on the right side of eq. can be cast symbolically as ⟨0|(q¯​P−​γ3​q)​𝒯t​𝒲†|0⟩quantum-operator-product0¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞subscript𝒯𝑡superscript𝒲†0braket0(barqP-gamma3q)TtWdagger0. From it, the string-breaking transition rate g𝑔g can be obtained by evaluating the time derivative with an appropriate normalization coefficient after some relaxation time for which higher excited light QCD configurations have decayed; see ref. citebib.bib5. Thus, it follows from eq. that the transition rates between Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) are all proportional to the string-breaking transition rate g𝑔g. For the 0gsubscript0𝑔0g configuration, the coefficients are simply the numerical factors multiplying Q¯​γ3​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄barQgamma3Q inside eqs. . The transition rates then read GQ​Q¯​,B​B¯​g,0​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄1𝐵¯𝐵0𝑔0𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BbarBg,0(r) =−12​g​(r),absent12𝑔𝑟displaystyle=-frac12g(r), (6a) GQ​Q¯​,B​B¯∗​g,0​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄1𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗1𝑔0𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BbarBastg,0(r) =0,absent0displaystyle=0, (6b) GQ​Q¯​,B∗​B¯∗​g,0​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄1superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗0𝑔0𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BastbarBastg,0(r) =−12​3​g​(r),absent123𝑔𝑟displaystyle=-frac12sqrt3g(r), (6c) GQ​Q¯​,B∗​B¯∗​g,0​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄1superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗2𝑔0𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BastbarBastg,0(r) =−23​g​(r),absent23𝑔𝑟displaystyle=-sqrtfrac23g(r), (6d) where we have specified the static BO quantum numbers η𝜂eta and λ𝜆lambda of the configuration as superscripts, the total spin of the quark-antiquark and di-meson sources as Q​Q¯​(s)𝑄¯𝑄𝑠QbarQ(s) and B(∗)​B¯(∗)​(s)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗𝑠B(ast)barB(ast)(s), respectively, and the distance r𝑟r between the sources as an argument. One can proceed in the exact same manner for the ±1gplus-or-minussubscript1𝑔pm 1g and 0usubscript0𝑢0u configurations, by considering the corresponding quark-antiquark and di-meson sources. For the ±1gplus-or-minussubscript1𝑔pm 1g configurations, the di-meson sources in eqs. - become ℬ1,±1∗gsubscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent𝑔1plus-or-minus1displaystyleBast g1,pm 1 =±12​[(q¯​P−​γ±​q)​(Q¯​γ3​Q)−(q¯​P−​γ3​q)​(Q¯​γ±​Q)],absentplus-or-minus12delimited-¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾plus-or-minus𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾plus-or-minus𝑄displaystyle=pmfrac1sqrt2bigl[(barqP-gammapmq)(barQgamma3Q)-(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgammapmQ)bigr], (7a) ℬ2,±1∗∗subscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent∗2plus-or-minus1displaystyleBastast2,pm 1 =−12​[(q¯​P−​γ±​q)​(Q¯​γ3​Q)+(q¯​P−​γ3​q)​(Q¯​γ±​Q)]. absent12delimited-¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾plus-or-minus𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾plus-or-minus𝑄displaystyle=-frac1sqrt2bigl[(barqP-gammapmq)(barQgamma3Q)+(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgammapmQ)bigr]. (7b) Since the spin states of the heavy quark-antiquark pair in 𝒬1,±1subscript𝒬1plus-or-minus1Q1,pm 1 in eq. are given by Q¯​γ±​Q¯𝑄superscript𝛾plus-or-minus𝑄barQgammapmQ, the transition rates are GQ​Q¯​,B​B¯∗​g,±1​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄1𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗1𝑔plus-or-minus1𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BbarBastg,pm 1(r) =∓12​g​(r),absentminus-or-plus12𝑔𝑟displaystyle=mpfrac1sqrt2g(r), (8a) GQ​Q¯​,B∗​B¯∗​g,±1​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄1superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗2𝑔plus-or-minus1𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BastbarBastg,pm 1(r) =−12​g​(r). absent12𝑔𝑟displaystyle=-frac1sqrt2g(r). (8b) For the 0usubscript0𝑢0u configuration, the di-meson sources in eqs. - become ℬ1,0∗usubscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent𝑢10displaystyleBast u1,0 =−12​[(q¯​P−​γ5​q)​(Q¯​γ3​Q)+(q¯​P−​γ3​q)​(Q¯​γ5​Q)],absent12delimited-¯𝑞subscript𝑃subscript𝛾5𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞¯𝑄subscript𝛾5𝑄displaystyle=-frac1sqrt2bigl[(barqP-gamma5q)(barQgamma3Q)+(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgamma5Q)bigr], (9a) ℬ1,0∗∗subscriptsuperscriptℬ∗absent∗10displaystyleBastast1,0 =12​[(q¯​P−​γ5​q)​(Q¯​γ3​Q)−(q¯​P−​γ3​q)​(Q¯​γ5​Q)]. absent12delimited-¯𝑞subscript𝑃subscript𝛾5𝑞¯𝑄superscript𝛾3𝑄¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞¯𝑄subscript𝛾5𝑄displaystyle=frac1sqrt2bigl[(barqP-gamma5q)(barQgamma3Q)-(barqP-gamma3q)(barQgamma5Q)bigr]. (9b) Since the spin state of the heavy quark-antiquark pair in 𝒬0,0subscript𝒬00Q0,0 in eq. is given by Q¯​γ5​Q¯𝑄subscript𝛾5𝑄barQgamma5Q, the transition rates are GQ​Q¯​,B​B¯∗​u,0​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄0𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗1𝑢0𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BbarBastu,0(r) =−12​g​(r),absent12𝑔𝑟displaystyle=-frac1sqrt2g(r), (10a) GQ​Q¯​,B∗​B¯∗​u,0​(r)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑄¯𝑄0superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗1𝑢0𝑟displaystyle GQbarQ,BastbarBastu,0(r) =−12​g​(r). absent12𝑔𝑟displaystyle=-frac1sqrt2g(r). (10b) As explained in more detail in the next section, one can use eqs. - to verify that, for all four ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta configurations, a unitary change of basis reduces the interaction matrix between Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) down to a 2×2222times 2 string-breaking interaction sub-matrix 𝑮​(r)𝑮𝑟G(r) between 𝒲𝒲W and q¯​P−​γ3​q¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾3𝑞barqP-gamma3q, plus rows and columns associated to decoupled q¯​P−​γ±​q¯𝑞subscript𝑃superscript𝛾plus-or-minus𝑞barqP-gammapmq and q¯​P−​γ5​q¯𝑞subscript𝑃subscript𝛾5𝑞barqP-gamma5q components. Otherwise said, the relevant string-breaking interaction matrix is the same independently of the spins of the heavy quarks, consistently with HQSS. The 2×2222times 2 string-breaking interaction matrix reads 𝑮​(r)=(V𝒬​(r)g​(r)g​(r)0),𝑮𝑟matrixsubscript𝑉𝒬𝑟𝑔𝑟𝑔𝑟0G(r)=beginpmatrixVQ(r)g(r) g(r)0 endpmatrix, where V𝒬​(r)subscript𝑉𝒬𝑟VQ(r) is the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ static energy relative to twice the static B𝐵B meson mass. Notice that the zero in the lower right corner of eq. corresponds to approximating the B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) static energy by its constant value at large r𝑟r. The constant can be set to zero by taking the zero of the energy to be the static meson pair threshold. This ansatz provides a good fit to the lattice QCD data in citebib.bib5; see, for instance, ref. citebib.bib7. It is worth mentioning that the results presented here are straightforwardly extended to the case of dynamical quark fields with Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓Nf flavors and S​U​(Nf)𝑆𝑈subscript𝑁𝑓SU(Nf) flavor symmetry. The only notable difference is that the string-breaking transition rate with Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓Nf light flavors is equal to the single-flavor one times a factor Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓sqrtNf. If, alternatively, both light and strange quarks are considered citebib.bib6, then one needs to substitute eq. with an appropriate 3×3333times 3 string-breaking interaction matrix, like 𝑮​(r)=(V𝒬​(r)g​(r)gs​(r)g​(r)00gs​(r)02​δs)𝑮𝑟matrixsubscript𝑉𝒬𝑟𝑔𝑟subscript𝑔𝑠𝑟𝑔𝑟00subscript𝑔𝑠𝑟02subscript𝛿𝑠G(r)=beginpmatrixVQ(r)g(r)gs(r) g(r)00 gs(r)02deltas endpmatrix where gs​(r)subscript𝑔𝑠𝑟gs(r) is the string-breaking transition rate by strange quarks and δs=mBs−mBsubscript𝛿𝑠subscript𝑚subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑚𝐵deltas=mBs-mB is the mass difference between static Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs and B𝐵B mesons. Notice that we have assumed that the B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) and Bs(∗)​B¯s(∗)superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠∗Bs(ast)barBs(ast) static energies are well-approximated by their constant values at large r𝑟r. We have also assumed that the transition rate between B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) and Bs(∗)​B¯s(∗)superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠∗Bs(ast)barBs(ast) sources is negligible, since it requires the creation of s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars which is OZI suppressed. The ansatz in eq. with g​(r)𝑔𝑟g(r) and gs​(r)subscript𝑔𝑠𝑟gs(r) replaced by constants is identical to the parametrization used in ref. citebib.bib6 for fitting the lattice QCD interaction matrix with 2+1212+1 light-quark flavors.
content_sections.4 Threshold spin splittings: To go beyond the static approximation, one has to introduce corrections in increasing powers of 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ that typically depend on the heavy-quark momenta and/or spins. We shall limit ourselves to the first-order corrections, that is, the nonrelativistic kinetic energies of the heavy quarks and heavy-quark spin effects of first order in 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ. In this section we shall focus on the latter, leaving the treatment of the kinetic energy for later. At first order in 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ, the heavy-quark spin corrections to the static approximation consist of couplings between the angular momentum of the light QCD fields and the spins of the heavy quarks citebib.bib17. For the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ configurations with light BO quantum numbers Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ (Jlight=0subscript𝐽light0Jtextuplight=0), these 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ couplings vanish. For the B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) configurations, one can apply heavy-quark effective theory to calculate these 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ effects for each individual heavy-light meson, which are responsible for the B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast-B𝐵B mass splitting citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21. As for the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ-B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) transition rates, we shall assume that they are reasonably approximated by their HQSS expressions in eqs. , , and at first order in 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ. Therefore, for the current study, taking into account heavy-quark spin corrections of first order in 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ boils down to splitting the B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB, B​B¯∗η𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗absent𝜂BbarBasteta, and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗BastbarBast threshold masses. content_sections.5 Diabatic Born-Oppenheimer approximatio: The other first-order correction in 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ to the static approximation is the nonrelativistic kinetic energy for the relative motion of the heavy quark-antiquark pair, K=p2/mQ. 𝐾superscript𝑝2subscript𝑚𝑄K=p2/mQ. The introduction of the kinetic energy operator gives motion to the heavy sources, which can then be determined by solving a coupled-channel Schrödinger equation. This is the core idea of the BO approximation. In this section, we shall detail the formal derivation of this master Schrödinger equation in the the diabatic representation of BO, which allows one to study heavy quark-antiquark systems using the interaction matrix from ab initio lattice QCD. As we shall see next, one cannot naively plug the lattice-QCD interaction matrix in eq. or one of its corrected forms in eqs. - as a potential inside a coupled-channel Schrödinger equation. Instead, what governs the motion of the heavy quarks is the diabatic potential matrix, which is constructed from a combination of the interaction matrices corresponding to different ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta configurations. Formally, the interaction matrix from lattice QCD can be embedded in a quantum-mechanical framework using the following procedure. Let us indicate the interaction matrix with quark-antiquark distance r𝑟r within the static BO configuration ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta as 𝑮η,λ​(r)superscript𝑮𝜂𝜆𝑟Geta,lambda(r). Let Vnη,λ​(r)superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑛𝜂𝜆𝑟Vneta,lambda(r) be the n𝑛nth eigenvalue of 𝑮η,λ​(r)superscript𝑮𝜂𝜆𝑟Geta,lambda(r), that is, the n𝑛nth static energy level with static BO quantum numbers ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta, and |ζnη,λ​(𝒓)⟩ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛𝜂𝜆𝒓ketzetaneta,lambda(r) the corresponding static eigenstate. One can then construct a first-quantized operator, Hstatic​(𝒓)=∑η,λ,n|ζnη,λ​(𝒓)⟩​Vnη,λ​(r)​⟨ζnη,λ​(𝒓)|,subscript𝐻static𝒓subscript𝜂𝜆𝑛ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛𝜂𝜆𝒓superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑛𝜂𝜆𝑟brasuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛𝜂𝜆𝒓Htextupstatic(r)=sumeta,lambda,nketzetaneta,lambda(r)Vneta,lambda(r)brazetaneta,lambda(r), acting as a Hamiltonian for the light QCD fields in presence of a heavy quark-antiquark pair at relative position 𝒓𝒓r. From it, the BO Hamiltonian is constructed as HBO=K+Hstatic,subscript𝐻BO𝐾subscript𝐻staticHtextupBO=K+Htextupstatic, so that a quantum state |Ψ⟩ketΨketPsi of the heavy quark-antiquark system corresponds to a solution of the eigenvalue equation HBO​|Ψ⟩=E​|Ψ⟩subscript𝐻BOketΨ𝐸ketΨHtextupBOketPsi=EketPsi with E𝐸E the energy relative to the spin-average B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) threshold. Notice that, whilst the static energy levels Vnη,λ​(r)superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑛𝜂𝜆𝑟Vneta,lambda(r) depend on the distance r=|𝒓|𝑟𝒓r=lvertrrvert alone, the static eigenstates |ζnη,λ​(𝒓)⟩ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛𝜂𝜆𝒓ketzetaneta,lambda(r) depend on the direction 𝒓=𝒓/rbold-𝒓𝒓𝑟hatr=r/r as well. Most importantly, λ𝜆lambda itself depends on 𝒓bold-𝒓hatr through its definition λ=𝑺⋅𝒓𝜆⋅𝑺bold-𝒓lambda=Scdothatr. In this case one cannot simply identify the fixed axis 𝒛bold-𝒛hatz with 𝒓bold-𝒓hatr, since 𝒓𝒓r is a dynamical variable. Translating eq. into a differential equation in 𝒓𝒓r requires an appropriate expansion of the heavy quark-antiquark state |Ψ⟩ketΨketPsi in terms of some basis for the light QCD fields. One natural choice is the basis formed by the static eigenstates |ζnη,λ​(𝒓)⟩ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛𝜂𝜆𝒓ketzetaneta,lambda(r), called adiabatic basis, since it diagonalizes the light QCD energy operator in eq. . Another convenient choice is a basis that diagonalizes the kinetic energy operator in eq. , called diabatic basis citebib.bib22, bib.bib23. In general, it is possible to express each element of some diabatic basis as a linear combination of the static eigenstates, |ζiη,σ​(𝒓,𝒓0)⟩=∑λ,nAn,iλ,σ​(𝒓,𝒓0)​|ζnη,λ​(𝒓)⟩ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑖𝜂𝜎𝒓subscript𝒓0subscript𝜆𝑛superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑛𝑖𝜆𝜎𝒓subscript𝒓0ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛𝜂𝜆𝒓ketzetaieta,sigma(r,r0)=sumlambda,nAn,ilambda,sigma(r,r0)ketzetaneta,lambda(r) where the coefficients An,iλ,σ​(𝒓,𝒓0)superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑛𝑖𝜆𝜎𝒓subscript𝒓0An,ilambda,sigma(r,r0) form a unitary matrix 𝑨​(𝒓,𝒓0)𝑨𝒓subscript𝒓0A(r,r0) which can be obtained by solving a first-order differential equation with boundary conditions at 𝒓=𝒓0𝒓subscript𝒓0r=r0; see, for instance, refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib24. A conventional choice for the boundary conditions is to match the diabatic basis to the adiabatic one at 𝒓=𝒓0𝒓subscript𝒓0r=r0, |ζiη,σ​(𝒓=𝒓0,𝒓0)⟩=∑λ,nδλ,σ​δn,i​|ζnη,λ​(𝒓=𝒓0)⟩,ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑖𝜂𝜎𝒓subscript𝒓0subscript𝒓0subscript𝜆𝑛subscript𝛿𝜆𝜎subscript𝛿𝑛𝑖ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛𝜂𝜆𝒓subscript𝒓0ketzetaieta,sigma(r=r0,r0)=sumlambda,ndeltalambda,sigmadeltan,iketzetaneta,lambda(r=r0), which is the same as imposing 𝑨​(𝒓=𝒓0,𝒓0)=𝑰𝑨𝒓subscript𝒓0subscript𝒓0𝑰A(r=r0,r0)=I with 𝑰𝑰I for the identity matrix. It is important to realize that for 𝒓=𝒓0bold-𝒓subscriptbold-𝒓0hatr=hatr0 the cylindrical symmetry of BO implies An,iλ,σ​(𝒓=r​𝒓0,𝒓0)=δλ,σ​An,i​(r,r0),superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑛𝑖𝜆𝜎𝒓𝑟subscriptbold-𝒓0subscript𝒓0subscript𝛿𝜆𝜎subscript𝐴𝑛𝑖𝑟subscript𝑟0An,ilambda,sigma(r=rhatr0,r0)=deltalambda,sigmaAn,i(r,r0), where r0=|𝒓0|r0=lvertr0lvert and An,i​(r,r0)subscript𝐴𝑛𝑖𝑟subscript𝑟0An,i(r,r0) are the coefficients of a radial unitary matrix 𝑨​(r,r0)𝑨𝑟subscript𝑟0A(r,r0) which can be obtained by solving a first-order differential equation in r𝑟r with boundary conditions 𝑨​(r=r0,r0)=𝑰𝑨𝑟subscript𝑟0subscript𝑟0𝑰A(r=r0,r0)=I. Notice that the right side of eq. does not depend on 𝒓0subscriptbold-𝒓0hatr0. Different choices of the matching point 𝒓0subscript𝒓0r0 with the adiabatic basis yield different but equivalent choices of diabatic basis. For the present study, it is most convenient to fix 𝒓0=0subscript𝒓00r0=0. The reason is that the diabatic basis in this case is matched to the light QCD eigenstates calculated in the static limit where the two sources are placed at the same point 𝒓=0𝒓0r=0. Unlike the general case, where a nonvanishing separation vector 𝒓𝒓r sets a preferred direction in space, the static limit with 𝒓=0𝒓0r=0 is symmetric under O​⊗Ctensor-product𝑂3𝐶Ootimes C. So, the static eigenstates at 𝒓=0𝒓0r=0 can be classified by the static spin quantum number s𝑠s and by the JlightP​Csubscriptsuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶lightJPCtextuplight quantum numbers of the light QCD fields, in addition to the static BO quantum numbers ληsubscript𝜆𝜂lambdaeta. As mentioned in section, we restrict our analysis to the ground and first-excited Σg+superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑔Sigmag+ light BO configurations generated by a Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ source and by di-meson sources for negative-parity mesons. The Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ operators for 𝒓=0𝒓0r=0 generate JlightP​C=0light++subscriptsuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶lightsubscriptsuperscript0absentlightJPCtextuplight=0++textuplight; see eqs. , , and . On the other hand, the B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) operators for 𝒓=0𝒓0r=0 generate JlightP​C=1light−−subscriptsuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶lightsubscriptsuperscript1absentlightJPCtextuplight=1--textuplight and 0light−+subscriptsuperscript0absentlight0-+textuplight; see eqs. , , and . The weaker cylindrical symmetry for 𝒓≠0𝒓0rneq 0 allows the 0light++subscriptsuperscript0absentlight0++textuplight component of the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ configurations to mix with the 1light−−subscriptsuperscript1absentlight1--textuplight (Λ=0Λ0Lambda=0) component of the B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) configurations through string breaking; see section. However, such mixing is forbidden by the stronger O​⊗Ctensor-product𝑂3𝐶Ootimes C symmetry for 𝒓=0𝒓0r=0.2From this argument, it follows that the string-breaking transition rate g​(r)𝑔𝑟g(r) must go to zero as r→0→𝑟0rto 0. Paradoxically, the absence of mixing for 𝒓=0𝒓0r=0 is what makes 𝒓0=0subscript𝒓00r0=0 the ideal choice of diabatic basis for studying string breaking. In fact, this choice ensures that the mixing problem is clearly organized in terms of channels with well-defined particle number and spin. Hence, we work with a truncated diabatic basis which consists of 4 Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and 16 B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) channels labeled by their total spin s𝑠s and the diabatic BO quantum numbers σηsubscript𝜎𝜂sigmaeta. This truncated diabatic basis can be formally written as a set |ζiη,σ​(𝒓,0)⟩ketsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑖𝜂𝜎𝒓0ketzetaieta,sigma(r,0) labeled by σηsubscript𝜎𝜂sigmaeta and the additional label i𝑖i that specifies the quark-antiquark or di-meson channels with total spin s𝑠s as Q​Q¯​(s)𝑄¯𝑄𝑠QbarQ(s) or B(∗)​B¯(∗)​(s)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗𝑠B(ast)barB(ast)(s). As a last remark, we illustrate how the diabatic BO approximation can be used in practical calculations of the quarkoniumlike spectrum. Since J𝐽J, P𝑃P, and C𝐶C are all conserved quantum numbers, one can concentrate on a specific JP​Csuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶JPC configuration and consider only the corresponding orbital angular momentum channels when calculating the spectrum. From eq. , one sees that the BO Hamiltonian in a particular JP​Csuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶JPC configuration can be represented as a matrix 𝑯JP​Csuperscript𝑯superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶HJPC acting on the reduced radial wave functions.
