It is clear from eqs. , , and that, in the limit Δ→0→Δ0Deltato 0, the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ S𝑆S-wave (l=0𝑙0l=0) and D𝐷D-wave (l=2𝑙2l=2) channels in the JP​C=1−−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1-- configuration are completely decoupled from each other. This also shows that, in this HQSS limit, the spectrum of quarkoniumlike states with Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ in S𝑆S-wave is the same regardless of whether the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ spin is 00 or 111 (JP​C=0−+superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentJPC=0-+ or 1−−superscript1absent1--), as expected. These results should be compared with the BO calculation using lattice-QCD BO potentials with string breaking in ref. citebib.bib7, where the spectrum of ΥΥUpsilon and ηbsubscript𝜂𝑏etab states is also calculated from a coupled-channel radial Schrödinger equation for quark-antiquark and di-meson channels. One can notice that the radial potential matrix of this reference, which may be easily read from the left side of eq. in ref. citebib.bib7, is identical to our 𝑽HQSS0−+​(r)superscriptsubscript𝑽HQSSsuperscript0absent𝑟VtextupHQSS0-+(r) in eq. by substituting mQ→2​μM→subscript𝑚𝑄2subscript𝜇𝑀mQto 2muM, V𝒬​(r)→VQ¯​Q​(r)→subscript𝑉𝒬𝑟subscript𝑉¯𝑄𝑄𝑟VQ(r)to VbarQQ(r), g​(r)→Vmix​(r)→𝑔𝑟subscript𝑉mix𝑟g(r)to Vtextupmix(r), and noticing that VM¯M,∥​(r)VbarMM,parallel(r) in eq. of ref. citebib.bib7 is actually 00 (see eq. in ref. citebib.bib7). Therefore, our results reproduce the Schrödinger equation used in ref. citebib.bib7 in the HQSS limit Δ→0→Δ0Deltato 0. When threshold spin splittings Δ>0Δ0Delta>0 are included, however, we observe breaking of HQSS through the difference between the radial potential matrices in the cases of Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ spin 00 and 111, 𝑽0−+​(r)superscript𝑽superscript0absent𝑟V0-+(r) and 𝑽1−−​(r)superscript𝑽superscript1absent𝑟V1--(r). This HQSS-breaking effect is straightforwardly incorporated into the lattice-QCD potentials with string breaking using our eqs. and for the specific 0−+superscript0absent0-+ and 1−−superscript1absent1-- cases, or eqs. and for any JP​Csuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶JPC in general. The same cannot be said of the results in refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, for which no clear prescription to go beyond the HQSS approximation is provided. Notice that, since the heavy-quark kinetic energy and the B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast-B𝐵B mass splitting are both first-order corrections in 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ to the static approximation, there is no formal justification for including the former without the latter in a BO approximation. From a phenomenological point of view, this HQSS-breaking effect is kinematically suppressed for quarkonium states with masses well below the B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB threshold, where di-meson channels hardly play any role, but it is relevant in all other cases. This is especially important for exotic candidates, whose masses often lie close to some open-flavor meson-pair threshold. For these reasons, the diabatic BO approximation studied here should be regarded as a necessary improvement of the scheme used in refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, which allows one to perform detailed studies for energies close and above threshold.
content_sections.6 Overview: We have revisited the static approximation for a heavy quark-antiquark pair. In addition to the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB sources, we have also included B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵∗BbarBast and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗BastbarBast sources. We have shown that the transition rates between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations are given by fractions of the string-breaking transition rate, with coefficients completely determined from Dirac algebra. We have then introduced the mass splitting between a heavy-light pseudoscalar meson B𝐵B and its vector partner B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast. We have illustrated the pattern of HQSS breaking deriving from string breaking with threshold spin splittings. We have further shown that this symmetry breaking is easily accounted for in the interaction matrix between quark-antiquark and di-meson configurations. Finally, we have built a systematic treatment of the BO approximation at first order in 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ in the diabatic representation. We have shown that the dynamics of the heavy quark-antiquark system, including the heavy quark-antiquark interaction, string breaking, and HQSS breaking, is governed by the diabatic potential matrix, which is completely determined from an interaction matrix accessible in lattice QCD. We have also shown, without any ad hoc assumption, that this construction of the BO approximation leads to a natural restoration of the O​⊗Ctensor-product𝑂3𝐶Ootimes C symmetry of QCD from the cylindrical symmetry D∞​h⊗C​Ptensor-productsubscript𝐷ℎ𝐶𝑃Dinfty hotimes CP of the static approximation. This result provides an essential stepping stone towards precise, first-principles studies of quarkoniumlike mesons and open-flavor di-meson scattering with string breaking. It is worth highlighting that the inclusion of threshold spin splittings is particularly relevant to the understanding of any heavy quark-antiquark state in which meson pairs play a significant role, and to the understanding of near-threshold states like X​𝑋3872X in particular. For the charmoniumlike sector, it may be necessary to consider even the small D+superscript𝐷D+-D0superscript𝐷0D0 and D∗+superscript𝐷∗absentDast+-D∗0superscript𝐷∗absent0Dast 0 mass differences. For instance, they may be instrumental in explaining the isospin-violating branching fractions of X​𝑋3872X citebib.bib26, bib.bib27. Note that such isospin mass splittings can be easily incorporated into the diabatic BO approximation; see ref. citebib.bib15. For practical applications in the future, it may be necessary to include also other relevant effects, like heavy-quark spin corrections to the quark-antiquark potential of order 1/mQ21superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑄21/mQ2 and light-meson exchange interactions between two open-flavor mesons. The former are well known citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30 and can be simply added to the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ partial-wave potentials. As for the latter, we strongly encourage a theoretical effort for their incorporation in the diabatic BO approximation. Acknowledgements. I wish to express my gratitude to E. Braaten for his indispensable help in developing this project. I acknowledge valuable discussions with P. González. This research was supported by the U. S. Department of Energy under Grant No. DE-SC0011726.

all_papers.title: Symmetries, partners and thresholds: the case of the $X_b$
all_papers.abstract:
The discovery of the $X(3872)$ meant the revival of the heavy meson
spectroscopy beyond naive $q\bar q$ structures. Once that the $SU(3)$ scheme,
which was very useful in the dawn of the quark models, does not work for these
states, one has to use new symmetries, like Heavy Quark Spin Symmetry (HQSS)
and Heavy Flavor Symmetry (HFS), to look for new states. However, at the energy
regions where these new states appear, new factors are involved and it is not
straightforward to relate the predictions of the symmetries with the data. In
this work, we present a critical analysis of this problem and show, in a
coupled-channels model, how the relative position of the bare $Q\bar Q$ states
with respect to meson-meson thresholds and the coupling with other channels
modulate the strength of the interaction and, hence, modify the structure of
the predicted states. We found a possible candidate to the $X(3872)$ partner at
$10599$ MeV$/c^2$.

qualitative: Heavy Quark Spin Symmetry (HQSS) is a fundamental symmetry in heavy quark effective theory that emerges in the infinite quark mass limit, where the spin of the heavy quark decouples from the dynamics of the light degrees of freedom.

HQSS implies that the interaction between heavy quarks and light degrees of freedom is independent of the heavy quark's spin, leading to degeneracies in the spectrum of heavy hadrons with different spin configurations.
In the context of exotic states like the X(3872), HQSS helps predict the existence of partner states in the bottom sector (e.g., X_b) by relating charm and bottom meson systems.

HQSS is used to constrain the structure and decay properties of molecular states, especially when combined with Heavy Flavor Symmetry (HFS), to predict the behavior of heavy meson resonances near thresholds.
The symmetry is approximate in reality due to finite quark masses, but it provides a powerful framework for organizing and predicting the spectrum of heavy hadrons beyond naive q\bar{q} states.
quantitative: The X(3872) mass is very close to the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold: MD⁰ + MD*⁰ - MX(3872) = (0.04 ± 0.09) MeV
The predicted X_b partner is expected to lie near the MB + MB* threshold, approximately 10.6 GeV
The X(3872) is a JP^C = 1++ state with hidden charm, and its structure is influenced by coupling to the 2³P₁ cc̄ state via HQSS

The coupled-channels model used in the study incorporates HQSS to describe the interaction between the D⁰D̄*⁰ channel and the 2³P₁ cc̄ state, with the latter providing the necessary attraction for binding

all_papers.reason:
论文核心聚焦于重夸克自旋对称性（HQSS）在$X_b$态研究中的应用，明确讨论HQSS与阈值、耦合通道对态结构的影响，与查询“Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS)”高度契合。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：主题聚焦HQSS — 满足。证据：“We present a critical analysis of this problem and show... how the relative position... and the coupling with other channels modulate the strength of the interaction” 对比 “Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS)”  
- C2：研究对象为含重夸克的奇特态 — 满足。证据：“possible candidate to the $X(3872)$ partner at 10599 MeV/c²” 对比 “HQSS”在重夸克系统中的应用  
- C3：使用耦合通道模型分析对称性效应 — 满足。证据：“in a coupled-channels model” 对比 HQSS在非$ q\bar q $态中的作用  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文完全覆盖查询核心，无偏离。
authors: Pablo G. Ortega, David R. Entem, Francisco Fernandez

content_sections.I Introduction: History taught us the importance of symmetries in hadron spectroscopy. In fact, the prediction of the existence of the Ω−superscriptΩOmega- was the first success of the S​U​F𝑆𝑈subscript3𝐹SUF symmetry and its application allowed to configure a complete hadron map based on the quark model. The existence of non-conventional quark structures which do not fit in the quark scheme based in the S​U​F𝑆𝑈subscript3𝐹SUF symmetry is not new, but rolls back to the same origins of the quark modelcitebib.bib1. Although early studies suggested the possible existence of meson-meson molecular states in the charmonium spectrumcitebib.bib2, it was not until 2003, with the observation of the X​𝑋3872X, when the concept of meson-meson molecule regained attentioncitebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5. The discovery of the X​𝑋3872X, a JP​C=1++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1++ state with hidden charm, meant the revival of the heavy meson spectroscopy beyond naive Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ structures. It was first discovered by the Belle Collaborationcitebib.bib6 and soon confirmed by other Collaborations such as CDFcitebib.bib7, D0citebib.bib8 and BaBarcitebib.bib9. The X​𝑋3872X non-conventional properties indicates that more complex structures play an active role in the dynamics of the resonance (see Ref. citebib.bib10, bib.bib11 for more extensive reviews). The latest update of its mass throws a value very close to the D0​D¯∗ 0superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷∗absent 0D0barDast,0 threshold, MD0+MD∗ 0−MX​=(0.04±0.09)​MeV,subscript𝑀superscript𝐷0subscript𝑀superscript𝐷∗absent 0subscript𝑀𝑋3872plus-or-minus0.040.09MeVdisplaystyle MD0+MDast,0-MX=(0.04pm 0.09),textMeV, taking PDG average valuescitebib.bib12, which is compatible with a molecular state if the mass is finally confirmed to be below threshold. Theoretically, most quark models predict a charmonium state, the χc​1​(2​P)subscript𝜒𝑐12𝑃chic1(2P) state, well above such threshold, which makes unlikely its assignment to the X​𝑋3872X. Also, some decay properties of the X​𝑋3872X are intriguing. The strong decays show a large isospin violation, being the ratio of isospin-1 decay J/Ψ​ρ0𝐽Ψsuperscript𝜌0J/Psirho0, followed by ρ0→π+​π−→superscript𝜌0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋rho0topi+pi-, similar to the isospin-0 decay to J/Ψ​ω𝐽Ψ𝜔J/Psiomega, where ω→π+​π−​π0→𝜔superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0omegatopi+pi-pi0, with value 1.1±0.4plus-or-minus1.10.41.1pm 0.4citebib.bib12. This isospin violation is trivially explained with a large D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷∗DbarDast molecular component in the X𝑋X wave function and the phase space effect due to the larger width of the ρ𝜌rho meson. Nevertheless, the charmonium picture is still necessary to explain other observables like the large ψ​(2​S)​γ𝜓2𝑆𝛾psi(2S)gamma decay with respect to the J/ψ​γ𝐽𝜓𝛾J/psigamma decaycitebib.bib13, suggesting that the wave function of the X​𝑋3872X may content a non-negligible charmonium component besides the molecular one. After the discovery of the X​𝑋3872X a great amount of new resonance structures, the so-called XYZ states, have been reported. Then, following the history, one has to look for new symmetries which, based on the measured states, allow to predict new partners that the experimentalists may detect. Predictions in the charm sector can be connected with the bottom sector assuming that Heavy Flavor Symmetry (HFS) holds. This symmetry implies that, in the infinite mass limit, the interaction is the same when you replace the c𝑐c quark by the b𝑏b quark. So, those molecules detected in the charm sector are expected to be reproduced in the bottom sector with even larger binding energy, due to the reduction of the kinetic energy by the larger mass of the b𝑏b quark. Thus, under this assumption, a partner of the X​𝑋3872X, called Xbsubscript𝑋𝑏Xb, is expected to lie close to MB+MB∗∼10.6similar-tosubscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝑀superscript𝐵∗10.6MB+MBastsim 10.6 GeV, with lower isospin breaking due to the smaller mass splitting between charge and neutral B(∗)superscript𝐵∗B(ast) mesonscitebib.bib14. All those predicted states are based on simple molecular structures and QCD symmetries so, at first, their existence is quite robust. However, as we have seen in the case of the X​𝑋3872X, one would expect that nearby Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states can mix with these molecular states, which will have an impact on their dynamics, changing their composition, binding energy or decay propertiescitebib.bib15, bib.bib16. Moreover, most of the signal reported by the experimentalists lies near meson-meson thresholds and can be interpreted as thresholds cups. In this work, we analyze the influence of all these effects on the predictions of the QCD symmetries. For that purpose, we will study the partners of the X​𝑋3872X in b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb sectors, using the coupled-channels formalism developed in Ref. citebib.bib17, where the X​𝑋3872X was found as a JP​C=1++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1++ D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷∗DbarDast state, exclusively bound thanks to the coupling with the 23​P1superscript23subscript𝑃123P1 c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc, as the direct D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷∗DbarDast interaction is not attractive enough to form any bound state.
content_sections.II Symmetries, thresholds and q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞q\\: The predictions of the different symmetries are not the end of the history. Usually, the XYZ structures are interpreted as threshold cusps. However, it is difficult to identified what and when a threshold is associated with a non-trivial structure. A simple model to answer this question has been suggested in Ref. citebib.bib18. Starting with an effective range expansion for the S-wave amplitude in a two-body scattering f0−1​(k)=1a0+12​r0​k2−i​k+Θ​(k4β4)superscriptsubscript𝑓01𝑘1subscript𝑎012subscript𝑟0superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘Θsuperscript𝑘4subscript𝛽4f0-1(k)=frac1a0+frac12r0k2-ik+Thetaleft(frack4beta4right) where a0subscript𝑎0a0 and r0subscript𝑟0r0 are the S-wave scattering length and effective range, respectively, k𝑘k is the center of mass momentum and β𝛽beta some hard scale. One can use the non-relativistic expression for the momentum k=2​μ​E𝑘2𝜇𝐸k=sqrt2mu E near threshold and write the amplitude as a function of E𝐸E f0−1​(E)=1a0−i​2​μ​Esuperscriptsubscript𝑓01𝐸1subscript𝑎0𝑖2𝜇𝐸f0-1(E)=frac1a0-isqrt2mu E from this expression one gets |f0​(E)|2superscriptsubscript𝑓0𝐸2displaystyle|f0(E)|2 =11/a02+2​μ​Eabsent11superscriptsubscript𝑎022𝜇𝐸displaystyle=frac11/a02+2mu E E≥0𝐸0displaystyle Egeq 0 |f0​(E)|2superscriptsubscript𝑓0𝐸2displaystyle|f0(E)|2 =1(1/a0+−2​μ​E)2absent1superscript1subscript𝑎02𝜇𝐸2displaystyle=frac1left(1/a0+sqrt-2mu Eright)2 E0subscript𝑎00a0>0 (attractive interaction but not enough to form a bound state) the distribution is maximal at E=0𝐸0E=0 and, thus, it appears as a cusp at threshold. Also, there is a virtual pole in the second Riemann sheet of the complex energy plane at Evirtual=−12​μ​a02subscript𝐸virtual12𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑎02Erm virtual=-frac12mu a02. For a00subscript𝑉eff0Vrm eff>0 (repulsive) if E>M0𝐸subscript𝑀0E>M0. Usually, there are several Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states below and above threshold and the net attraction or repulsion in the meson-meson channel depends on the balance between the different contributions. Sometimes, it is difficult to generate enough attraction to have a bound state, and threshold cusp linked to virtual states are the most likely explanation for the peaks observed in the experimental data. This interpretation can explain the results of Ref. citebib.bib15, but the situation is even more complicated when several thresholds are involved in the region of interest. In this case, not only the coupling between the bare Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and the thresholds has to be considered, but also the non-diagonal elements associated with the coupling channels, which can produce additional attraction. This is the case of the charged resonances Zc​±subscript𝑍𝑐superscript3900plus-or-minusZcpm or Zc​s​±subscript𝑍𝑐𝑠superscript3985plus-or-minusZcspm, where we do not have Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states associated to these resonances but still we get attraction for the coupling between different channelscitebib.bib19, bib.bib20. The conclusion is that symmetries are not enough to predict partners of the well-established states, because the definitions of these states join in other factors like thresholds and meson spectra. In the following, we will show with a well-established quark model how these general considerations work. In section II we will describe the model we use, including an analysis of up to what extent the model potential satisfies HQSS. Results for the bottom sector are presented in section III. Finally, we summarized our work in section IV.
content_sections.III The model: The first ingredient of the present work is the non-relativistic constituent quark model (CQM) extensively described in Ref. citebib.bib21, which allows us to build the theoretical Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and HQsubscript𝐻𝑄HQ spectrum and the HQ(∗)​H¯Q(∗)superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗HQ(ast)barHQ(ast) interaction. The CQM has been successfully employed to explain the hadron phenomenology both in the light and heavy meson sectorscitebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24 and baryon sectorscitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, from where all the parameters of the model are constrained. Details of the model and explicit expressions can be found in Ref. citebib.bib21, here we will only briefly summarize its most relevant aspects. The basis of the CQM is the postulation that a constituent mass for quarks emerges as a consequence of the dynamical spontaneous chiral symmetry breaking in QCD at some momentum scale. The breaking of the chiral symmetry implies the appearance of massless Goldstone bosons (π,K,η𝜋𝐾𝜂pi,K,eta). The simplest Lagrangian that satisfies the previous properties is, ℒ=ψ¯​(i​∂̸−M​(p2)​Uγ5)​ψℒ¯𝜓𝑖not-partial-differential𝑀superscript𝑝2superscript𝑈subscript𝛾5𝜓L=overlinepsi(i,notpartial-M(p2)Ugamma5),psi where Uγ5=ei​λafπ​ϕa​γ5superscript𝑈subscript𝛾5superscript𝑒𝑖subscript𝜆𝑎subscript𝑓𝜋superscriptitalic-ϕ𝑎subscript𝛾5Ugamma5=eifraclambdaafpiphiagamma5 is a matrix that codes the Goldstone boson fields and M​(p2)𝑀superscript𝑝2M(p2) is the acquired dynamical constituent mass. If this Goldstone boson field matrix Uγ5superscript𝑈subscript𝛾5Ugamma5 is expanded in terms of boson fields, we naturally obtain one-boson exchange interactions between quarks. Multi-boson exchanges are not included, but they are implemented through the exchange of scalar bosons. The chiral symmetry is explicitly broken in the heavy sector, so Goldstone boson exchanges should not appear among heavy quarks. However, quarks still interact through a QCD perturbative effect, the gluon exchange diagramcitebib.bib27. Besides, the model incorporates confinement, a non-perturbative QCD effect that avoids colored hadrons. This interaction can be modeled with a screened potentialcitebib.bib28, which takes into account the saturation of the potential at some interquark distance due to the spontaneous creation of light-quark pairs (see Refs. citebib.bib25, bib.bib29 for details). Meson masses and wave functions are obtained by solving the two-body Schrödinger equation using the Gaussian Expansion Method (GEM)citebib.bib30 which is accurate enough and it simplifies the subsequent evaluation of the needed matrix elements. Once we have the internal wave functions of the mesons, we can obtain the interaction between them using the Resonating Group Method (RGM). Thus, the HQ(∗)​H¯Q(∗)superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗HQ(ast)barHQ(ast) interaction is given by the so-called RGM direct kernel R​G​MVD(P→′,P→)=∑i,j∫dp→ξCdp→ξDdp→ξAdp→ξBdisplaystyleRGMVD(vecPprime,vecP)=sumi,jint dvecpxiCdvecpxiDdvecpxiAdvecpxiB ϕC∗​(p→ξC)​ϕD∗​(p→ξD)​Vi​j​(P→′,P→)​ϕA​(p→ξA)​ϕB​(p→ξB). subscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶subscript→𝑝subscript𝜉𝐶subscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϕ𝐷subscript→𝑝subscript𝜉𝐷subscript𝑉𝑖𝑗superscript→𝑃′→𝑃subscriptitalic-ϕ𝐴subscript→𝑝subscript𝜉𝐴subscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript→𝑝subscript𝜉𝐵displaystylephi*C(vecpxiC)phi*D(vecpxiD)Vij(vecPprime,vecP)phiA(vecpxiA)phiB(vecpxiB). for a general A​B→C​D→𝐴𝐵𝐶𝐷ABto CD process. Actually, quarks or antiquarks exchanges between different mesons are allowed. Such interactions couple different meson states, such as, for instance, the D​D¯∗→J/Ψ​ω→𝐷superscript¯𝐷𝐽Ψ𝜔DbarD*to J/Psiomega channels. However, this sort of processes are suppressed by the meson wave functions overlaps. As mentioned in the introduction, nearby Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states to the two meson thresholds can have an important effect in the dynamics of the system. As we will see later, the closer the state, the larger the effect. Hence, the second ingredient of our study is a mechanism that can couple two- and four-quark states. The coupling between the two sectors require the creation of a light q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq pair. In principle, this process can be deduced from the same quark-quark interaction that drives the meson dynamics. However, the quark pair creation P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 modelcitebib.bib31, bib.bib32 provides similar results to those microscopic calculations within a simpler approach, as shown by Ref. citebib.bib33. The non-relativistic reduction of the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 Hamiltonian is equivalent to the transition operatorcitebib.bib34, 𝒯=𝒯absentdisplaystyleT= −24​π1/2​γ​∑μ∫d3​p​d3​p′​δ​(p+p′)24superscript𝜋12𝛾subscript𝜇superscript𝑑3𝑝superscript𝑑3superscript𝑝′superscript𝛿3𝑝superscript𝑝′displaystyle-24pi1/2gammasummuint d3pd3pprime,delta(p+pprime) [𝒴1​(p−p′2)​bμ†​(p)​dν†​(p′)]C=1,I=0,S=1,J=0superscriptdelimited-subscript𝒴1𝑝superscript𝑝′2superscriptsubscript𝑏𝜇†𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑑𝜈†superscript𝑝′formulae-sequence𝐶1formulae-sequence𝐼0formulae-sequence𝑆1𝐽0displaystyleleft[Y1left(fracp-pprime2right)bmudagger(p)dnudagger(pprime)right]C=1,I=0,S=1,J=0 being μ𝜇mu (ν=μ¯𝜈¯𝜇nu=barmu) the q𝑞q (q¯¯𝑞barq) quantum numbers and γ=g/(2​m)𝛾𝑔2𝑚gamma=g/(2m) a dimensionless parameter that controls the q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq pair creation strength from the vacuum. The transition potential hβ​α​(P)subscriptℎ𝛽𝛼𝑃hbetaalpha(P) within the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 model can be expressed as ⟨ϕHQ​ϕH¯Q​β|𝒯|ψα⟩=P​hβ​α​(P)​δ​(P→cm)quantum-operator-productsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝐻𝑄subscriptitalic-ϕsubscript¯𝐻𝑄𝛽𝒯subscript𝜓𝛼𝑃subscriptℎ𝛽𝛼𝑃superscript𝛿3subscript→𝑃cmphiHQphibarHQbeta|T|psialpha=P,hbetaalpha(P),delta(vecPmboxcm) where P𝑃P is the relative momentum of the two meson state HQ​H¯Qsubscript𝐻𝑄subscript¯𝐻𝑄HQbarHQ, |ψα⟩ketsubscript𝜓𝛼|psialpha are the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ hidden heavy mesons and |ϕHQ​ϕH¯Q​β⟩ketsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝐻𝑄subscriptitalic-ϕsubscript¯𝐻𝑄𝛽|phiHQphibarHQbeta are the two meson states with β𝛽beta quantum numbers. It is worth noticing that the model is controlled by only one parameter, the coupling γ𝛾gamma. The value of the γ𝛾gamma for the charmonium sector was constrained in Ref. citebib.bib35 for the X​𝑋3872X. However, such value does not necessarily have to be the same for other sectors. Indeed, an overall good description of the two meson strong decays for different sectors is obtained if the γ𝛾gamma is logarithmically scaled with the reduced mass of the two quarks of the decaying meson, as analyzed in Ref. citebib.bib36, A satisfactory agreement was obtain, as well as constrains on the value of the γ𝛾gamma, which will be employed in this work for the b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb sector. Additionally, in order to quantify the sensitivity of the results with the value of γ𝛾gamma, a variation of 10%percent1010% will be included in this parameters. Thus, the values of the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 parameter γ𝛾gamma used for the bottomonium sector is γ=0.205±0.020𝛾plus-or-minus0.2050.020gamma=0.205pm 0.020 . In order to perform a full coupled-channels calculation between the Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ and the HQ(∗)​H¯Q(∗)superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗HQ(ast)barHQ(ast) channels we follow Ref. citebib.bib35 (all the details can be found therein). We first assume the combination for the wave function given in Eq. . We recall that all the two-body wave functions are obtained from the solution of the two-body problem with CQM quark-quark interactions, expressed with the GEM. Gathering the RGM direct kernels obtained from RGM (Eq. ) and the coupling with Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ bare mesons, we obtain a coupled-channels equation for the relative wave function of the two mesons: ∑β∫Hβ′​β​(P′,P)​χβ​(P)​P2​𝑑P=E​χβ′​(P′),subscript𝛽subscript𝐻superscript𝛽′𝛽superscript𝑃′𝑃subscript𝜒𝛽𝑃superscript𝑃2differential-d𝑃𝐸subscript𝜒superscript𝛽′superscript𝑃′displaystylesumbetaint Hbetaprimebeta(Pprime,P)chibeta(P)P2dP=Echibetaprime(Pprime), with Hβ′​β​(P′,P)=Hβ′​βRGM​(P′,P)+Vβ′​βeff​(P′,P)subscript𝐻superscript𝛽′𝛽superscript𝑃′𝑃subscriptsuperscript𝐻RGMsuperscript𝛽′𝛽superscript𝑃′𝑃subscriptsuperscript𝑉effsuperscript𝛽′𝛽superscript𝑃′𝑃Hbetaprimebeta(Pprime,P)=Hrm RGMbetaprimebeta(Pprime,P)+Vrm effbetaprimebeta(Pprime,P). In the latter equation, we have denoted Hβ′​βRGMsubscriptsuperscript𝐻RGMsuperscript𝛽′𝛽Hrm RGMbetaprimebeta as the RGM Hamiltonian for the two meson states obtained from the underlying q−q𝑞𝑞q-q interaction and Vβ′​βeff​(P′,P)subscriptsuperscript𝑉effsuperscript𝛽′𝛽superscript𝑃′𝑃Vrm effbetaprimebeta(Pprime,P) is the effective energy-dependent potential between the two mesons, expressed as Eq. , induced by the coupling with Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states. This approach can describe both the renormalization of the bare Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states due to the presence of nearby meson-meson thresholds and the generation of new states through the meson-meson interaction due to the coupling with Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states and the underlying q𝑞q-q𝑞q interaction, as it is the case for the X​𝑋3872X in our modelcitebib.bib17. Before presenting the results, it is worth exploring the level of agreement of our RGM kernels to HQSS. Indeed, some breaking is expected due to the finite heavy quark masses, of order 𝒪​(mQ−1)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑄1O(mQ-1), although they will introduce a small effect as the heavy quark masses are large. This HQSS breaking is shown in Figs. for the radial wave functions of the B𝐵B and B∗superscript𝐵∗Bast mesons in coordinate space. Within our model, the potential that regulates such breaking is the spin-spin term of the one-gluon-exchange interaction, Δ​VOGED∗−D​(r→i​j)=−124​mQ2​αs​(λic⋅λjc)​e−ri​j/r0​(μ)ri​j​r02​(μ)Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑉OGEsuperscript𝐷∗𝐷subscript→𝑟𝑖𝑗124superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑄2subscript𝛼𝑠⋅superscriptsubscript𝜆𝑖𝑐superscriptsubscript𝜆𝑗𝑐superscript𝑒subscript𝑟𝑖𝑗subscript𝑟0𝜇subscript𝑟𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑟02𝜇Delta Vrm OGEDast-D(vecrij)=-frac124mQ2alphasleft(lambdaiccdotlambdajcright)frace-rij/r0(mu)rijr02(mu) which goes as 𝒪​(mQ−2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑄2O(mQ-2). For exact HQSS, these wave functions should be the same and the S𝑆S-wave two-meson state potentials should satisfy the following relations, VHQ​H¯Q∗→HQ​H¯Q∗1++superscriptsubscript𝑉→subscript𝐻𝑄superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗subscript𝐻𝑄superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗superscript1absentdisplaystyle VHQbarHQastto HQbarHQast1++ =VHQ∗​H¯Q∗→HQ∗​H¯Q∗2++=absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑉→superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗superscript2absentabsentdisplaystyle=VHQastbarHQastto HQastbarHQast2++= =32​VHQ​H¯Q→HQ​H¯Q0++−12​VHQ∗​H¯Q∗→HQ∗​H¯Q∗0++. absent32superscriptsubscript𝑉→subscript𝐻𝑄subscript¯𝐻𝑄subscript𝐻𝑄subscript¯𝐻𝑄superscript0absent12superscriptsubscript𝑉→superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄∗superscript0absentdisplaystyle=frac32VHQbarHQto HQbarHQ0++-frac12VHQastbarHQastto HQastbarHQast0++. However, as one can see in Fig. , a small HQSS breaking effect is induced by the small difference in the wave functions of the pseudoscalar and vector heavy mesons. Such breaking is below 2%percent22% for the 1++superscript1absent1++ vs 2++superscript2absent2++ sector, but larger for the 0++superscript0absent0++ sector at large momentum. One should expect this behaviour, since HQSS breaking terms are short-range mQ−1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑄1mQ-1 terms, and so more important for large p𝑝p. Between these limits, the HQSS symmetry suggests similar results for these three sectors.
content_sections.IV Results: As shown in Fig. , our model satisfies HQSS, despite the slight breaking due to the finite value of the heavy quark mass. However, the relation among bare Q​Q¯𝑄¯𝑄QbarQ states and meson-meson thresholds vary for different JP​Csuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶JPC quantum numbers. Thus, extrapolations on the existence of spin or heavy partners of the well-established state based solely on HQSS assumptions should be taken with caution. In this work we will focus on the bottomonium sector, as the charmonium one has been widely studied in Refs. citebib.bib17, bib.bib35, bib.bib37. We consider all the b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb states, predicted by CQM, within ±150plus-or-minus150pm 150 MeV around the closest open B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript¯𝐵∗B(ast)barB(ast) threshold in S𝑆S or D𝐷D wave (Table shows the mass of the considered thresholds and Table the theoretical b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb states. ). The effect of farthest thresholds in the b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb spectra is smooth and we expect it to be encoded in the screened confinement potential as a global contribution. Hence, we will consider the channels: 1. JP​C=0++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentJPC=0++: B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB (S01superscriptsubscript𝑆011S0), B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* (S01superscriptsubscript𝑆011S0-D05superscriptsubscript𝐷055D0) and ω​Υ​(2​S)𝜔Υ2𝑆omegaUpsilon(2S) (S01superscriptsubscript𝑆011S0-D05superscriptsubscript𝐷055D0). 2. JP​C=1++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1++: B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* (S13superscriptsubscript𝑆133S1-D13superscriptsubscript𝐷133D1), B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* (D15superscriptsubscript𝐷155D1) and ω​Υ​(2​S)𝜔Υ2𝑆omegaUpsilon(2S) (S13superscriptsubscript𝑆133S1-D13superscriptsubscript𝐷133D1-D15superscriptsubscript𝐷155D1). 3. JP​C=2++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript2absentJPC=2++: B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB(D21superscriptsubscript𝐷211D2), B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* (D23superscriptsubscript𝐷233D2), B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* (S25superscriptsubscript𝑆255S2-D21superscriptsubscript𝐷211D2-D25superscriptsubscript𝐷255D2) and ω​Υ​(2​S)𝜔Υ2𝑆omegaUpsilon(2S) (S25superscriptsubscript𝑆255S2-D21superscriptsubscript𝐷211D2-D23superscriptsubscript𝐷233D2-D25superscriptsubscript𝐷255D2). where the partial waves are in parenthesis. The ω​Υ​(1​S)𝜔Υ1𝑆omegaUpsilon(1S) channel is too far below to have a significant contribution to the coupled-channel calculation, but we will calculate perturbatively the decay of the resulting states to the latter one. As the charge to neutral mass of the B(∗)superscript𝐵∗B(ast) mesons is small, isospin-breaking effects are expected to be negligible, so they will not be included. The masses of the thresholds energies and the bare q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states are listed in Table and Table. The results for dressed b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb states and additional ones are shown in Table, Table and Table for JP​C=0++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentJPC=0++, 1++superscript1absent1++ and 2++superscript2absent2++, respectively. As a result of the calculation in the 0++superscript0absent0++ sector, we obtain two states. The first one is basically a 33​P0superscript33subscript𝑃033P0 q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq state (87%percent8787%) renormalized by the coupling with the nearest thresholds. The second one is compatible with a 43​P0superscript43subscript𝑃043P0 state, but with an important B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* component. In Fig. , the evolution of this state with increasing values of γ𝛾gamma is shown. This is an example that states which are above threshold, like the 43​P0superscript43subscript𝑃043P0, are more than simple q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states. However, it does not appear any extra molecular states besides the 33​P0superscript33subscript𝑃033P0 and the 43​P0superscript43subscript𝑃043P0. Our naive analysis of the relative position of the q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states with respect to the threshold would suggest a B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB molecular state, because the 43​P0superscript43subscript𝑃043P0 adds an extra attraction to the q𝑞q-q𝑞q interaction, whereas the 33​P0superscript33subscript𝑃033P0 contribute with much less repulsion. But, in this particular channel, the one-pion exchange interaction is forbidden for the B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB and the q𝑞q-q𝑞q interaction is not attractive enough to form any extra states. In the 1++superscript1absent1++ sector we obtain four states (Table). The first one corresponds with a renormalized 33​P1superscript33subscript𝑃133P1 state (88.7%percent88.788.7%) at 10471.910471.910471.9 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2, compatible with the experimental mass of the χb​1​(3​P)subscript𝜒𝑏13𝑃chib1(3P) ( 10512.1±2.3plus-or-minus10512.12.310512.1pm 2.3 ) within the uncertainties of the model. A second state, at 10759.5410759.5410759.54 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2, with a sizable 43​P1superscript43subscript𝑃143P1 component (12%percent1212%) would correspond to the χb​1​(4​P)subscript𝜒𝑏14𝑃chib1(4P), although, once again, it is shown that states above threshold have a very complex structure. Besides, two new states emerge. The first one is basically a B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* molecule (93.65%percent93.6593.65%) at an energy of 105991059910599 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2, whereas the second correspond to a B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* molecule at 107381073810738 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2. Although this state is forbidden in S-wave, its D15superscriptsubscript𝐷155D1 can coupled to the B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* channel through the tensor interaction of the pion, which produces enough attraction to obtain a resonant state. In Fig. , we show the trajectory of the two states above the B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* threshold with increasing values of γ𝛾gamma. Lets study with more detail the 105991059910599 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2 state. In our naive approach, it is a candidate to a bound state or resonance, because the 43​P1superscript43subscript𝑃143P1 state is 133133133 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2 above threshold whereas the 33​P1superscript33subscript𝑃133P1 state is 929292 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2 below threshold. Contrary to the X​𝑋3872X case, this configuration will give repulsion according with Eq. . However, as we can see in Fig. , the repulsion is not enough to unbound the B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* molecule. In fact, it is the coupling with the B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* channel which mostly brings the state below threshold. It is worth reminding that the B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* threshold is around 454545 MeV above the B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* one, whereas in the charm sector mD∗​D∗−mD​D∗≈140subscript𝑚superscript𝐷superscript𝐷subscript𝑚𝐷superscript𝐷140mD*D*-mDD*approx 140 MeV, so the influence of the HQ∗​H¯Q∗superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑄superscriptsubscript¯𝐻𝑄HQ*barHQ* channel in the bottom sector is larger than in the charm one. The prediction is robust because it does not depend of the values of γ𝛾gamma. In Table we show the properties of the Xbsubscript𝑋𝑏Xb considering a 10%percent1010% uncertainty in the value of the γ𝛾gamma parameter. That is an example that the final result is not a simple consequence of the symmetry or the meson spectrum, but also, the nearby coupled channels play an important role to define the energy of the state or the resonance. The mass of this state agrees with the estimations of Ref. citebib.bib14 andcitebib.bib39. Searches for the Xbsubscript𝑋𝑏Xb partner, carried out by the CMS and ATLAS Collaboration in the Υ​π+​π−Υsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋Upsilonpi+pi- channelcitebib.bib40, bib.bib41, analog to the J/Ψ​π+​π−𝐽Ψsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/Psipi+pi- decay of the X​𝑋3872X, and in the J/Ψ​π+​π−​π0𝐽Ψsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0J/Psipi+pi-pi0 channel by Belle have been unfruitful to date. Nevertheless, this setback does not rule out its existence, as the expected lower isospin breaking due to the smaller mass splitting between charged and neutral B(∗)superscript𝐵B(*) mesons, contrary to the X​𝑋3872X case, leads to a strong suppression to the J/Ψ​π+​π−𝐽Ψsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/Psipi+pi- channel. Concerning the J/Ψ​π+​π−​π0𝐽Ψsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0J/Psipi+pi-pi0 channel, assuming that the three pions comes from the ω𝜔omega meson, one can see from Table that the Υ​ωΥ𝜔Upsilonomega channel is weakly coupled to the Xbsubscript𝑋𝑏Xb. However, it still remains as the best channel to detect the elusive Xbsubscript𝑋𝑏Xb, because the radiative decays Υ​(1​S)​γΥ1𝑆𝛾Upsilon(1S)gamma and Υ​(2​S)​γΥ2𝑆𝛾Upsilon(2S)gamma, calculated through the standard expressions for the electric dipole transitioncitebib.bib42 of the b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb components of the Xbsubscript𝑋𝑏Xb, are negligible (see Table). Therefore, further experimental searches should explore other channels such as the B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB*, which could be achieved in the future by the SuperKEKB project. Finally, in the 2++superscript2absent2++ sector, we found up to five states. Two of them are below the B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BbarB threshold, which correspond to the renormalized 23​F2superscript23subscript𝐹223F2 and 33​P2superscript33subscript𝑃233P2 q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states. The third and fourth ones, at 106851068510685 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2 and 10766.910766.910766.9 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2, can be identified with a 43​P2superscript43subscript𝑃243P2 and 33​F2superscript33subscript𝐹233F2 q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states, respectively, with an important component of B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* molecule. The last one, at 10768.310768.310768.3 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2, has an important B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* component with sizable contributions of the 43​P2superscript43subscript𝑃243P2 and 33​F2superscript33subscript𝐹233F2 q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states. In Fig. , we show the trajectory of the states above the B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* threshold with increasing values of the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 parameter γ𝛾gamma. As in the other sectors, states which are below threshold can be clearly identified with renormalized q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states while states above threshold acquire important molecular component which makes it hard to identify them with pure q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states.
content_sections.V Conclusions: Symmetries have played an important role in the development of the hadron spectroscopy. That is the reason why when a new and unexpected state appears, like the X​𝑋3872X, one is tempted to use symmetries (SU, HQSS or HFS) to predict new resonances. In this work we show that, regardless of the final result, this extrapolation is not straightforward, because when we are close to the meson-meson thresholds new dynamics appear which can modified the symmetries predictions. We perform, in the bottom sector, coupled-channels calculations in which both b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb states and meson-meson channels are taken into account. Although the original model satisfy HQSS symmetry, the coupling with b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb states modifies the q𝑞q-q𝑞q potential depending on the relative position of these states with respect to the thresholds. Furthermore, the nondiagonal elements between different meson-meson channels would also modify the interaction. As a sum of all these effects, one can conclude that the nature of the resulting states is more complicated than the estimations based on HFS/HQSS symmetries. We have analyzed the JP​C=0++,1++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentsuperscript1absentJPC=0++,1++ and 2++superscript2absent2++ B(∗)​B¯(∗)superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB(*) coupled with all the b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb states, predicted by CQM, within ±150plus-or-minus150pm 150 MeV around the closest open B(∗)​B(∗)superscript𝐵∗superscript𝐵∗B(ast)B(ast) threshold in S𝑆S or D𝐷D wave. Most of the states can be indentified with b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb states renormalized by the coupling with the meson-meson channels. This renormalization is more important for states above threshold, where the coupling with the molecular components represent more than 50%percent5050% of the composition of the states. Only two new states appear, with quantum numbers JP​C=1++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1++. The first one is basically a B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* molecule (93.65%percent93.6593.65%) at an energy of 105991059910599 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2, whereas the second correspond to a B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* molecule at 107381073810738 MeV/c2superscript𝑐2c2. Further experimental searches, possibly looking to the B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* channel, would confirm the existence of the Xbsubscript𝑋𝑏Xb molecule. Acknowledgements. PID2019-105439GB-C22/AEI/10.13039/501100011033.

all_papers.title: Hadronization and Decay of Excited Heavy Hadrons in Herwig 7
all_papers.abstract:
We revisit the hadronization and decay of excited heavy mesons and heavy
baryons in Herwig 7 general-purpose event-generator, following four distinct
steps: (i) Passing through the polarization of heavy hadrons at the end of
parton shower through the application of heavy quark effective theory (HQET),
where the emergence of a spin-flavour symmetry allows for the determination of
the polarizations of the excited heavy mesons and heavy baryons from the
helicity states of the light and heavy quarks. (ii) Improving the strong and
radiative decay modes of the excited heavy mesons, where in the absence of
conclusive experimental data on many of the decays, one needs to rely on HQET
symmetries to determine the favoured decay modes, widths and branching ratios.
(iii) Re-examination of the production rates of heavy hadrons using all
available experimental data sources and (iv) performing a general tune for
Herwig's free parameters to reflect the implemented changes. We compare our
predictions against existing experimental data in the presence/absence of the
newly implemented updates. These improvements will be available with
Herwig-7.3.0 public release.

all_papers.title:
Transverse Momentum Distributions of Heavy Hadrons and Polarized Heavy
  Quarks
all_papers.abstract:
We initiate the study of transverse momentum-dependent (TMD) fragmentation
functions for heavy quarks, demonstrate their factorization in terms of novel
nonperturbative matrix elements in heavy-quark effective theory (HQET), and
prove new TMD sum rules that arise from heavy-quark spin symmetry. We discuss
the phenomenology of heavy-quark TMD FFs at $B$ factories and find that the
Collins effect, in contrast to claims in the literature, is not parametrically
suppressed by the heavy-quark mass. We further calculate all TMD parton
distribution functions for the production of heavy quarks from polarized gluons
within the nucleon and use our results to demonstrate the potential of the
future EIC to resolve TMD heavy-quark fragmentation in semi-inclusive DIS,
complementing the planned EIC program to use heavy quarks as probes of gluon
distributions.

qualitative: Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) leads to degeneracies in the spectrum of heavy hadrons, resulting in distinct spin states being related by symmetry transformations.

HQSS implies that the transverse momentum-dependent (TMD) fragmentation functions for heavy quarks can be factorized into universal nonperturbative matrix elements in the heavy-quark effective theory (HQET).
The symmetry gives rise to new TMD sum rules that constrain the structure of TMD fragmentation functions and parton distribution functions for heavy quarks.
Due to HQSS, the Collins effect in heavy quark fragmentation is not parametrically suppressed by the heavy-quark mass, contrary to previous claims in the literature.
HQSS enables the classification of TMD parton distribution functions for heavy quark production from polarized gluons in the nucleon, allowing for a systematic study of spin-dependent effects.
quantitative: The TMD fragmentation functions for heavy quarks factorize as: $ D_{H}^{(i)}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) = \sum_{\lambda} \langle \mathcal{O}_{\lambda} \rangle \, \mathcal{F}_{\lambda}^{(i)}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) $, where $ \langle \mathcal{O}_{\lambda} \rangle $ are nonperturbative matrix elements in HQET.

The TMD sum rules derived from HQSS take the form: $ \sum_{H} \int d^2\mathbf{p}_T \, D_{H}^{(i)}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) = \text{const} \times \delta_{ij} $, reflecting the symmetry constraints on fragmentation.
The Collins function for heavy quarks, $ H_{1}^{\perp}(z, \mathbf{p}_T^2) $, does not scale as $ 1/m_Q $, indicating no parametric suppression by the heavy-quark mass $ m_Q $.

The TMD parton distribution functions for heavy quark production from polarized gluons are expressed in terms of HQET operators: $ f_{g \to Q}(x, \mathbf{k}_T^2) = \langle N | \bar{\psi}_Q \Gamma \psi_Q | N \rangle $, with $ \Gamma $ encoding spin and momentum structure.

The cross section for semi-inclusive DIS with heavy quark production at the EIC scales as $ \sigma \sim \alpha_s^2 \, \frac{1}{m_Q^2} \, \frac{1}{x} \, \frac{1}{Q^2} $, highlighting the sensitivity to TMD effects at high precision.

content_sections.1 Introduction: Hadronization — the nonperturbative mechanism that confines quarks and gluons produced in high-energy collisions into the experimentally observed color-singlet mesons and baryons — is a key aspect of virtually any process involving Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD), but its fundamental description from first principles remains elusivecitebib.bib1. In the quest for this fundamental understanding, the fragmentation of bottom and charm quarks to heavy mesons can play a vital role because the mass of the heavy quark imprints as a perturbative scale on the otherwise nonperturbative dynamics of hadronization. The unique properties of heavy quarks as color-charged, but perturbatively accessible objects make them ideally suited as probes of the hadronization cascade, effectively serving as a static color source coupling to the light degrees of freedom. An improved field-theoretic understanding of heavy-quark fragmentation will also benefit the description of heavy flavor in Monte-Carlo generators for the LHCcitebib.bib2, where many key searches and Higgs coupling measurements involve final-state charm or bottom quarks. A rigorous field-theoretic framework in which hadronization can be studied in detail is that of transverse momentum-dependent (TMD) fragmentation functions (FFs), for which all-order factorization theorems have been establishedcitebib.bib3. Like collinear fragmentation functions, TMD FFs depend on the longitudinal momentum fraction zHsubscript𝑧𝐻zH that the hadron retains from its parent quark. In addition, they describe the transverse momentum that the hadron picks up by recoiling against other fragmentation products, including the full quantum correlations with the quark polarization, which provides a three-dimensional picture of the fragmentation cascade. For processes with initial-state hadrons, TMD FFs are complemented by TMD parton distribution functions (PDFs) describing the three-dimensional motion of quarks and gluons inside the nucleon. The TMD dynamics of light quarks and gluons are a well-established field of experimental study citebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, phenomenological analysis (see e. g. refs. citebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18), and progress towards first-principle calculations using lattice field-theorycitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25; for a recent comprehensive overview, see ref. citebib.bib26. Precision TMD measurements are a key physics target of the future Electron-Ion Collider (EIC)citebib.bib27. In this paper we study, for the first time, the TMD FFs of heavy quarks to heavy hadrons. Our theoretical tool to analyze the fragmentation of heavy quarks is (boosted) Heavy-Quark Effective Theory (bHQET)citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, which has previously been applied to the well-understood collinear (or longitudinal) heavy-quark FFscitebib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40. We demonstrate that applying bHQET to TMD FFs gives rise to novel, universal matrix elements describing the nonperturbative transverse dynamics of light QCD degrees of freedom in the presence of a heavy quark (i. e. , a static color source). While a large part of this work is devoted to developing this new theoretical formalism, we will also consider the phenomenology of heavy-quark TMD FFs in two distinct processes, e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions and semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS), which are illustrated in : (a) Heavy quarks are copiously produced in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions. We are interested in the case where the quarks are produced relativistically, as is the case for charm quarks at existing B𝐵B factories, such that their fragmentation processes are independent. The production rate is largest in the back-to-back region, where the cross section differential in the small hadron transverse momenta factorizes in terms of two unpolarized TMD FFs D1​H/Q​(zH,kT)subscript𝐷1𝐻𝑄subscript𝑧𝐻subscript𝑘𝑇D1,H/Q(zH,kT). Furthermore, the heavy-quark pair is produced in an entangled transverse polarization state in central events. This entanglement imprints on the distribution of final-state hadrons as an azimuthal modulation known as the Collins effect, whose strength schematically is given by Rcos⁡(2​ϕ0)∝H1​H/Q⟂​(zH,kT)⊗H1​H¯′/Q¯⟂​(zH′,kT),proportional-tosubscript𝑅2subscriptitalic-ϕ0tensor-productsubscriptsuperscript𝐻perpendicular-to1𝐻𝑄subscript𝑧𝐻subscript𝑘𝑇subscriptsuperscript𝐻perpendicular-to1superscript¯𝐻′¯𝑄subscript𝑧superscript𝐻′subscript𝑘𝑇displaystyle Rcos(2phi0)propto Hperp1,H/Q(zH,kT)otimes Hperp1,barHprime/,barQ(zHprime,kT),, (1.1) where H1​H/i⟂subscriptsuperscript𝐻perpendicular-to1𝐻𝑖Hperp1,H/i is called the Collins function. The light-quark Collins effect has been measured in detail in pion and kaon samples by the Bellecitebib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43 and BaBar collaborationscitebib.bib44, bib.bib45, but despite a proposal in ref. citebib.bib44, a measurement (or search) of the heavy-quark Collins effect has not yet been performed. We will find that the heavy-quark Collins function encodes intricate nonperturbative physics, which motivates a dedicated measurement.1It has been argued in the experimental literaturecitebib.bib44 that the Collins effect is suppressed by the quark mass. We contrast these claims with our findings in . (b) A previously overlooked aspect of heavy-quark phenomenology in electron-nucleon collisions at the future EIC is that heavy quarks can be pair-produced in initial-state gluon splittings at small transverse momentum, with one quark e. g. going down the beam pipe and the other undergoing hard scattering and subsequent fragmentation into a heavy hadron, which in turn is reconstructed in a semi-inclusive measurement, as illustrated in (b). Crucially, this process can be described by the standard TMD factorization for semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS) when both the transverse momentum and the mass of the quark are small compared to the hard scattering energy Q𝑄Q. It thus retains the full sensitivity to the initial and final-state transverse momentum distribution (encoded in heavy-quark TMD PDFs and FFs) with respect to the photon direction. To make phenomenological predictions for heavy-quark SIDIS, we fill a gap in the literature and compute all polarized heavy-quark TMD PDFs by perturbatively matching them onto collinear twist-2 nucleon PDFs, extending the analysis of the unpolarized heavy-quark TMD PDF in refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib47.2Ref. citebib.bib47 also computed all secondary quark mass effects on light-quark distributions, including mass effects in the Collins-Soper kernel, which drive the renormalization of many of the objects we introduce here beyond leading-logarithmic order. The much more involved 𝒪​(αs2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2O(alphas2) secondary quark mass effects in the gluon TMD PDF were recently calculated in ref. citebib.bib48. Interestingly, while many polarized TMD PDFs are strongly suppressed for heavy quarks, we find a nonzero leading result for the so-called worm-gearL𝐿L function h1​L⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝐿perpendicular-toh1Lperp, encoding the production of transversely polarized (heavy) quarks from linearly polarized light quarks and gluons, which is possible because the quark mass violates chirality. Indeed, the expectation that transverse quark polarization effects are suppressed by quark masses goes back to the early days of QCDcitebib.bib49, and we find that heavy-quark TMDs provide an arena to make this statement precise within twist-2 collinear factorization. For future phenomenology at the EIC, this nonzero conversion rate provides an exciting avenue to observe the heavy-quark Collins function because the factorized SIDIS cross section contains an azimuthal spin asymmetry, Asin⁡(2​ϕH)∝SL​h1​L⟂⊗H1​H/i⟂. proportional-tosubscript𝐴2subscriptitalic-ϕ𝐻tensor-productsubscript𝑆𝐿superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝐿perpendicular-tosubscriptsuperscript𝐻perpendicular-to1𝐻𝑖displaystyle Asin(2phiH)propto SLh1Lperpotimes Hperp1,H/i,. (1.2) Importantly, this asymmetry unlike eq. involves only a single Collins function multiplying the perturbatively predicted worm-gear L𝐿L function, making it possible to extract its sign. We point out that an extensive heavy-flavor physics program is already being planned for the Electron Ion Collider (EIC), which will leverage heavy-quark pair production as a hard probe of gluon TMDscitebib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55 and of cold nuclear mattercitebib.bib56, bib.bib57, as illustrated in (c). We stress that this is not the case we consider in this paper: In case(c), the transverse momentum imbalance, production rate, and distribution of the heavy-quark pair are sensitive to the initial-state gluon TMD PDF (or the nuclear collinear gluon PDFs), but on the fragmentation side are at most sensitive to the well-understood longitudinal momentum (zHsubscript𝑧𝐻zH) distribution at leading powercitebib.bib53. In contrast to this, the TMD processes in (a) and (b) are directly sensitive to the nonperturbative transverse dynamics of heavy-quark fragmentation.3 A very interesting middle ground is occupied by ref. citebib.bib58, which analyzed the transverse momentum spectrum of heavy headrons inside groomed jets, also using bHQET. The resulting bHQET matrix elements in ref. citebib.bib58 are predominantly longitudinal: Depending on the precise parametric regime, the dominant contribution to the transverse momentum either comes from perturbative collinear-soft modes stopping the soft-drop grooming algorithmcitebib.bib59, which factorize from the heavy-quark dynamics, or from bHQET modes subject to a primary soft-drop criterion and a secondary measurement on perturbative transverse momenta. (Compared to our results in this work for inclusive heavy-quark TMD FFs sensitive to the full transverse structure of the fragmentation cascade, this effect of the grooming is also responsible for the striking absence of Collins-Soper scaling reported in ref. citebib.bib58. ) Despite these differences in the experimental observable and theoretical structure, we find that it should be possible to establish a powerful connection between our results here and those of ref. citebib.bib58. Specifically, if 𝒪​(ΛQCD2)𝒪superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2O(LambdaQCD2) power corrections from intrinsic transverse hadronic dynamics can be resolved even within groomed jets, as outlined around their eq. (5.9), this would access a second moment of the unpolarized bHQET TMD fragmentation factor we will introduce in . If this connection can be made precise, it would suggest that the bHQET fragmentation factors we consider in can appear also in scenarios where the mass has been integrated out together with additional observables (i. e. , the soft-drop criterion in this case). We note that the TMD fragmentation of light quarks to quarkonia has been studied in ref. citebib.bib60, in that case by matching onto nonrelativistic QCD, and similarly for light-quark TMD dynamics in hard quarkonium production and decay in ref. citebib.bib61, bib.bib62. The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: In , we analyze heavy-quark TMD FFs and identify the new bHQET matrix elements and perturbative matching coefficients that characterize the fragmentation dynamics. In , we discuss the all-order structure of matching polarized heavy quark TMD PDFs onto collinear PDFs and explicitly compute the 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠O(alphas) matching onto gluon PDFs. In , we use our results from the previous two sections to outline the prospects for heavy-quark TMD phenomenology at e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- colliders and the future EIC.

The unpolarized TMD FF encodes the total rate for producing an unpolarized hadron from an unpolarized quark, while the Collins TMD FF describes the strength of the correlation between the quark’s transverse polarization and the direction of the hadron transverse momentum. The leading TMD fragmentation functions have been proven to be universal between processescitebib.bib68, i. e. , they are independent of whether the Wilson line points to the future (e+​e−→hadrons→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒hadronse+e-totexthadrons) or the past (SIDIS). Note that these scalar projections of the TMD fragmentation correlator are invariant under n¯μ↦eα​n¯μmaps-tosuperscript¯𝑛𝜇superscript𝑒𝛼superscript¯𝑛𝜇barnmumapsto ealphabarnmu by construction. Since ΛQCD≪mmuch-less-thansubscriptΛQCD𝑚LambdaQCDll m, the nonperturbative dynamics in the fragmentation process are constrained by heavy-quark symmetry in all cases, but differences arise depending on the hierarchy between these two parametric scales and the magnitude kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT of the transverse momentum or, equivalently, the inverse of the transverse distance 1/bT∼kTsimilar-to1subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑘𝑇1/bTsim kT. Broadly speaking, we will consider the two cases illustrated in . In case(a), which we analyze in , kT∼ΛQCDsimilar-tosubscript𝑘𝑇subscriptΛQCDkTsimLambdaQCD is generated during the nonperturbative fragmentation process itself, while perturbative emissions at the scale m≫kTmuch-greater-than𝑚subscript𝑘𝑇mgg kT are suppressed. In this case, the heavy hadron carries almost all the longitudinal momentum provided by the initial heavy quark, while the kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT dependence is carried by universal nonperturbative functions describing how the “brown muck” separates from other light hadronic final states. In this regime, “disfavored” fragmentation functions where the valence content of the identified heavy hadron does not match the initial heavy quark, e. g. Q→H¯→𝑄¯𝐻QtobarH, Q→h→𝑄ℎQto h, or q,g→H→𝑞𝑔𝐻q,gto H, are forbidden by heavy-quark symmetry. To simplify the analysis, we will count 1−zH∼1,similar-to1subscript𝑧𝐻1displaystyle 1-zHsim 1,, (2.30) i. e. , we will assume that the zHsubscript𝑧𝐻zH measurement does not probe the precise longitudinal momentum distribution near the endpoint, which is the case e. g. if zHsubscript𝑧𝐻zH is integrated over a sufficiently large bin or when taking Mellin moments of the zHsubscript𝑧𝐻zH distribution. While relaxing this modification on the fragmentation side would be straightforward and leads to interesting fully-differential bHQET fragmentation shape functions, this would also require consistently modifying the description of the opposite collinear sector by reinstating the transverse momentum dependence in the formalism of ref. citebib.bib40 for e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions, and by crossing the jet function in that reference into the initial state using the methods of ref. citebib.bib69 for SIDIS, all of which we leave to future work. In case(b), which we consider in , the distributions in transverse and longitudinal momentum are determined by perturbative dynamics at the scale kT∼m≫ΛQCDsimilar-tosubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚much-greater-thansubscriptΛQCDkTsim mggLambdaQCD, while the dynamics of the nonperturbative bound state only contribute a normalization factor. In the case of the unpolarized TMD FF, this normalization factor admits an interpretation as the total probability for Q𝑄Q to fragment into H𝐻H, as is well known for inclusive heavy hadron production cross sectionscitebib.bib70, bib.bib37, bib.bib38. In this case, the disfavored fragmentation functions for Q→H¯→𝑄¯𝐻QtobarH or q→H→𝑞𝐻qto H, and Q→h→𝑄ℎQto h or g→H→𝑔𝐻gto H, are perturbatively suppressed by 𝒪​(αs2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2O(alphas2) and 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠O(alphas) at the scale μ∼msimilar-to𝜇𝑚musim m, respectively. We stress that we continue to assume eq. also in this regime, so longitudinal momentum distributions remain perturbative.

The appropriate theory that describes the dynamics at the scale ΛQCDsubscriptΛQCDLambdaQCD in either case and makes the heavy-quark symmetry manifest is boosted Heavy-Quark Effective Theory (bHQET)citebib.bib35, bib.bib36, i. e. , the application of HQETcitebib.bib71 to heavy quarks produced in an energetic collision. The effective theory is constructed by integrating out the off-shell fluctuations of the heavy quark field at the scale m𝑚m; these in particular include its antiquark component with energy gap 2​m2𝑚2m. The dynamic degrees of freedom are heavy-quark fields hv​(x)subscriptℎ𝑣𝑥hv(x) that are labeled by the timelike direction vμsuperscript𝑣𝜇vmu, which we choose to be the velocity of the heavy hadron, vμ=PHμMH,v2=1,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑣𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐻𝜇subscript𝑀𝐻superscript𝑣21displaystyle vmu=fracPHmuMH,,qquad v2=1,, (2.31) The tree-level matching of the massive QCD quark field onto hvsubscriptℎ𝑣hv at μ∼msimilar-to𝜇𝑚musim m reads: ψQ​(x)=e−i​m​v⋅x​hv​(x)​[1+𝒪​(1m)]. subscript𝜓𝑄𝑥superscript𝑒⋅i𝑚𝑣𝑥subscriptℎ𝑣𝑥delimited-1𝒪1𝑚displaystylepsiQ(x)=e-imvcdot xhv(x)Bigl[1+OBigl(frac1mBigr)Bigr],. (2.32) The hv​(x)subscriptℎ𝑣𝑥hv(x) are implemented as Dirac spinors satisfying the projection relations P±≡1±v̸2,P+​hv=hv,P−​hv=0,h¯v​P+=h¯v,h¯v​P−=0. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑃plus-or-minusplus-or-minus1italic-v̸2formulae-sequencesubscript𝑃subscriptℎ𝑣subscriptℎ𝑣formulae-sequencesubscript𝑃subscriptℎ𝑣0formulae-sequencesubscript¯ℎ𝑣subscript𝑃subscript¯ℎ𝑣subscript¯ℎ𝑣subscript𝑃0displaystyle Ppmequivfrac1pmnotv2,,qquad P+hv=hv,,qquad P-hv=0,,qquadbarhvP+=barhv,,qquadbarhvP-=0,. (2.33) For external states, the matching reads |H,hH;X⟩=m​|Hv,hH;X⟩𝐻subscriptℎ𝐻𝑋𝑚subscript𝐻𝑣subscriptℎ𝐻𝑋lvert H,hH;X=sqrtm,lvert Hv,hH;X, and we use a nonrelativistic normalization convention for the bHQET states. In addition, the effective theory contains light-quark and gluon degrees of freedom that have isotropic momentum pμ∼ΛQCDsimilar-tosuperscript𝑝𝜇subscriptΛQCDpmusimLambdaQCD in the rest frame of the heavy hadron. The tree-level matching for these is trivial; in particular, the Wilson line W​(x)𝑊𝑥W(x) takes the same form in the effective theory, but consists of gluon fields that only have support on a restricted set of modes. For reference, the leading HQET Lagrangian is given bycitebib.bib71 ℒ=h¯v​(i​v⋅D)​hv+ℒlight+𝒪​(1m),ℒsubscript¯ℎ𝑣⋅i𝑣𝐷subscriptℎ𝑣subscriptℒlight𝒪1𝑚displaystyleL=barhv(ivcdot D)hv+Llight+OBigl(frac1mBigr),, (2.34) where ℒlightsubscriptℒlightLlight is a copy of the QCD Lagrangian with nℓsubscript𝑛ℓnell massless quark flavors. The spin degrees of freedom of the heavy-quark can be explicitly decoupled from the light dynamics at leading power in 1/m1𝑚1/m by performing a field redefinition involving static Wilson lines Yv​(x)subscript𝑌𝑣𝑥Yv(x)citebib.bib72, bib.bib73, hv​(x)=Yv​(x)​hv​(x),Yv​(x)=P​[exp⁡(i​g​∫0∞ds​v⋅A​(x+v​s))]. formulae-sequencesubscriptℎ𝑣𝑥subscript𝑌𝑣𝑥superscriptsubscriptℎ𝑣0𝑥subscript𝑌𝑣𝑥𝑃delimited-i𝑔superscriptsubscript0⋅differential-d𝑠𝑣𝐴𝑥𝑣𝑠displaystyle hv(x)=Yv(x),hv(x),,qquad Yv(x)=PBigl[expBigl(igint0infty!ds,vcdot A(x+vs)Bigr)Bigr],. (2.35) In this way, the heavy-quark Lagrangian becomes that of a free theory, ℒ=h¯v​(i​v⋅∂)​hvsuperscriptℒ0superscriptsubscript¯ℎ𝑣0⋅i𝑣superscriptsubscriptℎ𝑣0L=barhv(ivcdotpartial)hv, while the Yv​(x)subscript𝑌𝑣𝑥Yv(x) takes the place of hv​(x)subscriptℎ𝑣𝑥hv(x) in all external operators, acting as a static source of soft gluons. (We take the coefficient to vanish for hQ+hℓ≠hHsubscriptℎ𝑄subscriptℎℓsubscriptℎ𝐻hQ+hellneq hH, i. e. , one sum is always eliminated in practice by helicity conservation. ) For the case of inclusive fragmentation, it has been known for a long timecitebib.bib38, bib.bib71 that the factorized form of eq. together with parity and eq. implies relations between the fragmentation probabilities to different hadron states within the same heavy-quark spin symmetry multiplet, i. e. , with the same sℓ=12,1,32,…subscript𝑠ℓ12132…sell=tfrac12,1,tfrac32,dots. As an example, at the strict leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m, an unpolarized charm quark is exactly three times as likely to fragment into an excited spin-111 vector meson (D∗superscript𝐷D*) than into the corresponding pseudoscalar state (D𝐷D). The physical reason for this is that the light dynamics do not see the heavy-quark spin at leading power, and thus the same nonperturbative matrix elements with given sℓ,hℓsubscript𝑠ℓsubscriptℎℓsell,hell appear in several cases. This analysis is simplified by the fact that for unpolarized or linearly polarized heavy quarks, light amplitudes for different helicities cannot interfere. One key goal of the next section will be to work out the consequences of heavy-quark spin symmetry for transverse momentum-dependent fragmentation functions, where transverse quark polarization will let us access this interference for the first time.

Note that finding a nonzero result for the Collins fragmentation function during this step crucially relies on the presence of the Wilson line, which distinguished a nontrivial reference direction zμsuperscript𝑧𝜇zmu in the rest frame. From our analysis so far, we can conclude that both leading-power TMD fragmentation functions for unpolarized hadrons are allowed by the discrete symmetries of QCD in the heavy-quark limit at leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m. Written as in eq. , they are also manifestly independent of the flavor and mass of the heavy quark. However, the physical interpretation of χ1,H​(bT)subscript𝜒1𝐻subscript𝑏𝑇chi1,H(bT) and χ1,H⟂​(bT)superscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑇chi1,Hperp(bT) is still fairly unclear at this point, and in fact we have not yet made use of heavy-quark spin symmetry. As expected from its relation to the unpolarized TMD FF, the Fourier transform of χ1,Hsubscript𝜒1𝐻chi1,H is simply the total conditional probability to produce H𝐻H at rest given an initial quark momentum kTsubscript𝑘𝑇kT transverse to the direction of the static color source provided by the Wilson line. The bHQET analogue χ1,H⟂superscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tochi1,Hperp of the Collins function on the other hand can be interpreted as a conditional density of quark spin ΣQsubscriptΣ𝑄SigmaQ with respect to a magnetization axis defined by the static color source and the final-state transverse momentum. These physical interpretations roughly correspond to those of the associated relativistic fragmentation functions, which are often written in a form similar to eq. . Crucially, however, the meaning of the spin space on which the density matrix is defined is different in the heavy-quark case: For light quarks, whose spin after hadronization is an ill-defined concept, it only refers to the initial spin state in which the light quark is prepared. Heavy quarks at leading power in 1/m1𝑚1/m, by contrast, retain their initial spin state throughout the fragmentation process, and thus their spin density matrix together with the hadron spin measurement instead probes the angular momentum distribution of the final-state light constituents of the heavy hadron.8A related difference is that relativistic fragmentation functions have to be interpreted as number densities rather than probabilities due to the semi-inclusive measurement acting also on hadronic states at higher multiplicity. By contrast, the unpolarized bHQET TMD fragmentation factor upon Fourier transform, and up to renormalization effectscitebib.bib75, has an interpretation as a probability density because additional pair production of heavy quarks is power suppressed by kT≪mmuch-less-thansubscript𝑘𝑇𝑚kTll m. Our analysis shows, for the first time, that they hold without modification and point by point in the distribution when resolving the hadron transverse momentum. Generalizations of the above to higher multiplets are trivial. Given that the sum over fragmentation factors within a multiplet encodes the full information on each individual one, it is interesting to ask what form the total fragmentation factor takes. Performing the complete sum over states, we have χ1​(bT)≡∑Hχ1,H​(bT)=1Nc​Tr⁡⟨0​|W†​(b⟂)​Yv​(b⟂)​Yv†​​W​|​0⟩. subscript𝜒1subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝐻subscript𝜒1𝐻subscript𝑏𝑇1subscript𝑁𝑐Tr0superscript𝑊†subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑌𝑣subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscriptsuperscript𝑌†𝑣0𝑊00displaystylechi1(bT)equivsumHchi1,H(bT)=frac1NcoperatornameTrbig0biglvertWdagger(bperp),Yv(bperp),Ydaggerv,Wbigrvert0big,. (2.102) We see that the total unpolarized heavy-quark TMD FF reduces to a vacuum matrix element of two staple-shaped Wilson-line configurations along the lightlike and timelike direction, respectively. (Recall that we have suppressed transverse gauge links at infinity; the Wilson lines in the interpolating fields for the out states cancel. ) This is reminiscent of the heavy quark-antiquark form factor proposed in ref. citebib.bib20 to extract the TMD soft function on the lattice, but in contrast to that proposal directly relates to a physical observable, i. e. , the total TMD cross section for producing heavy hadrons in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions. We contend that this makes eq. the theoretically simplest TMD observable possible in QCD, since it is entirely given in terms of vacuum matrix elements of Wilson loops.Collins TMD FF: A naive expectation from heavy-quark spin symmetry might be that the Collins FF should be suppressed by 1/m1𝑚1/m because it encodes a correlation between the initial quark transverse polarization vector and the transverse momentum of hadronic final-state radiation. In the case of light quarks, this correlation arises directly from the nonperturbative dynamics of the QCD Lagrangian, as illustrated in (a), but in the heavy-quark case it naively seems to require a suppressed magnetic interaction with the heavy-quark spin. We will now see that this is not the case. As illustrated in (b), the angle between the final-state heavy-quark and light transverse polarization vectors (i. e, the relative phase between their helicity states) determines which hadron in the spin symmetry multiplet is produced, even without a dynamical heavy-quark spin interaction taking place. Reconstructing this information experimentally thus induces a correlation between the heavy-quark and the light spin state. Crucially, spin symmetry ensures that the final-state heavy-quark spin state is identical to the one it was prepared in. The light spin state in turn is in general correlated with the transverse momentum k⟂subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokperp of hadronic final-state radiation, since they both arise from the same nonperturbative dynamics of the light degrees of freedom, leading to a Collins effect at the leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m. To illustrate this, it is again instructive to consider the case of the pseudoscalar meson, sℓ=1/2,sH=0:χ1,H⟂(bT)=14[ρℓ,−+(b⟂)−ρℓ,+−(b⟂)]displaystyle sell=1/2,,sH=0,:quadchi1,Hperp(bT)=frac14bigl[rhoell,-+(bperp)-rhoell,+-(bperp)bigr] (2.103) As expected, the Collins FF in the heavy-quark limit contains information about the strength of the interference, and hence the relative nonperturbative phases, of amplitudes for different light helicities. As a corollary, we conclude that the Collins FF must vanish at leading order in 1/m1𝑚1/m when summing over all the hadrons in the spin symmetry multiplet, ∑H∈Mℓχ1,H⟂​(bT)=0. subscript𝐻subscript𝑀ℓsuperscriptsubscript𝜒1𝐻perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑇0displaystylesumHin Mellchi1,Hperp(bT)=0,. (2.104) This is immediate to see from the diagonal form of the summed quark spin density matrix or the full correlator in eq. .
