content_sections.I Introduction: The X​𝑋3872Xcitebib.bib1, also known as χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1, and the Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zccitebib.bib2, bib.bib3 have been proposed as candidates of isoscalar and isovector D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷DbarD* hadronic molecules, respectively, since longcitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52. They are expected to have heavy quark spin and light flavor SU siblingscitebib.bib27, with the observed Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐4020Zccitebib.bib53 and Zc​s​subscript𝑍𝑐𝑠3985Zcscitebib.bib54, bib.bib55 structures being the candidates for the Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zc partners. Very recently, the LHCb Collaboration announced the observation of a new resonant structure X​𝑋3960X in the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- invariant mass distribution of the B+→Ds+​Ds−​K+→superscript𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾B+rightarrow Ds+Ds-K+ decaycitebib.bib56. The peak structure is just above the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- threshold with a statistical significance larger than 12​σ12𝜎12sigma. The mass and width reported by LHCb, from an analysis using a Breit-Wigner (BW) parametrization, are M=(3955±6±11)​MeV,Γ=(48±17±10)​MeV,formulae-sequence𝑀plus-or-minus3955611MeVΓplus-or-minus481710MeVM=(3955pm 6pm 11)MeV,quadGamma=(48pm 17pm 10)MeV, respectively, with JP​C=0++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentJPC=0++ favored quantum numbers. This resonance couples to the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- in an S𝑆S wave, and is an excellent candidate for a Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- hadronic molecule, which has been predicted in various theoretical calculationscitebib.bib27, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60. In particular, a recent lattice work predicted a shallow Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- bound state below the threshold with a binding energy 2​mDs−M=6.2−3.8+2.0​MeV2subscript𝑚subscript𝐷𝑠𝑀subscriptsuperscript6.22.03.8MeV2mDs-M=6.2+2.0-3.8MeVcitebib.bib58, and the vector-meson-dominance model in Ref. citebib.bib60 predicted a shallow virtual state with a virtual energy roughly in the range of [4.7,35.5]4.735.5[4.7,35.5]MeV. The difference between a bound and a virtual state is that the former is a pole of the scattering amplitude below threshold on the real axis of the first Riemann sheet (RS) of the complex energy plane, while the latter is on the second RS. The bound state found in the lattice calculationcitebib.bib58 becomes a narrow resonance when the D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD channel is coupled to the Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs; however, it still couples predominantly to the Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs and owes its origin to the interactions of the Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs pair. In this sense and in an abuse of language, we will refer to such a pole as a “bound state”, even if it becomes metastable due to the opening of the lower channel which weakly couples to the pole. Such a notation is also widely used in the literature; e. g. , the positronium is ubiquitously called an e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- bound state although it decays into photons. The LHCb measurements of the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- near-threshold structurecitebib.bib56, the mass of the X​𝑋3872Xcitebib.bib61, the isospin breaking in its decayscitebib.bib62, and the BESIII measurements of the Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zccitebib.bib63, bib.bib64 and Zc​s​subscript𝑍𝑐𝑠3985Zcscitebib.bib54 allow us to make a prediction of the full spectrum of the S𝑆S-wave hadronic molecules formed by a pair of ground state charmed and anticharmed mesons, at leading order (LO) of the nonrelativistic effective field theory (NREFT)citebib.bib10, bib.bib65, bib.bib23, bib.bib27, bib.bib29, bib.bib43.
content_sections.II NREFT: Since the charm quark mass, mc∼1.5similar-tosubscript𝑚𝑐1.5mcsim 1.5GeV, is much larger than the nonperturbative scale of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD), ΛQCD∼0.3similar-tosubscriptΛQCD0.3Lambdarm QCDsim 0.3GeV, the interaction depending on the charm quark spin inside hadrons is suppressed, compared with the spin-independent one, by a factor of 𝒪​(ΛQCD/mc)∼0.2similar-to𝒪subscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑐0.2O(Lambdarm QCD/mc)sim 0.2. Consequently, there is an approximate heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS), and hadrons containing a charm (heavy) quark fall into spin multiplets characterized by the parity of the hadrons and the total angular momentum of the light degrees of freedom jℓPsuperscriptsubscript𝑗ℓ𝑃jellP, including the spin of the light quarks and gluons and the orbital angular momentum. The ground state pseudoscalar and vector charmed mesons D𝐷D and D∗superscript𝐷D* belong to the multiplet with jℓP=1/2−superscriptsubscript𝑗ℓ𝑃1superscript2jellP=1/2-, denoted in this work as H𝐻H. In the energy region near the thresholds of a pair of jℓP=1/2−superscriptsubscript𝑗ℓ𝑃1superscript2jellP=1/2- charmed and anticharmed mesons, the approximate HQSS is also manifested in the interaction between the two hadrons in the pair. At LO of the heavy quark and nonrelativistic expansion, the S𝑆S-wave interaction between a pair of charmed and anticharmed mesons can be parametrized into constant contact terms, which are then inserted into the Lippmann-Schwinger equation (LSE) to solve for poles (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib27 and the next section). These poles would correspond to hadronic molecules formed by the meson pairs. The total angular momentum of the light degrees of freedom for the H​H¯𝐻¯𝐻HbarH pair can have nℓ=2subscript𝑛ℓ2nell=2 possible values: 00 and 111. If we further consider the light flavor SU symmetry, all H​H¯𝐻¯𝐻HbarH pairs form nf=2subscript𝑛𝑓2nf=2 irreducible representations. This follows from the reduction [3¯]⊗=⊕tensor-productdelimited-¯3delimited-3direct-sumdelimited-1delimited-8[bar3]otimes=oplus, where [3¯]delimited-¯3[bar3] and delimited-3 (SU antitriplet and triplet, respectively) stand for the light quark content of charmed and anticharmed mesons. Therefore, at LO, there are only nℓ×nf=4subscript𝑛ℓsubscript𝑛𝑓4nelltimes nf=4 independent constant contact terms, which are denoted here as 𝒞0​a,𝒞0​b,𝒞1​asubscript𝒞0𝑎subscript𝒞0𝑏subscript𝒞1𝑎C0a,C0b,C1a and 𝒞1​bsubscript𝒞1𝑏C1b. For more details we refer to Ref. citebib.bib27, though we will explicitly construct below the LO Lagrangian. In brief, isoscalar molecules (I=0,S=0formulae-sequence𝐼0𝑆0I=0,S=0) without hidden strangeness can be described with 𝒞0​asubscript𝒞0𝑎C0a and 𝒞0​bsubscript𝒞0𝑏C0b. Isospinor (I=1/2,S=±1formulae-sequence𝐼12𝑆plus-or-minus1I=1/2,S=pm 1) and isovector (I=1,S=0formulae-sequence𝐼1𝑆0I=1,S=0) octet states are in turn determined by 𝒞1​asubscript𝒞1𝑎C1a and 𝒞1​bsubscript𝒞1𝑏C1b. Finally, for molecular states with hidden strangeness, the contact range interactions are the average of the isoscalar and isovector ones. content_sections.III Extraction of the pole position from: Although the mass in Eq. is above the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- threshold, 2​mDs=(3936.70±0.14)2subscript𝑚subscript𝐷𝑠plus-or-minus3936.700.142mDs=(3936.70pm 0.14)MeV, the peak in the immediate vicinity of the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- threshold indicates that it may also be compatible with a below-threshold pole, which can be either a virtual or bound state. From the LHCb measured spectrum, we can see that no additional structures in Ds±​K±superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾plus-or-minusDspmKpm channels are needed.4In fact, the higher D0∗superscriptsubscript𝐷0D0* state with a mass about 2.45GeV claimed in Refs. citebib.bib68, bib.bib69 couples dominantly to the Ds​K¯subscript𝐷𝑠¯𝐾DsbarK. It was shown in Ref. citebib.bib70 that the existence of such a state and a lower D0∗superscriptsubscript𝐷0D0* one, with a mass around 2.1GeV, is consistent with the LHCb data on the D​π𝐷𝜋Dpi angular momentscitebib.bib71. One notices that there is some hint of a near-threshold enhancement in the LHCb data of the Ds−​K+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾Ds-K+ invariant mass distributioncitebib.bib56. Averaging the differential decay width for each experimental bin and fitting to the LHCb data, we obtain the interaction strength of Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- and, in turn, the pole position of this system. The fitted distribution with Λ=0.5Λ0.5Lambda=0.5 GeV is shown in Fig. . Actually there are two solutions, one which develops a bound state pole, and the other one with a virtual state pole, but they are almost indistinguishable, which follows from the fact that there are only data points above the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- threshold.5The pole is located on the real axis below Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs threshold in the single-channel case, and it corresponds to a bound or virtual state of Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs. If the couplings to the D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD or the OZI suppressed J/ψ​ω𝐽𝜓𝜔J/psiomega lower-energy channels are considered, the pole will move to the complex plane becoming a resonance. Abusing the notation, we will continue to call such pole a bound or virtual state, since it would have essentially the same nature as in the single channel case—it is formed by the attraction between the Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs mesons. The fit with Λ=1.0Λ1.0Lambda=1.0 GeV has the same quality and leads to similar pole positions, as seen in Table. With the binding or virtual energy given in Table, we get a mass of the Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- bound or virtual state of MX​=(3928±3)​MeV. subscript𝑀𝑋3960plus-or-minus39283MeVdisplaystyle MX=(3928pm 3)rm MeV. The lattice QCD calculation in Ref. citebib.bib58 predicts the existence of a Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- bound state with a binding energy in the range of [2.4, 8.2]MeV,6The authors of Ref. citebib.bib58 obtain two shallow bound states when they consider separately the D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD and Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs single channels, which agree with our results. Then, they find that the masses slightly change by including D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD–Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs coupled-channel effects, which gives support to perform single-channel analyses. In addition, they also found a third resonance above the Ds​D¯ssubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠DsbarDs threshold, which appears due to the use of an energy-dependent potential, and a D𝐷D-wave 2++superscript2absent2++ state. These two latter results are beyond the scope of this work, where we will only consider S𝑆S-wave constant interactions. and the model in Ref. citebib.bib48, which assumes that the interaction between a pair of charmed hadrons is dominated by the exchange of light-flavor vector mesons, predicts a Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- virtual state with a virtual energy in the range of [4.7, 35.5]MeV. Both predictions are consistent with the determinations in Table.

content_sections.V Summary and discussions: Using the LECs determined in the previous section, we obtain the complete spectrum of the S𝑆S-wave H​H¯𝐻¯𝐻HbarH hadronic molecules, which is collected in Tables, , and and shown in Fig. . Some further remarks on the estimation of errors in these tables are given in Appendix . We see that several HQSS/SU siblings of the X​𝑋3872X, Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zc and X​𝑋3960X resonances are predicted. Some of them might not be real QCD bound states, but rather virtual state poles, which will nevertheless produce some nontrivial structures in the experimental distributions of H​H¯𝐻¯𝐻HbarH or other final states that can couple to H​H¯𝐻¯𝐻HbarH (for a discussion on some general features of the virtual state line shapes, see Ref. citebib.bib48). The SU content of the found spectrum is determined from the flavour decomposition [3¯]⊗=⊕tensor-productdelimited-¯3delimited-3direct-sumdelimited-1delimited-8[bar3]otimes=oplus. Let us pay, for instance, attention to the pseudoescalar-vector JP​C=1+−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1+- sector. The octet interaction can be read off from that in the I=1𝐼1I=1 or I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 isospin channelscitebib.bib27, bib.bib46, bib.bib52 and it is determined by the linear combination [𝒞1​a−𝒞1​b]delimited-subscript𝒞1𝑎subscript𝒞1𝑏[C1a-C1b] (see Tables and ). Hence, the obtained molecular-like exotic states Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zc and Zc​s​subscript𝑍𝑐𝑠3985Zcs belong to the same octetcitebib.bib46, bib.bib52, as depicted in Fig. . For I=0𝐼0I=0, one has the LECs (𝒞0​a−𝒞0​b)subscript𝒞0𝑎subscript𝒞0𝑏(C0a-C0b) and 12​(𝒞0​a−𝒞0​b+𝒞1​a−𝒞1​b)12subscript𝒞0𝑎subscript𝒞0𝑏subscript𝒞1𝑎subscript𝒞1𝑏frac12left(C0a-C0b+C1a-C1bright) for the D∗​D¯superscript𝐷∗¯𝐷DastbarD and Ds∗​D¯ssuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠Ds*barDs (hidden strangeness) pairs, respectively. In the SU limit, it would be necessary to account for the coupled-channel dynamics, and the I=0𝐼0I=0 potential would diagonalize into octet and singlet components. The eigenvalue of the octet piece would be (𝒞1​a−𝒞1​b)subscript𝒞1𝑎subscript𝒞1𝑏(C1a-C1b), and it would give rise to the I=0𝐼0I=0 member of the octet in Fig. . Hence due the single-channel scheme used here, appropriate because of the sizable Ds(∗)−D(∗)subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐷D(*)s-D(*) mass splitting (see discussion below), neither the D∗​D¯superscript𝐷∗¯𝐷DastbarD nor the Ds∗​D¯ssuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠Ds*barDs ​1+−0superscript1absent1+- molecular-like states reported in Tables and can be directly identified with the SU octet and singlet isoscalar states missing in Fig. . The former ones will be linear combinations of the SU flavor eigenstates, similarly to that, for instance, the physical ω𝜔omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi mesons are obtained from the mixing of the isoscalar SU singlet and octet states. One would find a second JP​C=1+−superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1+- nonet (octet+singlet) of HQSS siblings of the latter one in the vector-vector sector, where the charged Zc∗​subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑐4020Z*c and Zc​s∗subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑐𝑠Z*cs would be located. The same SU/HQSS pattern would be repeated for the JP​C=1++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript1absentJPC=1++ pseudoscalar-vector and JP​C=2++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript2absentJPC=2++ vector-vector systems, and finally there would be two (pseudoscalar-pseudoscalar and vector-vector) independent JP​C=0++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentJPC=0++ nonets. Nevertheless, in some cases the interaction might not be strong enough to even generate a virtual state, close enough to the relevant threshold, that can produce non-negligible signatures in observable distributions. To summarize, we have shown that the freshly measured Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- invariant mass distribution in the B+→Ds+​Ds−​K+→superscript𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾B+to Ds+Ds-K+ reaction, reported by the LHCb Collaborationcitebib.bib56, can be well described by a Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- bound or virtual state below threshold. The pole is located at (3928±3)plus-or-minus39283(3928pm 3)MeV on the first or second RS for a bound or a virtual state, respectively. Thus, the observed peak in the data above threshold can be just a consequence of the phase-space suppression. In this and other similar situations, the line shape in the near-threshold region should be analyzed using a parametrization which can be analytically continued to the below-threshold region (a nice example in this context is provided by the LHCb analysis of the X​𝑋3872X line shape using the Flattè parametrizationcitebib.bib61). The pole position obtained from the analytically-extended amplitude in such analyses is always more meaningful than the BW parameters. This possible new Ds+​Ds−superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+Ds- molecule [X​𝑋3960X], together with inputs from the mass and isospin breaking decays of the X​𝑋3872X, and the virtual state pole position of the Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zc determined in Ref. citebib.bib52, have allowed us to fully fix the four LECs that appear in the 4​H4𝐻4H NREFT Lagrangian at LO (ℒ4​Hsubscriptℒ4𝐻L4H). Next, we have used this contact-range interaction to predict the complete spectrum of hadronic molecules formed by an S𝑆S-wave pair of ground state charmed and anticharmed mesons. It is expected that coupling channels, with the same quantum numbers and thresholds more than 100MeV away from each other, can bring in corrections to the numerical results, but the general pattern should hold; see the analyses in Ref. citebib.bib23 for H​H¯𝐻¯𝐻HbarH systems in the SU flavor case and Ref. citebib.bib73 for the hidden-charm pentaquarks. A similar remark applies to the effects of pion exchanges, which are also not considered in this work. We have regarded near-threshold virtual states as molecular states despite that their wave functions are not normalizable. It is worthwhile to notice that the wave function of a resonance, whose poles are located on the unphysical Riemann sheet of the complex energy plane just like a virtual state pole, is not normalizable either. Although both virtual states and resonances are not asymptotic states, they are able to produce observable signatures, such as peaks in invariant mass distributions, as long as the poles are not far from the physical region. In particular, it is important to stress that the pole position of a virtual state, as that of a resonance or a bound state, is the same in every reaction with the same quantum numbers and initial or final state. Thus, a virtual state may be regarded as a particle just like what is normally done for a resonance on the same footing. Notice that we have ignored higher order interactions and coupled-channel dynamics. In principle, coupled channels may have some effects on the spectrum. However, in this work we have mainly focused on poles near thresholds, for which only considering single-channel dynamics should be a good approximation, since the energy-distance to the corresponding threshold is much smaller than the separation to the closest energy channel.10The typical momentum scale of the coupled channel is 2​μ​mπ∼similar-to2𝜇subscript𝑚𝜋absentsqrt2mu mpisim 500 MeV, with μ𝜇mu the heavy-light meson pair reduced mass and (mD∗−mD)∼mπsimilar-tosubscript𝑚superscript𝐷subscript𝑚𝐷subscript𝑚𝜋(mD*-mD)sim mpi, which turns out to be a hard scale of the order of the ultraviolet cutoff, and much larger than the binding momentum of a shallow S𝑆S-wave bound or virtual state. Note that the Ds−Dsubscript𝐷𝑠𝐷Ds-D mass splitting, though smaller than (mD∗−mD)subscript𝑚superscript𝐷subscript𝑚𝐷(mD*-mD), is still of around 100 MeV. On the other hand, we should point out that the lowest-order counterterm structure stemming from the NREFT cannot absorb the kind of divergences associated with the coupled channel calculations and one would need to introduce higher-order new counterterms to renormalize the coupled channel dynamics. These problems were addressed in Ref. citebib.bib23, and shown there that in any case the effects should be safely covered by uncertainties coming from the 1/mQ1subscript𝑚𝑄1/mQ corrections. See also Ref. citebib.bib73 for discussions in the case of the pentaquark Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc. In this case, the poles are always located at real axis below the threshold. If we had taken into account lower-energy channels, the near threshold poles would have only moved to the complex plane, which rarely affects the mass of the state. Molecular states can also in principle couple to near-threshold q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq components if their quantum numbers are the same. The recent analysis in Ref. citebib.bib74 suggests that it is plausible to treat hadronic molecules isolated from q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states of the same quantum numbers. Nevertheless, the interactions driven by q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq compact states located some few tens of MeV below/above thresholds can be incorporated into the EFT by appropriate LECs, when only narrow energy windows around some relevant two-meson thresholds are being considered. This is because then the energy-dependence of the interaction can be safely neglected. In this context, the study carried out in Ref. citebib.bib41 for the X​𝑋3872X and its 2++superscript2absent2++ HQSS partner is very illustrative.

all_papers.title: Enhanced breaking of heavy quark spin symmetry
all_papers.abstract:
Heavy quark spin symmetry is useful to make predictions on ratios of decay or
production rates of systems involving heavy quarks. The breaking of spin
symmetry is generally of the order of $O({\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/m_Q})$, with
$\Lambda_{\rm QCD}$ the scale of QCD and $m_Q$ the heavy quark mass. In this
paper, we will show that a small $S$- and $D$-wave mixing in the wave function
of the heavy quarkonium could induce a large breaking in the ratios of partial
decay widths. As an example, we consider the decays of the $\Upsilon(10860)$
into the $\chi_{bJ}\omega\, (J=0,1,2)$, which were recently measured by the
Belle Collaboration. These decays exhibit a huge breaking of the spin symmetry
relation were the $\Upsilon(10860)$ a pure $5S$ bottomonium state. We propose
that this could be a consequence of a mixing of the $S$-wave and $D$-wave
components in the $\Upsilon(10860)$. Prediction on the ratio
$\Gamma(\Upsilon(10860)\to\chi_{b0}\omega)/\Gamma(\Upsilon(10860)\to\chi_{b2}\omega)$
is presented assuming that the decay of the $D$-wave component is dominated by
the coupled-channel effects.
all_papers.reason:
论文紧扣重夸克自旋对称性（HQSS）主题，系统分析其破缺机制，明确讨论S-D波混合作用导致的自旋对称性显著破缺，与查询高度契合。研究对象为Υ(10860)衰变中χ_bJω的分支比，直接关联HQSS预测的偏差，且提出理论解释，完全满足核心任务与领域要求。

约束项检查：

- C2：分析衰变过程中的对称性破缺机制 — 状态：满足。证据：“large breaking in the ratios of partial decay widths” 对比 “HQSS”  
- C3：基于实验数据（Belle测量）提出理论解释 — 状态：满足。证据：“recently measured by the Belle Collaboration” 对比 “HQSS”

all_papers.title:
Analysis of $DD^*$ and $\bar{D}^{(*)}\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ molecule by one
  boson exchange model based on Heavy quark symmetry
all_papers.abstract:
Numerous exotic hadrons with heavy quarks have been reported in the
experiments. In such states, symmetries of heavy quarks are considered to play
a significant role. In particular, the superflavor symmetry, or also called the
heavy quark anti-diquark symmetry is one of the interesting symmetries, which
links a quark $Q$ to an anti-diquark $\bar{Q}\bar{Q}$ having the same color
representation as $Q$. In this paper, we study a $\bar{D}\Xi_{cc}$ molecular
state as a superflavor partner of the doubly charm tetraquark $T_{cc}$ reported
by LHCb recently. $T_{cc}$ locating slightly below the $DD^*$ threshold is a
candidate of the hadronic molecule. Thus by replacing the singly charm meson
$D^{(*)}$ with the doubly charm baryon $\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$, superflavor symmetry
predicts the existence of the $\bar{D}^{(*)}\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ bound state. We
employ the one boson exchange model respecting with the heavy quark spin
symmetry where the parameter is obtained to reproduce the $T_{cc}$ binding
energy. We apply this model with superflavor symmetry to the
$\bar{D}^{(*)}\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ molecule and predict a bound state with $I(J^P) =
0(\frac{1}{2}^-)$. If the pentaquark state corresponding to $\bar{D}^{(*)}
\Xi_{cc}^{(*)}$ molecular state is observed in future experiments as predicted
in this work, it is more likely that $T_{cc}$ is a $DD^*$ molecular state.

sections.5: Gell-Mann, A Schematic Model of Baryons and Mesons, Phys
sections.12: Zweig, An SU(3) model for strong interaction symmetry and its breaking
sections.17: Suzuki, The X(3872) boson: Molecule or charmonium, Phys

qualitative: Heavy quark spin symmetry (HQSS) arises due to the suppression of heavy quark spin interactions by a factor of 𝒪(1/mQ), leading to approximate degeneracy in masses of heavy-light meson spin multiplets.

HQSS implies that the mass differences between pseudoscalar (spin-0) and vector (spin-1) mesons are small, as seen in the charm sector where the mass difference between D* and D is about 140 MeV, comparable to the pion mass.
HQSS is used to justify coupled-channel analyses involving D and D* mesons (or Ξcc and Ξcc* baryons) by treating them as nearly degenerate states, allowing for simplified interaction models.
The symmetry is respected in the one boson exchange model (OBEM) used in this study to describe both the Tcc tetraquark and its superflavor partner D̄(∗)Ξcc(∗) molecule.
quantitative: Mass difference between D* and D mesons: mD∗ − mD ≃ 140 MeV
Mass difference between Ξcc* and Ξcc baryons: mΞcc∗ − mΞcc ≃ 105 MeV
Binding energy of Tcc from Breit-Wigner fit: δmBW = −273 ± 61 ± 5+11−14 keV
Pole position of Tcc: δmpole = −360 ± 40+4−0 keV
Decay width from Breit-Wigner: ΓBW = 410 ± 165 ± 43+18−38 keV
Pole decay width: Γpole = 48 ± 2+0−14 keV

content_sections.I Introduction: Hadrons are composite particles held together by strong interaction and almost all of them can be classified into baryons (q​q​q𝑞𝑞𝑞qqq) and mesons (q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq). However, in recent years, exotic hadrons that cannot be classified as baryons or mesons have been extensively studied due to many observations of such particles. The existence of exotic hadrons was already suggested by Gell-Manncitebib.bib1 and Zewigcitebib.bib2 at the early stage of the quark model. In the charm sector, the first candidate of the exotic state, X​𝑋3872X, was reported in 2003citebib.bib3, triggering further studies of exotic hadronscitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, such as X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ tetraquarks and Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc pentaquarks. In 2021, LHCb collaboration reported the observation of Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ near the D​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷DD* thresholdcitebib.bib7, which is the first doubly charm tetraquark (c​c​u¯​d¯𝑐𝑐¯𝑢¯𝑑ccbarubard). The observed state is consistent with an isoscalar state with spin-parity quantum numbers JP=1+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript1JP=1+. In the first reportcitebib.bib7, the mass and decay width of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ were obtained by using the Breit-Wigner parametrisations as δ​mB​W=−273±61±5−14+11𝛿subscript𝑚𝐵𝑊plus-or-minus27361subscriptsuperscript51114delta mBW=-273pm 61pm 5+11-14 keV and ΓB​W=410±165±43−38+18subscriptΓ𝐵𝑊plus-or-minus410165subscriptsuperscript431838GammaBW=410pm 165pm 43+18-38 keV, respectively, where the mass is measured from the D∗+​D0superscript𝐷absentsuperscript𝐷0D*+D0 threshold. In Ref. citebib.bib8, the model analysis accounting for the relevant thresholds was done, where the pole position was found as δ​mpole=−360±40−0+4𝛿subscript𝑚poleplus-or-minus360subscriptsuperscript4040delta mrm pole=-360pm 40+4-0 keV, and Γpole=48±2−14+0subscriptΓpoleplus-or-minus48subscriptsuperscript2014Gammarm pole=48pm 2+0-14 keV. Doubly heavy tetraquarks Q​Q​q¯​q¯𝑄𝑄¯𝑞¯𝑞QQbarqbarq including Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc have been studied theoretically in literaturecitebib.bib6. In the 1980s, a genuine exotic doubly heavy tetraquark was discussed in Refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13. Doubly heavy tetraquarks as compact multiquarks have been studied by various approaches such as the (di)quark modelcitebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16. The hadronic moleculescitebib.bib17 as a two-meson composite state have also been investigated, which have been expected to be realized near thresholds. In fact, the mass of Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc reported by LHCb is just below the D​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷DD* threshold. There have been many studies of doubly heavy tetraquarks as hadron molecule by using the meson exchange model citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, the effective field theorycitebib.bib22, and so on. Lattice QCD simulation has also been a useful approach to investigate the exotic hadrons from QCD, where Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc tetraquarks have been studied in Refs. citebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28. Observations of exotic hadrons containing heavy quarks indicate that the understanding of these states is greatly aided by the concept of heavy quark symmetry. Specifically, the heavy quark spin interaction is suppressed by a factor of 𝒪​(1/mQ)𝒪1subscript𝑚𝑄O(1/mQ), which induces Heavy Quark Spin Symmetry (HQSS) (see, for a review Ref. citebib.bib29). This symmetry leads to the heavy quark spin multiplet structure. In a case of heavy-light mesons, mass differences of a pseudoscalar meson with spin 00 and a vector meson with spin 111 are (approximately) suppressed. Actually in the charm quark sector, the mass difference between D∗​superscript𝐷2008D* and D​𝐷1867D is about 140140140 MeV, which is as small as mπsubscript𝑚𝜋mpi. Additionally, there exists an interesting symmetry which is the superflavor symmetry, or also called the heavy quark anti-diquark symmetry. This symmetry arises from the replacement of a heavy quark Q𝑄Q with a heavy anti-diquark Q¯​Q¯¯𝑄¯𝑄barQbarQ having the same color representation 3csubscript3𝑐3c as Q𝑄Qcitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, indicating that D¯​(Q¯​q)¯𝐷¯𝑄𝑞barD(barQq) and Ξc​c​(Q​Q​q)subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐𝑄𝑄𝑞Xicc(QQq) is related. Thus, the superflavor symmetry predicts an existence of multi-heavy hadronic states corresponding to the D¯(∗)superscript¯𝐷barD(*) meson molecules by replacing D¯(∗)superscript¯𝐷barD(*) mesons with Ξc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc(*) baryons. In this paper, we study a D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) molecular state as a superflavor partner of the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc tetraquark assumed to be a D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) molecule. We firstly construct a model to describe Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc reported by LHCb as the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) molecule with I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+). The one boson exchange model (OBEM) is employed as for the interactions between D(∗)superscript𝐷D(*) mesons, where a cutoff parameter ΛΛLambda in OBEM is determined to reproduce the experimental data of Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc. As the binding energy of Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc referred in this study, we employ the Breit-Wigner mass δ​mBW𝛿subscript𝑚BWdelta mrm BW given in the first report of LHCb. However In this study, we employ the Breit-Wigner mass δ​mBW𝛿subscript𝑚BWdelta mrm BW as the binding energy of Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc. As far as the bound-state problem is considered, the qualitative results do not change if the pole mass δ​mpole𝛿subscript𝑚poledelta mrm pole is employed. there are no qualitative difference between cases employing δ​mBW𝛿subscript𝑚BWdelta mrm BW and δ​mpole𝛿subscript𝑚poledelta mrm pole. We conduct a coupled-channel analysis of D​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷DD* and D∗​D∗superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D*D* respecting HQSS due to the small mass difference of mD∗−mD≃140similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑚superscript𝐷subscript𝑚𝐷140mD*-mDsimeq 140 MeV. We expand this model into a D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) molecular state by using superflavor symmetry. We obtain effective Lagrangians for Ξc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc(*), where the coupling constants are estimated from those of the D(∗)superscript𝐷D(*) mesons based on superflavor symmetry. The coupled-channel analysis including the possible D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) channels are performed, respecting HQSS due to the small mass difference of mΞc​c∗subscript𝑚subscriptsuperscriptΞ𝑐𝑐mXi*cc and mΞc​csubscript𝑚subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐mXicc, about 105105105 MeV. We study a D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) bound state as a superflavor partner of the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc state being the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) molecule. We also discuss the breaking effect of superflavor symmetry by changing the value of the coupling constants. The paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , we study the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) molecule. We construct the OBEM to describe the reported Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc. In Sec. , the OBEM is applied to the D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) molecule. We study a possible existence of the D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) bound states as a superflavor partner of the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) molecule. Finally Sec. is devoted to the summary.

In Refs. citebib.bib39, bib.bib40, gσsubscript𝑔𝜎gsigma is estimated as gσ=gσ​N​N3=3.4subscript𝑔𝜎subscript𝑔𝜎𝑁𝑁33.4gsigma=fracgsigma NN3=3.4 with the sigma-nucleon coupling constant gσ​N​Nsubscript𝑔𝜎𝑁𝑁gsigma NN. The factor of 1/3131/3 is chosen by assuming that the σ𝜎sigma meson couples to light quarks inside hadrons. The nucleon contains three light quarks, while the D(∗)superscript𝐷∗D(ast) meson does one light quark and one anti heavy quark. Alternatively, in Refs. citebib.bib41, bib.bib42, the small value of the sigma coupling is obtained as gσ=gπ/2​6=0.76subscript𝑔𝜎subscript𝑔𝜋260.76gsigma=gpi/2sqrt6=0.76, where gπ∼3.73similar-tosubscript𝑔𝜋3.73gpisim 3.73 is given by the Goldberger-Treiman relation and mDs∗−mDs∼349similar-tosubscript𝑚superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗subscript𝑚subscript𝐷𝑠349mDsast-mDssim 349 MeVcitebib.bib43. For the hadrons masses, we employ the isospin average of the masses listed in the PDGcitebib.bib44. From those Lagrangians, we derive the OBEP in momentum space using the formula V​(𝒒)=i​ℳΠi​2​mi​Πf​2​mf,𝑉𝒒𝑖ℳsubscriptΠ𝑖2subscript𝑚𝑖subscriptΠ𝑓2subscript𝑚𝑓displaystyle V(q)=fraciMsqrtPii2miPif2mf,, where ℳℳM is the t𝑡t-channel scattering amplitude of the meson exchange process, and misubscript𝑚𝑖mi and mfsubscript𝑚𝑓mf are masses of initial and final hadrons, respectively. To account for the finite size of hadrons, we introduce a monopole-type form factor F​(𝒒)=Λ2−μe​f​f2Λ2+𝒒2,𝐹𝒒superscriptΛ2superscriptsubscript𝜇𝑒𝑓𝑓2superscriptΛ2superscript𝒒2displaystyle F(q)=fracLambda2-mueff2Lambda2+q2,, where the cutoff parameter ΛΛLambda is a free parameter in our model. In this work, we determine the value to reproduce the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc experimental data (δmB​W≃0.273similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝛿subscript𝑚𝐵𝑊0.273deltamBWsimeq 0.273 MeV). μe​f​fsubscript𝜇𝑒𝑓𝑓mueff is the effective mass defined as μe​f​f2=me​x2−(q0)2superscriptsubscript𝜇𝑒𝑓𝑓2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑒𝑥2superscriptsuperscript𝑞02mueff2=mex2-(q0)2, where me​xsubscript𝑚𝑒𝑥mex is the mass of the exchanged meson and q0superscript𝑞0q0 is the zeroth component of the momentum given by q0≃m22−m12+m32−m422​(m3+m4)similar-to-or-equalssuperscript𝑞0superscriptsubscript𝑚22superscriptsubscript𝑚12superscriptsubscript𝑚32superscriptsubscript𝑚422subscript𝑚3subscript𝑚4q0simeqfracm22-m12+m32-m422left(m3+m4right) citebib.bib42. In the case for μe​f​f2<0superscriptsubscript𝜇𝑒𝑓𝑓20mueff2<0, the potential has an imaginary part implying a decay into the D​D​π𝐷𝐷𝜋DDpi channelcitebib.bib45, bib.bib4, bib.bib21. Since we focus on the bound state problem in this study, we take only the real part of the potential. The OBEP in coordinate space is obtained by the Fourier transformation V​(r)=1(2​π)3​∫d3​q​V​(𝒒)​[F​(𝒒)]2. 𝑉𝑟1superscript2𝜋3superscript𝑑3𝑞𝑉𝒒superscriptdelimited-𝐹𝒒2displaystyle V(r)=frac1(2pi)3int d3qV(q)[F(q)]2,. We note that the contact term given by the delta function in the central part of the potential is subtracted in this paper. We solve the coupled-channel Schrödinger equations with the obtained potentials using the Gaussian expansion methodcitebib.bib46, and study a bound state of the D​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷∗DDast molecule. By using appropriate value of the cutoff ΛΛLambda, we obtain a bound state. First we employ the large value of the sigma coupling gσ=gσ=3.4subscript𝑔𝜎superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎13.4gsigma=gsigma=3.4. Numerical results of bound states with various values of ΛΛLambda are summarized in Table. The table presents the values of ΛΛLambda, the binding energy, the probability of each channel, and the root mean square (RMS) radius. By setting Λ≃1182similar-to-or-equalsΛ1182Lambdasimeq 1182 MeV, we reproduce the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc binding energy in the experimental data. The radial wave function and the matrix of the Hamiltonian expectation value with this cutoff are shown in Figs. and , respectively. As evidenced by the wave function plot in Fig. and the probability in Table, the DD∗(3S1)DD*(3S1) channel dominates, which is the lowest threshold channel. In Fig, it is found that the mixing between the D​D∗​(S)𝐷superscript𝐷𝑆DD*(S) and D​D∗​(D)𝐷superscript𝐷𝐷DD*(D) channels driven by the tensor force plays a significant role in producing the bound state. Furthermore, the mixing between D​D∗​(S)𝐷superscript𝐷𝑆DD*(S) and D∗​D∗​(S)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷𝑆D*D*(S), as well as D∗​D∗​(D)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷𝐷D*D*(D), also contributes to the binding. These results indicate that the D∗​D∗superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D*D* channel should be included in the D​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷∗DDast molecular state, because of HQSS and the D​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷DD* mixing effect. Actually, the coupling constant of σ𝜎sigma meson is ambiguous and the small value of gσ=0.76superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎20.76gsigma=0.76citebib.bib47 has also been used in the literature. We consider the case with the σ𝜎sigma coupling and perform the coupled-channel analysis again. We obtain the cutoff Λ=1631Λ1631Lambda=1631 MeV to reproduce the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc binding energy. In this model, the larger cutoff ΛΛLambda is needed to reproduce the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc data due to the weaker σ𝜎sigma meson exchange. However, we obtain that the qualitative properties of the bound state are the same as those of the case with gσ=3.4superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎13.4gsigma=3.4. The bound state properties, wave functions and energy expectation values are summarized in Table and Figs. and , respectively.
content_sections.III D¯​Ξc​c¯𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐\\bar{D}\\Xi_{c: In the previous section, we study the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc state as a D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) molecule. With respect to superflavor symmetrycitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, we expect that there should be a D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) state that is a partner of D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) by replacing a charm quark as c¯​c¯¯𝑐¯𝑐barcbarc diquark1We note that the superflavor partner of D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) should be D(∗)​Ξ¯c​c(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscriptsubscript¯Ξ𝑐𝑐∗D(ast)barXicc(ast). In this paper, however, we study D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) with C=+1𝐶1C=+1 rather than the anti-charm state D(∗)​Ξ¯c​c(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscriptsubscript¯Ξ𝑐𝑐∗D(ast)barXicc(ast). These states are equivalent each other by charge conjugation. We study a coupled-channel analysis of D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) molecular state, where with respect to HQSS, the mixing of the HQS doublet states, D¯¯𝐷barD and D¯∗superscript¯𝐷∗barDast for mesons and Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc and Ξc​c∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗Xiccast for baryons, are introduced. Thus, we consider the following coupled channels: (D¯Ξc​c(2S12)D¯Ξc​c∗(4D12)D¯∗Ξc​c(2S12,4D12)D¯∗Ξc​c∗(2S12,4D12,6D12)),displaystylebeginpmatrixbarDXicc(2Sfrac12) barDXicc*(4Dfrac12) barD*Xicc(2Sfrac12,4Dfrac12) barD*Xicc*(2Sfrac12,4Dfrac12,6Dfrac12)endpmatrix, including possible mixing between S𝑆S and D𝐷D wave states by the tensor force. The D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) system has more channels than the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) one shown in Eq. . We define a doubly heavy baryon doublet field ψQ​Qμsubscriptsuperscript𝜓𝜇𝑄𝑄psimuQQ which is a linear combination of double heavy baryons ℬQ​Q​(12+)subscriptℬ𝑄𝑄superscript12BQQ(frac12+) and ℬQ​Q∗μ​(32+)subscriptsuperscriptℬabsent𝜇𝑄𝑄superscript32B*muQQ(frac32+) degenerating in HQ limit as followscitebib.bib48 ψQ​Qμsuperscriptsubscript𝜓𝑄𝑄𝜇displaystylepsiQQmu =ℬQ​Q∗μ+13​(γμ+vμ)​γ5​ℬQ​Q,absentsubscriptsuperscriptℬabsent𝜇𝑄𝑄13superscript𝛾𝜇superscript𝑣𝜇superscript𝛾5subscriptℬ𝑄𝑄displaystyle=B*muQQ+sqrtfrac13(gammamu+vmu)gamma5BQQ, ψ¯Q​Qμsuperscriptsubscript¯𝜓𝑄𝑄𝜇displaystylebarpsiQQmu =ℬ¯Q​Q∗μ−13​ℬ¯Q​Q​γ5​(γμ+vμ),absentsubscriptsuperscript¯ℬabsent𝜇𝑄𝑄13subscript¯ℬ𝑄𝑄superscript𝛾5superscript𝛾𝜇superscript𝑣𝜇displaystyle=barB*muQQ-sqrtfrac13barBQQgamma5(gammamu+vmu), where ℬQ​Q=Λ+​BQ​Qsubscriptℬ𝑄𝑄subscriptΛsubscript𝐵𝑄𝑄BQQ=Lambda+BQQ, and ℬQ​Q∗μ=Λ+​BQ​Q∗μsubscriptsuperscriptℬabsent𝜇𝑄𝑄subscriptΛsubscriptsuperscript𝐵absent𝜇𝑄𝑄B*muQQ=Lambda+B*muQQ with the positive-energy projection operator Λ+=1+/v2subscriptΛ1/𝑣2Lambda+=frac1+ooalignhfil/hfilcrcrv2. BQ​Qsubscript𝐵𝑄𝑄BQQ is Dirac spinor, while BQ​Q∗μsubscriptsuperscript𝐵absent𝜇𝑄𝑄B*muQQ is Rarita-Schwinger spinor. In the ground state of doubly heavy baryons, the spin of the heavy diquark Q​Q𝑄𝑄QQ must be 111 due to the Fermi-Dirac statistics. Therefore, the total spin of the doubly heavy baryon is given by combination of the Q​Q𝑄𝑄QQ spin 1 and the rest light quark spin 1212frac12, i. e. , 1⊗12=12⊕32tensor-product112direct-sum12321otimesfrac12=frac12oplusfrac32. These two spin states are degenerate in HQ limit, and correspond to Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc and Ξc​c∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc* in the charm sector. We utilize the experimental value of the Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc mass obtained by the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib49, while the mass of Ξc​c∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc* with no data is estimated by using the quark model mass relation, 34​(mΞc​c∗−mΞc​c)=mD∗−mD34subscript𝑚superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐subscript𝑚subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐subscript𝑚superscript𝐷subscript𝑚𝐷frac34(mXicc*-mXicc)=mD*-mDcitebib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52. This yields mΞc​c≃3727similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑚subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐3727mXiccsimeq 3727 MeV. We employ the effective Lagrangians satisfying HQSS and S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU flavour symmetry. The values of the coupling constants between Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc and π,σ,ρ,ω𝜋𝜎𝜌𝜔pi,sigma,rho,omega are unknown due to a lack of experimental data. Therefore, we estimate the coupling constants based on the superflavor symmetry by using those of D¯¯𝐷barD as a reference. Since the quark configuration of D¯(∗)superscript¯𝐷barD(*) and Ξc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc(*) is Q¯​q¯𝑄𝑞barQq and Q​Q​q𝑄𝑄𝑞QQq, respectively, the heavy quark objects should have the same color source represented by the color anti triplet to make a color singlet hadron, i. e. Q¯∼3¯similar-to¯𝑄¯3barQsimbar3 representation and Q​Q∼3¯similar-to𝑄𝑄¯3QQsimbar3 (we note Q⊗Q∼3¯⊗3¯=3¯⊕6similar-totensor-product𝑄𝑄tensor-product¯3¯3direct-sum¯36Qotimes Qsimbar3otimesbar3=bar3oplus 6). The heavy diquark is a sizable object with finite mass, but it becomes a point-like particle in the HQ limit. Thus, the heavy diquark can be identified with anti-heavy quark as a point-like static colour source in the HQ limit. Thus we obtain the coupling constants of Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc to light hadrons from the D¯¯𝐷barD coupling constants by the superflavor symmetry. In OBEM, only the light quark is involved in the interaction of boson exchange and the light quark structures in D¯¯𝐷barD and Ξc​c∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc* are the same in the HQ limit. Thus the coupling constants of Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc are given by those of D¯¯𝐷barD as g𝑔displaystylehatg =2​g,absent2𝑔displaystyle=2g, gσsubscript𝑔𝜎displaystylehatgsigma =2​gσ,absent2subscript𝑔𝜎displaystyle=2gsigma, β𝛽displaystylehatbeta =2​β,absent2𝛽displaystyle=2beta, λ𝜆displaystylehatlambda =2​λ. absent2𝜆displaystyle=2lambda. We note that the factor of 2 is from the normalization factorcitebib.bib53. We also use the same cutoff parameter obtained in the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) study fitted to reproduce the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc data. By solving coupled-channel Schrödinger equations using Gaussian expansion method, we obtain a bound state of D¯​Ξc​c¯𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barDXicc for I​(JP)=0​(1/2−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃01superscript2I(JP)=0(1/2-). The numerical results with gσ=gσ=3.4subscript𝑔𝜎superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎13.4gsigma=gsigma=3.4 are summarized in Table. The radial wave function and the matrix of the hamiltonian expectation value are also shown in Figs. and , respectively. The probabilities in Table show that the dominant component of the wave functions is in the D¯Ξc​c(2S12)barDXicc(2Sfrac12) channel with PD¯​Ξc​c(2S12)∼99%PbarDXicc(2Sfrac12)sim 99%, which is also shown in the plot of the wave functions in Fig. . Interestingly, the second dominant component is the D¯∗Ξc​c∗(6D12)barD*Xicc*(6Dfrac12) channel with PD¯∗​Ξc​c∗(6D12)∼1%PbarD*Xicc*(6Dfrac12)sim 1%, while the threshold energy is far from the D¯​Ξc​c¯𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barDXicc channel and furthermore it is a D𝐷D-wave state. The important contribution of the D¯∗Ξc​c∗(6D12)barD*Xicc*(6Dfrac12) channel is indicated in the matrix of the energy expectation value in Fig. . The off-diagonal component between the D¯Ξc​c(2S12)−D¯∗Ξc​c∗(6D12)barDXicc(2Sfrac12)-barD*Xicc*(6Dfrac12) channels contributes to a strong attraction which is produced by the tensor force. It’s turned out that this attraction plays an important role to generate the bound state. In contrast, for the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) molecule, both the DD∗(3S1)−DD∗(3D1)DD*(3S1)-DD*(3D1) and D​D∗−D∗​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷superscript𝐷superscript𝐷DD*-D*D* channel couplings are important to generate the attraction. The mixing effects shown in the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) and D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) molecules imply that HQSS should be respected in heavy hadronic molecular systems. In the bound state analysis, we couldn’t obtain a bound state in the other JPsuperscript𝐽𝑃JP states. We also study the case with gσ=gσ=0.76subscript𝑔𝜎superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎20.76gsigma=gsigma=0.76. Although small sigma coupling is used, we find a bound state of D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) for I​(JP)=0​(1/2−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃01superscript2I(JP)=0(1/2-). The bound state properties are summarized in Table. In the probabilities, the dominant component is in the D¯Ξc​c(2S12)barDXicc(2Sfrac12) channel, and the probability of the D¯∗Ξc​c∗(6D12)barD*Xicc*(6Dfrac12) one induced by the tensor force is also negligible, PD¯∗Ξc​c∗(6D12)∼2.3%PbarD*Xicc*(6Dfrac12)sim 2.3% as seen in the case with large gσsubscript𝑔𝜎gsigma. Interestingly the probability of the D¯∗Ξc​c∗(2S12)barD*Xicc*(2Sfrac12) channel, which is suppressed in the case with gσ=gσ=3.4subscript𝑔𝜎superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎13.4gsigma=gsigma=3.4, is as large as that of the D¯∗Ξc​c∗(6D12)barD*Xicc*(6Dfrac12) one. The D¯Ξc​c(2S12)−D¯∗Ξc​c∗(2S12)barDXicc(2Sfrac12)-barD*Xicc*(2Sfrac12) mixing is induced by the spin-spin interaction of the vector meson exchanges. This contribution producing an attraction has an important role to generate the bound state as shown in the energy expectation values in Fig. . The smaller strength of the σ𝜎sigma exchange potential emphasizes the role of the vector meson exchanges in addition to the π𝜋pi exchange. The D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) bound state is predicted under reasonable assumptions that Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc near the threshold has a D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷superscript𝐷D(*)D(*) molecular structure and the superflavor symmetry is present between D¯¯𝐷barD mesons with a c¯¯𝑐barc quark and Ξc​csubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc baryons with a c​c𝑐𝑐cc diquark. We expect that the D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barD(*)Xicc(*) state will be found in future experimental studies. We obtain the coupling constants of Ξc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc(*) by applying the superflavor symmetry between D¯(∗)superscript¯𝐷barD(*) and Ξc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐Xicc(*). However, the error of the superflavor symmetry is estimated as ΛQ​C​D/mc​v∼30%−40%similar-tosubscriptΛ𝑄𝐶𝐷subscript𝑚𝑐𝑣percent30percent40LambdaQCD/mcvsim 30%-40% with v𝑣v being the velocity of the charm quarkcitebib.bib31, bib.bib54, which is larger than the error ΛQ​C​D/mc∼10%−15%similar-tosubscriptΛ𝑄𝐶𝐷subscript𝑚𝑐percent10percent15LambdaQCD/mcsim 10%-15% for the HQSS. Therefore, we investigate the breaking effect of the superflavor symmetry by evaluating the dependence of binding energies on the coupling constants. Here we multiply a factor a𝑎a to the coupling constants g𝑔hatg, gσsubscript𝑔𝜎hatgsigma, β𝛽hatbeta and λ𝜆hatlambda in Eqs. - as g=2​a​g,gσ=2​a​gσ,β=2​a​β,λ=2​a​λ,formulae-sequence𝑔2𝑎𝑔formulae-sequencesubscript𝑔𝜎2𝑎subscript𝑔𝜎formulae-sequence𝛽2𝑎𝛽𝜆2𝑎𝜆displaystylehatg=2ag ,quadhatgsigma=2agsigma ,quadhatbeta=2abeta ,quadhatlambda=2alambda , and vary a𝑎a from 0.80.80.8 to 1.21.21.2. The results are shown in Fig. The dependence of the binding energy in coupling constants is not insignificant. When the ratio a=0.8𝑎0.8a=0.8, the bound state disappears in both models of gσ=gσ=3.4subscript𝑔𝜎superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎13.4gsigma=gsigma=3.4 and gσ=gσ=0.76subscript𝑔𝜎superscriptsubscript𝑔𝜎20.76gsigma=gsigma=0.76. However, the incorporation of such a significant error into the effective Lagrangian for doubly heavy baryons is not yet clearly established. Further theoretical and experimental studies are important to evaluate the the superflavor symmetry breaking effect in the effective theory.
content_sections.IV Summary: We have investigated the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) and D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) molecular states as candidates of the heavy exotic hadrons. The D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) molecule is considered to be a structure of the doubly charm tetraquark Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc near the threshold, reported by LHCb. We have studied a bound state of D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) with I​(JP)=0​(1+)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript1I(JP)=0(1+), introducing the one boson exchange potential model. By fitting the cutoff parameter of the potentials, we obtained the model that reproduced the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc binding energy. In this analysis, we have found that the σ𝜎sigma exchange potential produces the strong attraction. In addition, the off-diagonal terms of the potentials, especially given by the tensor force of the π𝜋pi exchange and the spin-spin term of the vector meson exchange, also play the important role to make the bound state. The significant contributions from the off-diagonal components in the D​D∗−D∗​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗DDast-DastDast coupling indicate that the heavy quark spin symmetry inducing the small D​D∗𝐷superscript𝐷∗DDast mass splitting is important in a bound state of D(∗)superscript𝐷∗D(ast) mesons. We have also studied the D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) bound state as a partner of the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) bound state under the superflavor symmetry. The one boson exchange potential has also introduced as for the D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) interaction, where unknown parameters for the Ξc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗Xicc(ast) baryons are determined by using the superflavor symmetry from the parameters of the D¯(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗barD(ast) mesons. Using the obtained potentials, we predicted the D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) bound state with I​(JP)=0​(1/2−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃01superscript2I(JP)=0(1/2-). As discussed in the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) bound state, we also found that the σ𝜎sigma exchange in the diagonal terms and the spin-dependent interactions in the off-diagonal terms play the important role to produce the attraction. The superflavor symmetry predicts the existence of the D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) bound state, if the reported Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc is the D(∗)​D(∗)superscript𝐷∗superscript𝐷∗D(ast)D(ast) molecule. Our prediction will be useful to understand natures of the heavy hadron spectra including not only D¯(∗)​Ξc​c(∗)superscript¯𝐷∗superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐∗barD(ast)Xicc(ast) but also the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc tetraquark and other hadronic molecules. However, it is crucial to take into account the breaking of HQS caused by the finite charm quark mass. Therefore, we need to consider the next leading order of 𝒪​(1/mQ)𝒪1subscript𝑚𝑄O(1/mQ) in the effective Lagrangian. Although the effective Lagrangian in the next leading order of 𝒪​(1/mQ)𝒪1subscript𝑚𝑄O(1/mQ) have been studiedcitebib.bib55, the parameters of coupling constants have not been determined yet. Moreover, the validation of the the superflavor symmetry and the extent of its breaking have not been investigated. Therefore, more experiment data of exotic state with heavy quarks are needed.

all_papers.title: Relations among $B_c \to J/\psi, \eta_c$ form factors
all_papers.abstract:
We analyze the form factors parametrizing the $B_c \to J/\psi, \eta_c$ matrix
elements of the operators in a generalized low-energy $b \to c$ semileptonic
Hamiltonian. We consider an expansion in nonrelativistic QCD, classifying the
heavy quark spin symmetry breaking terms and expressing the form factors in
terms of universal functions in a selected kinematical range. Using as an input
the lattice QCD results for the $B_c \to J/\psi$ matrix element of the SM
operator, we obtain information on other form factors. The extrapolation to the
full kinematical range is also presented.

all_papers.title:
Is the $\Lambda_c(2625)^+$ the heavy quark spin symmetry partner of the
  $\Lambda_c(2595)^+$?
all_papers.abstract:
We use a $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_\text{QCD}/m_c)$ heavy quark effective theory
scheme, where only $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_\text{QCD}/m_b)$ and perturbative QCD
short distance corrections are neglected, to study the matrix elements of the
scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector and tensor currents between the
$\Lambda_b$ ground state and the odd parity charm $\Lambda_c(2595)^+$ and
$\Lambda_c(2625)^+$ resonances. We show that in the near-zero recoil regime,
the scheme describes reasonably well, taking into account uncertainties, the
results for the 24 form-factors obtained in lattice QCD (LQCD) just in terms of
only 4 Isgur-Wise (IW) functions. We also find some support for the possibility
that the $\Lambda_c(2595)^+$ and $\Lambda_c(2625)^+$ resonances might form a
heavy-quark spin symmetry (HQSS) doublet. However, we argue that the available
LQCD description of these two resonances is not accurate enough to disentangle
the possible effects of the $\Sigma_c \pi$ and $\Sigma_c^*\pi$ thresholds,
located only a few MeV above their position, and that it cannot be ruled out
that these states are not HQSS partners. Finally, we study the ratio
$\frac{d\Gamma[\Lambda_b\to \Lambda_{c,1/2^-}^* \ell
\bar\nu_\ell]/dq^2}{d\Gamma[\Lambda_b\to \Lambda_{c,3/2^-}^*\ell
\bar\nu_\ell]/dq^2}$ of the Standard Model differential semileptonic decay
widths, with $q^\mu$ the momentum transferred between the initial and final
hadrons. We provide a natural explanation for the existence of large
deviations, near the zero recoil, of this ratio from 1/2 (value predicted in
the infinite heavy quark mass limit, assuming that the $\Lambda_{c,1/2^-}^*$
and $\Lambda_{c,3/2^-}^*$ are the two members of a HQSS doublet) based on
S-wave contributions to the $\Lambda_b\to \Lambda_{c,1/2^-}^*$ decay amplitude
driven by a sub-leading IW function.

all_papers.title: New insights into the doubly charmed exotic mesons
all_papers.abstract:
Using effective Lagrangians constrained by the heavy quark spin symmetry and
chiral symmetry, for the light quarks, we analyze the $D^0 D^0\pi^+$,
$\bar{D}^0D^0\pi^0$ and $D^0\bar{D}^{*0}$ invariant mass spectra. Performing a
simultaneous analysis of the doubly charmed and charm-anti-charm states gives
further insights into the nature of the $T^+_{cc}$ and $\chi^0_{c1}(3872)$,
exotic hadrons. It is confirmed that both states should lie below their
respective $DD^*$/$D\bar{D}^*$ thresholds. Also, the contributions of the
triangle and box diagrams are negligible.

all_papers.title:
Masses and radiative decay widths of the $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and
  $D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ and their bottom analogs
all_papers.abstract:
We study the mass spectra and radiative decays of $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and
$D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ in an unquenched framework. In addition to coupled
channel effects between the $c\bar{s}$ cores and $D^{(*)}K$ channels,
$D^{(*)}K$-$D^{(*)}K$ self interactions are also considered in this work and we
succeed to reproduce their mass spectra. Furthermore, we study the radiative
decays of the $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and $D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ by simultaneously
including the compound structures of conventional $c\bar{s}$ cores and
$D^{(*)}K$ components. We also calculate their bottom analogs with heavy quark
symmetry. Our study offers useful insights into the important unquenched
effects in the formation of $D_{s0}^*(2317)$, $D_{s1}^{\prime}(2460)$ and the
bottom counterparts.

all_papers.title: Study of B, Bs mesons using heavy quark effective theory
all_papers.abstract:
Inspired by the lower statistical information in the bottom {\bf sector, in
this paper, we} calculate the masses and the strong decays of excited $B$ and
$B_s$ mesons in the framework of heavy quark effective theory (HQET). Using an
effective chiral Lagrangian approach based on heavy quark spin-flavor and light
quark chiral symmetry, we explore the flavor independent parameters
$\Delta_F^{(c)} = \Delta_F^{(b)}$ and $\lambda_F^{(c)} = \lambda_F^{(b)}$ to
calculate the masses of experimentally unknown bottom mesons. Our predictions
are consistent with the available experimental results and other theoretical
studies. Their strong decay to the ground state bottom mesons plus light
pseudoscalar mesons is calculated in terms of the square of the couplings
$g_H$, $g_S$, $g_T$, $g_X$, $g_Y$, and $g_R$. The weighted average value of the
couplings $g_H$, $g_S$ and $g_T$ {\bf is} obtained in the charm sector [Phys.
Rev. D \textbf{86}, 054024 (2012)] by fitting the calculated decay widths with
experimental measurements, which will be used in the present study to analyze
the strong decays of excited open bottom mesons. Moreover, the ratio of the
decay rates is also predicted, which can be countered with future experimental
data.

all_papers.title:
Effective-theory description of heavy-flavored hadrons and their
  properties in a hot medium
all_papers.abstract:
This dissertation investigates exotic hadrons with heavy-quark content that
may be understood as being generated dynamically from the hadron-hadron
interaction. This interaction is derived from a suitable effective Lagrangian
and properly unitarized in a full coupled-channel basis. In particular, we
discuss the possible interpretation of some of the {\Omega}c* excited states
recently discovered at LHCb as being meson-baryon molecular states. We also
discuss the dynamical generation of excited open-charm mesons from the
scattering of pseudoscalar and vector charmed mesons off light mesons. We show
that a double-pole structure is predicted for the D0*(2300) state, as well as
for the D1(2430), while the Ds0*(2317) and the Ds1(2460) may be interpreted as
molecular bound states. Extensions of these calculations to the bottom sector
are also presented. Furthermore, we investigate the thermal modification of the
open heavy-flavor mesons in a hot medium. By means of an extension to finite
temperature of the unitarized effective interactions with the light mesons, we
obtain the in-medium spectral properties of the D, D*, Ds, and Ds* ground-state
mesons. We also analyze the temperature dependence of the masses and the decay
widths of the dynamically generated states. Additionally, we provide results
for the bottomed mesons by exploiting the heavy-quark spin-flavor symmetry of
the Lagrangian. We employ the temperature-dependent spectral functions to
compute charm Euclidean correlators. We also present calculations of off-shell
transport coefficients in the hadronic phase implementing in-medium scattering
amplitudes and the thermal dependence of the heavy-meson spectral properties.

all_papers.title:
Model-independent predictions for decays of double-heavy hadrons into
  pairs of heavy hadrons
all_papers.abstract:
Double-heavy hadrons can decay into pairs of heavy hadrons through
transitions from confining Born-Oppenheimer potentials to heavy-hadron-pair
potentials with the same Born-Oppenheimer quantum numbers. The states of the
double-heavy hadron are constrained by a Born-Oppenheimer exclusion principle
from the identical heavy quarks. The states of a pair of identical heavy
hadrons are constrained by exclusion principles from identical particles. The
transitions are also constrained by conservation of angular momentum and
parity. From these constraints, we derive model-independent selection rules for
decays of double-heavy hadrons into pairs of heavy hadrons. The coupling
potentials are expressed as sums of products of Born-Oppenheimer transition
amplitudes and angular-momentum coefficients. If there is a single dominant
Born-Oppenheimer transition amplitude, it factors out of the coupling
potentials between double-heavy hadrons in the same Born-Oppenheimer multiplet
and pairs of heavy hadrons in specific heavy-quark-spin-symmetry multiplets and
out of the corresponding partial decay rates. As examples, we discuss the
Born-Oppenheimer potentials and multiplets for conventional double-heavy
baryons and for double-heavy tetraquark mesons. We also discuss the relative
partial decay rates for conventional double-heavy baryons into pairs of heavy
hadrons.

all_papers.title:
Model-independent predictions for decays of hidden-heavy hadrons into
  pairs of heavy hadrons
all_papers.abstract:
Hidden-heavy hadrons can decay into pairs of heavy hadrons through
transitions from confining Born-Oppenheimer potentials to hadron-pair
potentials with the same Born-Oppenheimer quantum numbers. The transitions are
also constrained by conservation of angular momentum and parity. From these
constraints, we derive model-independent selection rules for decays of
hidden-heavy hadrons into pairs of heavy hadrons. The coupling potentials are
expressed as sums of products of Born-Oppenheimer transition amplitudes and
angular-momentum coefficients. If there is a single dominant Born-Oppenheimer
transition amplitude, it factors out of the coupling potentials between
hidden-heavy hadrons in the same Born-Oppenheimer multiplet and pairs of heavy
hadrons in specific heavy-quark-spin-symmetry doublets. If furthermore the
kinetic energies of the heavy hadrons are much larger than their spin
splittings, we obtain analytic expressions for the relative partial decay rates
in terms of Wigner 6-j and 9-j symbols. We consider in detail the decays of
quarkonia and quarkonium hybrids into the lightest heavy-meson pairs. For
quarkonia, our model-independent selection rules and relative partial decay
rates agree with previous results from quark-pair-creation models in simple
cases and give stronger results in other cases. For quarkonium hybrids, we find
disagreement even in simple cases.

all_papers.title: Bottom-charmed baryons in a nonrelativistic quark model
all_papers.abstract:
In this work, we study the low-lying mass spectra for bottom-charmed baryons
in a nonrelativistic quark model by solving the three-body Schr\"odinger
equation. The lowest $\Xi_{bc}$, $\Xi_{bc}^\prime$, $\Omega_{bc}$, and
$\Omega_{bc}^\prime$ states are predicted to be about 6979, 6953, 7109, and
7092 MeV, respectively. Also, the strong decays for the low-lying excited
states are investigated. Our results indicate that some of $\lambda-$mode
$P-$wave bottom-charmed baryons are relatively narrow, which can be searched
for in future experiments. For the low-lying $\rho-$mode and $\rho-\lambda$
hybrid states, their strong decays are highly suppressed and they can survive
as extremely narrow states. Moreover, the mass spectra and strong decays for
bottom-charmed baryons preserve the heavy quark symmetry well. We hope our
calculations can provide helpful information for further experimental and
theoretical researches.

all_papers.title: Investigation on the bottom analogs of the $T_{cc}^{+}$
all_papers.abstract:
We investigate the doubly bottom state $T^-_{bb}$ composed of two bottom
mesons with $J^P=1^+$. The potentials are obtained using the one-boson exchange
model. With the heavy quark flavor symmetry, all the parameters in the model
are determined by fitting the experimental data of doubly charmed state
$T_{cc}^{+}$ from our previous work. Our analysis indicates that the isospin
symmetry is well-preserved. We identify a deeply bound $T^-_{bb}$ state with
quantum numbers $I(J^{P})=0(1^+)$, in contrast to the loosely bound
$T_{cc}^{+}$. Additionally, we discover a resonant $T^-_{bb}$ state with
$I(J^{P})=1(1^+)$. Furthermore, our investigation into the
$\bar{B}\bar{B}^*$-$\bar{B}^*\bar{B}^*$ coupled channel effect reveals its
important impact. The binding energy of the bound $I(J^{P})=0(1^+)$ states
becomes deeper, while the resonant $T^-_{bb}$ state with $I(J^{P})=1(1^+)$
remains nearly unchanged.

all_papers.title: Improved method to determine the $\Xi_c-\Xi_c'$ mixing
all_papers.abstract:
We develop an improved method to explore the $\Xi_c- \Xi_c'$ mixing which
arises from the flavor SU(3) and heavy quark symmetry breaking. In this method,
the flavor eigenstates under the SU(3) symmetry are at first constructed and
the corresponding masses can be nonperturbatively determined. Matrix elements
of the mass operators which break the flavor SU(3) symmetry sandwiched by the
flavor eigenstates are then calculated. Diagonalizing the corresponding matrix
of Hamiltonian gives the mass eigenstates of the full Hamiltonian and
determines the mixing. Following the previous lattice QCD calculation of
$\Xi_c$ and $\Xi_c'$, and estimating an off-diagonal matrix element, we extract
the mixing angle between the $\Xi_c$ and $\Xi_c'$. Preliminary numerical
results for the mixing angle confirm the previous observation that such mixing
is incapable to explain the large SU(3) symmetry breaking in semileptonic
decays of charmed baryons.

all_papers.title:
Appraising constrained second-order power corrections in HQET with
  $\Lambda_b \to \Lambda_c l \nu$
all_papers.abstract:
We derive the $\Lambda_b \to \Lambda_c$ form factors for the Standard Model
and beyond at second order in Heavy Quark Effective Theory (HQET), applying the
recently-proposed Residual Chiral Expansion (RCE) to reduce the set of unknown
subsubleading hadronic functions to a single, highly-constrained function, that
is fully determined by hadron mass parameters at zero recoil. We fit a form
factor parametrization based on these results to all available Lattice QCD
(LQCD) predictions and experimental data. We find that the constrained and
predictive structure of the form factors under the RCE is in excellent
agreement with LQCD predictions and experimental data, as well as prior
HQET-based fits.

all_papers.title: Mass and decay width of $T_{ccs}$ from symmetries
all_papers.abstract:
We analyze the mass and width of the doubly heavy tetraquark $T_{ccs}$
composed of a heavy diquark and a light-quark cloud with strangeness with
assuming that a color antitriplet heavy diquark is a dominant component of the
doubly charmed tetraquarks $T_{cc}$ and $T_{ccs}$. We construct an effective
Lagrangian for masses of heavy hadrons based on the superflavor symmetry
between the doubly heavy tetraquarks and the singly heavy baryons by including
the terms that simultaneously break the heavy-quark and light-flavor
symmetries, and predict the mass of $T_{ccs}$ as $M(T_{ccs}) = 4047\pm11$\,MeV.
The comparison of this prediction with future experimental observation will
give a clue to understand the color structure of the heavy diquark. We also
predict the mass of $\Omega_{cc}$ as $M(\Omega_{cc}) = 3706^{+14}_{-15}\,$MeV.
We next calculate the decay width of $T_{ccs}$, based on solely the
light-flavor symmetry, as $\Gamma(T_{ccs}) = 42\pm 24$\,MeV.

sections.4: Gell-Mann, A Schematic Model of Baryons and Mesons, Phys

all_papers.title: Effect of continuum states on the double-heavy hadron spectra
all_papers.abstract:
We present the leading order coupling of double-heavy hadrons to heavy hadron
pairs in Born-Oppenheimer effective field theory. We obtain the expressions for
the contribution of heavy hadron pairs to the masses and widths of double-heavy
hadrons. We apply our result for the specific case of the coupling of the
lowest lying heavy hybrids and
$D_{(s)}^{(*)}\bar{D}_{(s)}^{(*)}(B_{(s)}^{(*)}\bar{B}_{(s)}^{(*)})$ obtaining
a set of selection rules for the decays. We build a model for the coupling
potential and compute the corresponding decay widths and the contributions to
the mass of the heavy hybrids. We compare our results with the experimental
exotic quarkonium spectrum and discuss the most likely experimental candidates
for quarkonium hybrids.

all_papers.title:
Semileptonic $\Omega_{b}\rightarrow \Omega_{c}{\ell}\bar\nu_{\ell}$
  transition in full QCD
all_papers.abstract:
We investigate the semileptonic decay of
$\Omega_b\to\Omega_c~{\ell}\bar\nu_{\ell}$ in three lepton channels. To this
end, we use QCD sum rule method in three point framework to calculate the form
factors defining the matrix elements of these transitions. Having calculated
the form factors as building blocks, we calculate the decay widths and
branching fractions of the exclusive decays in all lepton channels and compare
the results with other theoretical predictions. The obtained results for
branching ratios and ratio of branching fractions at different leptonic
channels may help experimental groups in their search for these weak decays.
Comparison of the obtained results with possible future experimental data can
be useful to check the order of consistency between the standard model theory
predictions and data on the heavy baryon decays.

all_papers.title:
Doubly heavy tetraquark multiplets as heavy antiquark-diquark symmetry
  partners of heavy baryons
all_papers.abstract:
Symmetries play important roles in the understanding of hadron structures and
spectroscopy. Motivated by the discovery of the doubly charmed tetraquark
$T_{cc}^+(3875)$, we study the ground states of the doubly heavy tetraquarks
with the QCD inspired heavy antiquark-diquark symmetry in the constituent quark
model. Six ground states of $T_{QQ} (Q=c,b)$ are predicted and the lightest
$T_{cc}$ state has a mass of $3875.8\pm 7.6$ MeV and spin-parity $1^+$ which
are consistent with those of the observed $T_{cc}^+(3875)$. In addition, the
magnetic moments of the predicted tetraquarks $T_{cc}^+(3876)$ and
$T_{bb}^-(10396)$ are also estimated in the same model, which provide further
informations to distinguish the structures of the $T_{QQ}$ states. Our results
show that the heavy antiquark-diquark symmetry conserves well in these doubly
heavy tetraquarks in both the spectrum and magnetic moment perspectives.

all_papers.title: Doubly-Charged $T_{cc}^{++}$ States in the Dynamical Diquark Model
all_papers.abstract:
One of the celebrated tools in explaining the Hydrogen atom is
Born-Oppenheimer approximation. The resemblance of $QQ\bar{q}\bar{q}$
tetraquarks to Hydrogen atom within Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) implies usage
of Born-Oppenheimer approximation for these multiquark states. In this work, we
use dynamical diquark model to calculate mass spectra and sizes of doubly
charmed and charged tetraquark states denoted as $T_{cc}^{++}$. Our results for
mass spectra indicate some bound state candidates with respect to corresponding
two-meson thresholds. Calculation of expectation values of $\sqrt{\langle r^2
\rangle}$ reflects that doubly charmed and charged tetraquark states are
compact.

all_papers.title: $T_{cc}$ in the Diabatic Diquark Model: Effects of $D^*D$ Isospin
all_papers.abstract:
$T_{cc}^+$ is an isoscalar 4-quark state with mass lying barely below the
$D^{*+} D^0$ threshold, and several times further below the $D^{*0} D^+$
threshold. It allows both di-meson molecular and elementary diquark-antidiquark
$(cc)(\bar u \bar d)$ substructures. The diabatic generalization of the
adiabatic approximation within the Born-Oppenheimer formalism rigorously
incorporates the mixing of such elementary eigenstates with states
corresponding to two-particle thresholds. We examine the separate influence of
the two $D^* \! D$ isospin channels and find that the influence of $D^{*+} D^0$
is larger than that of $D^{*0} D^+$ but not overwhelmingly so, and that
$T_{cc}^+$ contains an $O(10\%)$ $(cc)(\bar u \bar d)$ component. We then
explore the variation of these results if the isospin breaking between the
di-meson thresholds is varied, and also the sensitivity of our results to
variation of the mixing-potential parameters.

all_papers.title: Heavy meson thresholds in Born-Oppenheimer Effective field theory
all_papers.abstract:
We consider heavy meson-antimeson pairs and their coupling to quarkonium in
the context of nonrelativistic EFTs incorporating the adiabatic expansion. We
work out all the leading order couplings of quarkonium to heavy meson-antimeson
pairs and obtain their contributions to the masses and widths of quarkonia. We
match the new potentials terms to NRQCD. Using the available lattice data for
the coupled system of quarkonium and the lowest laying heavy meson-antimeson
pair, we extract the mixing potential and use it to compute numerically the
contributions of $D\bar{D}(B\bar{B})$ and $D_s\bar{D}_s(B_s\bar{B}_s)$ to the
masses and widths of the charmonium (bottomonium) states for $l=0,1,2$ and up
to $n=6$ covering the states in threshold region. When a quarkonium state and a
heavy meson-antimeson pair are separated by small energy gaps, their
interactions can be described by a threshold EFT with contact interactions. We
work out the matching between the two EFTs obtaining the couplings of the
threshold EFT in terms of the mixing potential and quarkonium wave functions.

all_papers.title:
$B\to D \ell \nu_\ell$ form factors beyond leading power and extraction
  of $|V_{cb}|$ and $R(D)$
all_papers.abstract:
We investigate the subleading-power corrections to the exclusive $B\to D \ell
\nu_\ell$ form factors at ${\cal O} (\alpha_s^0)$ in the light-cone sum rules
(LCSR) framework by including the two- and three-particle higher-twist
contributions from the $B$-meson light-cone distribution amplitudes up to the
twist-six accuracy, by taking into account the subleading terms in expanding
the hard-collinear charm-quark propagator, and by evaluating the hadronic
matrix element of the subleading effective current $\bar q \, \gamma_{\mu} \, i
\not\!\!{D}_\perp / (2 \, m_b) \, h_v$. Employing further the available
leading-power results for the semileptonic $B \to D$ form factors at the
next-to-leading-logarithmic level and combining our improved LCSR predictions
with the recent lattice determinations, we then carry out a comprehensive
phenomenological analysis on the semi-leptonic $B\to D \ell \nu_\ell$ decay. We
extract $|V_{cb}| = \big( 40.2^{+0.6}_{-0.5} {\big |_{\rm th}}\,\,
{}^{+1.4}_{-1.4} {\big |_{\rm exp}} \big)\times 10^{-3}$ ($|V_{cb}| = \big(
40.9^{+0.6}_{-0.5} {\big |_{\rm th}}\,\, {}^{+1.0}_{-1.0} {\big |_{\rm exp}}
\big)\times 10^{-3}$) using the BaBar (Belle) experimental data, and
particularly obtain for the gold-plated ratio $R(D) = 0.302\pm 0.003$.

论文聚焦于B→Dℓν衰变的次领头幂修正与|V_cb|、R(D)的提取，虽涉及重夸克对称性相关物理背景，但未明确讨论重夸克自旋对称性（HQSS）的理论框架、对称性破缺或其在形式因子中的具体应用。论文未提及HQSS相关概念、推导或对称性约束，仅在背景中隐含使用重夸克极限。

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究主题需明确涉及重夸克自旋对称性（HQSS）——不满足。证据：“论文未出现‘HQSS’或相关对称性分析” 对比 “用户查询强调HQSS”  
- C2：方法需基于HQSS框架或其推论——不满足。证据：“LCSR与次领头幂修正未体现HQSS对称性” 对比 “查询要求HQSS相关方法”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 论文未讨论HQSS的理论基础或其在B→D衰变中的具体作用  
- 未分析对称性对形式因子的约束或简化机制
